  ---
  Q3 — Yogini Dasai starting-nakshatra lookup table source

  Background. E1a section 3.1 gives an explicit 27-row table mapping natchathiram number → starting Yogini per BPHS Ch.48 v.4–6. For example:
  - Natchathiram 1 (Aswini) → starting Yogini = Sankata (ruled by Rahu, 8 years)
  - Natchathiram 2 (Bharani) → starting Yogini = Mangala (ruled by Chandran, 1 year)
  - Natchathiram 3 (Karthikai) → starting Yogini = Pingala (ruled by Sooriyan, 2 years)
  - ... etc.

  Classical variance. Two classical sources publish the table with slight differences:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │                Source                │                                 Pattern                                  │                   Notes                    │   
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ BPHS Ch.48 v.4–6 (Parashara primary) │ Starting Yogini sequence begins with Sankata at Aswini                   │ Canonical Parashari reference; PRD default │   
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Saravali Ch.33 (Kalyana Varma)       │ Same 8-Yogini sequence but offset by one position for some natchathirams │ Older text; minor variant                  │   
  └──────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Magnitude of variance: About 3–5 of the 27 natchathirams get a different starting Yogini between BPHS and Saravali. For affected natchathirams, the period lengths 
  differ (e.g., someone born in a "variant" natchathiram gets Mangala-1yr starting instead of Sankata-8yr starting — massively different first-dasai duration).      

  Modern software:
  - Jagannatha Hora: BPHS Ch.48 table (explicit default)
  - Parashara's Light: BPHS Ch.48 table
  - Tamil Vakya Panchangam: BPHS Ch.48 table
  - Some Nadi jyotish texts cite Saravali variant

  Q3 — Yogini Dasai starting-natchathiram lookup:

  - A) BPHS Ch.48 table only — PRD default, matches JH + Parashara's Light + Tamil Vakya practice. Same for both user types. Saravali variant not implemented.       
  - B) BPHS default + Saravali variant as alternate source — Both rules shipped as sibling rules; BPHS is Josi default; astrologer can override per profile via F2   
  astrologer_source_preference.
  - C) BPHS default + Saravali only if astrologer is a known Nadi lineage practitioner — feature-flag the variant; low expected demand.
  - D) Research — verify exactly which rows differ between BPHS and Saravali before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Sautéed for 57s

❯ A                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q3 locked: Option A — BPHS Ch.48 table only. Matches JH / Parashara's Light / Tamil Vakya. No Saravali variant.                                                    

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q4 — Ashtottari Dasai starting-lord algorithm
                                                                                                                                                                       Background. Ashtottari Dasai has 8 mahadasais totaling 108 years (Sooriyan 6 + Chandran 15 + Sevvai 8 + Budhan 17 + Sani 10 + Guru 19 + Rahu 12 + Sukkiran 21 =      108). Given Chandran's sidereal longitude at birth, we need to determine: which dasai is the starting dasai, and how much of it has elapsed?                                                                                                                                                                                              Two classical algorithms in circulation:                                                                                                                           

  Variant A — Continuous longitude-based (PRD current default)

  cumulative_years = (moon_sidereal_longitude / 360) × 108
  Walk DASHA_SEQUENCE [(Sooriyan, 6), (Chandran, 15), (Sevvai, 8),
                      (Budhan, 17), (Sani, 10), (Guru, 19), (Rahu, 12),
                      (Sukkiran, 21)]:
    Find which dasai `cumulative_years` falls into
    balance_of_starting = dasai_years - elapsed_within_dasai

  Properties: Smooth continuous transition as Chandran moves. Matches Jagannatha Hora 7.x exactly. Every ecliptic degree has a unique dasai-fraction assignment.     

  Variant B — Discrete natchathiram-bucket per BPHS 47.4–7 verse-literal

  BPHS assigns each natchathiram to one of 8 starting-dasai buckets (the PRD section 3.2 table shows this — Sooriyan covers Karthikai, Rohini, Mrigasirsham-partial, 
  Thiruvathirai-partial; Chandran covers Thiruvathirai-remainder, Punarpoosam, Poosam, Ayilyam-partial; etc.).

  Properties: Textually closer to BPHS Sanskrit verse. Produces "sharp transitions" at natchathiram boundaries — a chart with Chandran at 13°19' Bharani starts with 
  Mangala Mahadasai; at 13°20' Bharani (next natchathiram) starts with Pingala Mahadasai, different dasai-fraction logic.

  Practical comparison

  ┌────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
  │             Factor             │ Variant A (continuous) │     Variant B (discrete)     │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ Matches JH 7.x                 │ ✅ Exact               │ ❌ Divergent near boundaries │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ Matches Parashara's Light      │ ✅                     │ ❌                           │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ Matches Tamil Vakya Panchangam │ ✅                     │ Variable                     │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ BPHS verse-literal             │ 🟡 Interpreted         │ ✅ Literal                   │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ Boundary behavior              │ Smooth                 │ Sharp transitions            │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
  │ Classical citation             │ Saravali + JH lineage  │ BPHS 47.4–7 direct           │
  └────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘

  Q4 — Ashtottari starting-lord algorithm:

  - A) Continuous longitude-based (PRD current default) — single algorithm, matches all modern Parashari software. Same for both user types.
  - B) Discrete natchathiram-bucket — closer to BPHS verse-literal; diverges from JH near natchathiram boundaries.
  - C) Continuous as B2C default + discrete as astrologer alternate source — astrologer toggle for verse-literal BPHS research. Ships as sibling rules (like we did  
  for Ravi Yogam in 3.9).
  - D) Research — verify the magnitude of divergence between A and B at natchathiram boundaries with a worked example before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Brewed for 33s

❯ A                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q4 locked: Option A — continuous longitude-based algorithm. Matches JH / Parashara's Light / Tamil Vakya. Single algorithm, same for both user types.              

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q7 — Paksham determination method for Ashtottari strict-mode eligibility
                                                                                                                                                                       Context. Ashtottari eligibility is permissive by default (DECISIONS 1.6) — Josi always computes it. BUT when an astrologer toggles strict mode on their profile,     Josi must determine whether the chart's Chandran falls in Krishna Paksham to decide applicability. This Q picks the implementation method.                                                                                                                                                                                                Two equivalent methods (classical):                                                                                                                                

  Method A — Tithi-based

  Look up the tithi at birth moment from the panchangam computation (tithi is already computed for every chart). Tithi 1–15 = Shukla Paksham; 16–30 = Krishna        
  Paksham.

  Method B — Sun-Moon longitude difference

  angular_separation = (moon_sidereal_long - sun_sidereal_long) mod 360°. 0°–180° = Shukla Paksham; 180°–360° = Krishna Paksham.

  Method C — Cross-validation

  Compute both A and B; assert they agree (they MUST — tithi is literally derived from angular_separation ÷ 12°). Log discrepancy as computation bug if ever
  mismatched.

  Classical note. These two methods are mathematically equivalent by construction: tithi is DEFINED as floor(angular_separation / 12°). So the "classical choice" is 
  really about which pre-computed value we look up, not which tradition we follow.

  Practical engineering angle: In Josi's pipeline, tithi is computed in the panchangam layer before dasai calculation. Method A is a free lookup from
  already-computed data. Method B requires a second angular-separation computation on the same data.

  Q7 — Paksham determination method for strict-mode Ashtottari:

  - A) Tithi-based lookup — reuse already-computed tithi from panchangam layer. Zero extra compute. Same for both user types (applies only when astrologer toggles   
  strict).
  - B) Sun-Moon longitude difference — recompute angular separation in dasai engine. Self-contained but redundant.
  - C) Cross-validation (both) — tithi for audit + longitude as validator; assert equality; log bug if discrepancy.
  - D) Other.

  Which?

✻ Worked for 37s

❯ what is actual calculation difference between a and b                                                                                                                                                                      
● Actual calculation difference between A and B                                                                                                                      
                             
  Zero numerical difference. They're mathematically equivalent by construction. Tithi is defined as floor(angular_separation / 12°) — so Method A just adds an         intermediate step that Method B skips.
                                                                                                                                                                       The two workflows side-by-side                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            Method A (tithi-based):                                                                                                                                              Step 1:  sun_long       = swe.calc_ut(jd, swe.SUN, SIDEREAL_FLAGS)[0]                                                                                              
  Step 2:  moon_long      = swe.calc_ut(jd, swe.MOON, SIDEREAL_FLAGS)[0]
  Step 3:  angular_sep    = (moon_long - sun_long) % 360.0
  Step 4:  tithi_num      = int(angular_sep / 12.0) + 1          # 1-30
  Step 5:  paksham        = "shukla" if tithi_num <= 15 else "krishna"

  Method B (longitude difference):
  Step 1:  sun_long       = swe.calc_ut(jd, swe.SUN, SIDEREAL_FLAGS)[0]
  Step 2:  moon_long      = swe.calc_ut(jd, swe.MOON, SIDEREAL_FLAGS)[0]
  Step 3:  angular_sep    = (moon_long - sun_long) % 360.0
  Step 4:  paksham        = "shukla" if angular_sep <= 180.0 else "krishna"

  Only difference: Method A computes tithi_num (a useful intermediate); Method B skips to the paksham check directly. The inputs (Sun longitude, Moon longitude) and 
  the fundamental test (is Moon within 180° forward of Sun?) are identical.

  Worked verification

  The paksham boundary at angular_sep = 180° corresponds to tithi_num = floor(180/12) + 1 = 16 (first tithi of Krishna Paksham).

  - angular_sep = 168° → tithi_num = 14+1 = 15 (Shukla Chaturdasi); A says shukla ≤15 ✓; B says 168 ≤180 ✓ — agree
  - angular_sep = 179.9° → tithi_num = 14+1 = 15 (still Shukla Chaturdasi); A says shukla; B says 179.9 ≤180 → shukla — agree
  - angular_sep = 180.1° → tithi_num = 15+1 = 16 (Krishna Prathamai); A says krishna; B says 180.1 >180 → krishna — agree
  - angular_sep = 0.0001° → tithi_num = 0+1 = 1 (Shukla Prathamai); A says shukla; B says 0.0001 ≤180 → shukla — agree

  Where the difference actually is (engineering, not calculation)

  ┌───────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐
  │            Factor             │                     Method A                     │           Method B            │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Code location                 │ Look up pre-computed tithi from panchangam layer │ Recompute in dasai engine     │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Extra Swiss Ephemeris calls   │ 0 (reuse)                                        │ 0 (if caching), or 2 (if not) │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Lines of code                 │ ~1 (import + lookup)                             │ ~4 (recompute)                │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Failure modes                 │ Depends on panchangam layer being computed first │ Self-contained                │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Dependency                    │ Dasai engine depends on panchangam engine        │ Dasai engine independent      │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tithi always computed anyway? │ Yes — panchangam computes for every chart        │ —                             │
  └───────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

  Floating-point edge case (not a classical difference)

  The only possible divergence is at exactly angular_sep = 180.00000000...° due to IEEE-754 rounding. In practice: near-impossible (Moon position has sub-arcsecond  
  resolution; probability of hitting exactly 180.000... is vanishingly small). If it ever happened, both methods would likely agree anyway (both compile to an ≤     
  comparison on the same floating-point value, just through different code paths).

  ---
  The real decision is architectural

  - Method A = keep dasai engine loosely coupled; reuse existing panchangam output; zero recomputation. Cleaner architecture.
  - Method B = dasai engine self-contained; no dependency on panchangam; easier to unit-test in isolation.
  - Method C = both + cross-validation assertion; flags floating-point or computation bugs.

  Numerically, pick whichever; the result is identical.

  ---
  Still waiting on your pick — Q7 A / B / C / D.

✻ Churned for 45s

❯ a                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q7 locked: Option A — tithi-based lookup. Reuse pre-computed tithi from panchangam layer. Zero extra compute. Applies only when astrologer toggles strict          
  Ashtottari mode.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q8 — Natchathiram-boundary edge case                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
  Background. Dasai starting-lord depends on Moon's natchathiram at birth. What if Moon is exactly on a natchathiram boundary (e.g., exactly 13°20'00.00" =            Aswini/Bharani boundary)?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  Practical rarity: Moon moves ~13°/day = ~0.54°/hour = ~0.009°/minute = ~0.54"/second. The probability of hitting exactly a boundary to sub-arcsecond precision is
  near-zero in real birth times. But rectification (E17) searches at 1-minute resolution over a window, so boundary-straddling IS possible for rectification
  candidates.

  Three approaches:

  Approach A — Boundary-inclusive lower (interval half-open)

  natchathiram = floor(moon_longitude / 13°20') + 1    # 1-indexed

  A natchathiram owns longitudes [n × 13°20', (n+1) × 13°20'). So Moon at exactly 13°20'00.00" → Bharani (not Aswini). The boundary value is always "in the next"    
  natchathiram.

  Approach B — Speed-direction-based assignment

  Check Moon's instantaneous longitudinal speed at birth (swe.calc_ut returns this). If Moon is moving forward (99.99% of time), it's "entering" the next
  natchathiram → assign next. If retrograde (Moon never retrogrades but formally the check can apply to any body) → assign previous.

  For Moon specifically: Moon is always direct; so this degenerates to Approach A + sanity check.

  Approach C — Ambiguity flag within 1 arc-second of boundary

  If |moon_long mod 13°20' − 0| < 1" OR |moon_long mod 13°20' − 13°20'| < 1", flag the chart as natchathiram-ambiguous. UI surfaces a badge "birth time may be       
  inaccurate — dasai starting-lord depends on resolution"; astrologer can run rectification (E17) to disambiguate. Primary assignment still follows Approach A for   
  computation; flag is informational.

  Approach D — Probabilistic confidence annotation

  For every chart: compute natchathiram + emit a "boundary distance" confidence score. If Moon is in middle of natchathiram (6°40' within the 13°20' span) → high    
  confidence. If near boundary (<10' from edge) → low confidence + recommend rectification.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Approach A (boundary-inclusive lower, silent)
  - Parashara's Light: Approach A + warning dialog if < 1 minute from boundary
  - Drik Panchang: Approach A, no warning
  - Astrosage: Approach A

  What the decision actually decides

  This matters for rectification (E17) candidates at 1-minute resolution. If a candidate birth time puts Moon within ~30 arc-minutes of a natchathiram boundary, the 
  dasai calculation could assign either neighbor. The candidate's dasai-score contribution to rectification is then unstable.

  Q8 — Natchathiram-boundary edge case handling:

  - A) Approach A — Boundary-inclusive lower, silent. Matches JH + all modern software. Same for both user types.
  - B) Approach A + warning flag within 1 arc-second of boundary (astrologer-visible only; B2C unaware).
  - C) Approach A + confidence score — every dasai result carries a boundary-distance confidence annotation. Astrologer sees; B2C sees only when confidence is       
  critically low (<10 arc-min from boundary → recommends rectification).
  - D) Approach A + integrate with E17 rectification — when Moon is within 10' of a boundary, AI chat offers: "Your birth time may shift your dasai starting lord.   
  Want me to run rectification using your life events?"
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 37s
✻
 ※ recap: We're reviewing E1a Multi-Dasai v1 as the first per-PRD Pass 1 after locking 43 cross-cutting decisions; you're answering E1a questions one by one. Next      action: pick A, B, C, D, or E on Q8 — natchathiram-boundary edge case handling.
                                                                                                                                                                     ❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q8 locked: Option A — Approach A (boundary-inclusive lower), silent. Matches JH / Parashara's Light / Drik Panchang / Astrosage. Same for both user types.         
                                                                                                                                                                     
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q9 — Dasai boundary precision / matching bar vs Jagannatha Hora 7.x
                                                                                                                                                                       Background. E1a section 1 (Purpose) specifies: "engine matches Jagannatha Hora 7.x (JH) period boundaries within ±1 day across a golden suite of 10 charts." This    is the test acceptance criterion for the dasai engine — how tight must Josi's computed dasai boundaries match JH's published dasai boundaries?                                                                                                                                                                                            What drives the divergence between Josi and JH (even with same inputs):                                                                                            
  - Ayanamsa subtle differences (Josi Lahiri SIDM_LAHIRI vs JH's internal Lahiri — historically JH used SIDM_LAHIRI_VP285 refinement in some versions)
  - Swiss Ephemeris version (Josi uses pyswisseph 2.x; JH uses Swiss Eph C library; minor arcsecond differences possible in Moon position at birth moment)
  - Paksham determination edge cases (rare)
  - Moon longitude at birth moment: computed to arcsecond precision; small differences cascade into dasai balance

  Three tightness levels:

  Level A — ±1 day (PRD current)

  Test passes if Josi's computed dasai boundary is within 1 day of JH's. Given a Vimshottari Mahadasai is typically 6–20 years, ±1 day error = ~0.02% error.
  Acceptable for practical astrology: an astrologer saying "your Rahu dasai ends March 15" vs JH saying "March 16" is immaterial for reading.

  Level B — ±1 hour

  ~0.0008% error. Requires tighter ayanamsa + floating-point control. Catches off-by-one-day bugs (timezone, UT conversion, DST handling) earlier.

  Level C — ±1 second

  ~0.00002% error. Requires bit-exact agreement with JH's internal Moon position + ayanamsa + proportional formulas. Practically achievable only if Josi uses        
  identical Swiss Ephemeris binary + identical ayanamsa constants.

  Level D — Match to seconds for computation, render to day

  Internal precision ±1 second; UI rendering rounded to day. Best of both worlds but more engineering effort — requires binary-exact match with JH which is not      
  guaranteed.

  Modern practice

  - Jagannatha Hora vs Parashara's Light cross-verify at ±1 day tolerance (industry default).
  - Academic Parashari research tools sometimes specify ±1 second for reproducibility.
  - Tamil Vakya Panchangam cross-verify at ±1 day.

  Q9 — Dasai boundary matching bar vs JH 7.x:

  - A) ±1 day (PRD current) — industry standard; matches Parashari practice expectation; tractable CI test bar. Same for both user types.
  - B) ±1 hour — tighter; catches timezone/DST bugs earlier; requires more golden-chart curation effort.
  - C) ±1 second — academic-grade reproducibility; requires bit-exact ayanamsa + floating-point match with JH; high engineering burden.
  - D) Internal ±1 second + UI renders ±1 day — hybrid; expensive engineering; diminishing returns.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

❯ what is PRD current                                                                                                                                                                      
● PRD = Product Requirements Document. In Josi's context, these are the 76 (now 89 with gap closure) specification files under docs/markdown/prd/ that define what   
  each feature will do.
                                                                                                                                                                       "PRD current" in Q9 refers to what the existing E1a PRD file (docs/markdown/prd/P1/E1a-multi-dasa-v1.md) specifies as its acceptance criterion. For the dasai      
  boundary matching bar, the E1a PRD states:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  ▎ Section 1 (Purpose): "Implementation expectation: engine matches Jagannatha Hora 7.x (JH) period boundaries within ±1 day across a golden suite of 10 charts."     ▎                                                                                                                                                                    ▎ Section 2.1 (In scope): "Golden chart fixtures: 10 known charts with expected dasa periods verified against JH."                                                 

  So PRD-current bar = ±1 day (which is exactly Option A in my question).

  The question is really asking whether you want to:
  - Confirm the PRD's existing ±1 day (Option A — no change)
  - Tighten it to ±1 hour (Option B — more stringent)
  - Tighten to ±1 second (Option C — academic-grade)
  - Hybrid internal/display (Option D)

  Most Parashari software cross-verifies at ±1 day tolerance. For practical astrology, anything tighter is engineering burden without practical benefit (no reading  
  changes materially whether Rahu Mahadasai ends "March 15" or "March 15 14:23:47 IST").

  My recommendation: A (confirm ±1 day) unless you want stricter testing discipline for some reason.

  Your pick — Q9 A / B / C / D / E?

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q9 locked: Option A — confirm PRD-current ±1 day matching bar vs JH 7.x. Industry standard. No tightening.                                                         

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q10 — Dasai balance rendering precision                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                       Background. Every chart produces dasai balance: how much time remains in the currently-active Mahadasai at this moment. Example: "Venus Mahadasai ends in X time."   How is X rendered?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Three common formats:                                                                                                                                              

  Format A — Float years

  Venus Mahadasai balance: 14.472 years

  Compact. Non-intuitive for most readers (does "0.472 years" mean 5 months? 6 months?).

  Format B — Year-Month-Day-Hour-Minute breakdown (classical)

  Venus Mahadasai balance: 14 years 5 months 20 days 6 hours 14 minutes

  Matches Jagannatha Hora / Parashara's Light / Drik Panchang / Tamil Vakya panchangam presentation. Classical Parashari astrologers expect this format. Cognitively 
  accessible.

  Format C — Store float internally, render Y/M/D/H/M

  Internal representation: float years (easy for arithmetic). UI rendering: parse the float into Y/M/D/H/M breakdown on display. Best of both.

  Format D — User-configurable

  B2C sees Y/M/D default; Astrologer sees Y/M/D/H/M with toggle to raw float for debugging.

  What modern software does

  ┌────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        Software        │                  Display                   │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Jagannatha Hora        │ Y/M/D/H/M + secondary float for copy-paste │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parashara's Light      │ Y/M/D breakdown (no H/M)                   │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Drik Panchang          │ Y/M/D breakdown + end-date                 │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tamil Vakya Panchangam │ Y/M/D breakdown                            │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Astrosage              │ Y/M/D format                               │
  └────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Worked example

  Chandran balance at birth for a chart with Chandran nakshatra at 50% elapsed:
  - Float: Chandran balance = 15 × (1 − 0.5) = 7.5 years
  - Y/M/D: 7 years 6 months 0 days
  - Y/M/D/H/M: 7 years 6 months 0 days 0 hours 0 minutes

  For a less-round case (Chandran at 23.47% elapsed in Chandran's Mahadasai):
  - Float: 15 × (1 − 0.2347) = 11.480 years
  - Y/M/D/H/M: 11 years 5 months 22 days 21 hours 8 minutes

  Q10 — Dasai balance rendering precision:

  - A) Y/M/D/H/M classical breakdown (matches JH standard). Same for both user types. Store internally as float for computation; render Y/M/D/H/M in UI.
  - B) Y/M/D breakdown (matches Parashara's Light + Drik). Simpler; drop hours/minutes precision.
  - C) Float years + Y/M/D/H/M side-by-side — full transparency; both shown.
  - D) B2C sees Y/M/D; Astrologer sees Y/M/D/H/M — split by user type.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

※ recap: You're reviewing E1a Multi-Dasai v1 PRD (Pass 1 astrologer workflow), locking calculation decisions. Next action: answer Q10 — dasai balance rendering        precision (A: Y/M/D/H/M classical, B: Y/M/D, C: both, D: split by user type).

● Q10 locked: Option A — Y/M/D/H/M classical breakdown for both user types. Store internally as float for arithmetic; render breakdown in UI.                        

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q11 — Timezone for dasai boundary date rendering (final E1a question)
                                                                                                                                                                       Background. Josi computes dasai boundary dates as precise UT moments (e.g., "Venus-Saturn AD begins 2028-06-15 14:23:47 UTC"). But users see dates in a local        timezone. Which timezone?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Three approaches:                                                                                                                                                  

  Approach A — Birthplace timezone always (classical)

  Venus-Saturn AD begins: 15 June 2028 · 19:53 IST   (birthplace: Chennai)

  Classical principle: your chart runs on your birth locale's time. Even if you move to New York, your dasai boundaries are reported in birthplace time. This matches
   how Tamil/Indian astrologers present dasai in printed horoscopes.

  Approach B — Always UTC + UI localizes per viewer

  Venus-Saturn AD begins: 15 June 2028 · 14:23 UTC (or viewer's local tz if selected)

  Server-canonical UTC; client-side rendering in viewer's current timezone. Breaks from classical tradition but aligns with modern web-app norms.

  Approach C — Store UTC + default render birthplace tz + astrologer can toggle

  Server stores UT moments. B2C UI always renders in birthplace timezone (classical). Astrologer gets a "timezone display" toggle: birthplace / current location /   
  UTC.

  Approach D — Birthplace primary + birthday-timezone adjustments

  For each dasai boundary date, render in the timezone that birthplace had on that date (accounts for historical timezone changes: pre-1947 partition in India, DST  
  rule changes, etc.). Most accurate classically but requires pytz-level historical tz awareness.

  Edge cases

  - Diaspora users (user in London, born in Chennai): Approach A shows Chennai time for dasai boundaries. User has to mentally convert. But it's consistent with     
  printed Tamil/Indian panchangam which always shows IST.
  - Users with DST-affected birthplaces (born in Germany, London, US): Birthplace tz changes twice yearly — dasai boundaries that cross DST transitions need to      
  display the timezone at the boundary date, not at birth.
  - Historical birth dates: Timezone rules change (India moved off Madras Time 1947; US DST rules change). Approach D handles this; A/B/C use current timezone rules,
   which is minor inaccuracy.

  Modern software

  ┌─────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            Software             │                         Convention                         │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Jagannatha Hora                 │ Birthplace timezone with historical awareness (Approach D) │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Parashara's Light               │ Birthplace timezone (Approach A)                           │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Drik Panchang                   │ Birthplace timezone (Approach A)                           │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tamil Vakya Panchangam printed  │ Birthplace IST always (Approach A)                         │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Modern web apps (non-astrology) │ User's current tz (Approach B)                             │
  └─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Q11 — Timezone for dasai boundary date rendering:

  - A) Birthplace timezone always (classical). Same for both user types. Historical tz rules use current timezone (minor inaccuracy for pre-1947 or pre-DST-change   
  births).
  - B) Always UTC + UI localizes per viewer — modern web-app norm; breaks classical tradition.
  - C) Store UTC + default render birthplace tz + astrologer toggle — astrologer can switch to viewer tz or UTC for cross-verification.
  - D) Birthplace primary + historical tz awareness — most accurate; uses pytz to apply the timezone rules in effect at each dasai boundary date (e.g., India        
  pre-1947 Madras Time, US DST transitions).
  - E) Other.

  Which?                                                                                                                           Option A for Dwipushkar and Tripushkar                                                                                                                               Also let's change all the names to tamil naming, all nakshatras, planets, zodiac signs okay. ❯ option D

● Q11 locked: Option D — Birthplace primary + historical tz awareness. Uses pytz to apply timezone rules in effect at each dasai boundary date. Most accurate        
  (handles pre-1947 Madras Time, pre-DST-change US births, etc.). Matches Jagannatha Hora convention.

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  E2a Ashtakavargam v2 — Pass 1 kick-off
                                                                                                                                                                       Step A — User story (context only)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        When an astrologer opens a chart's Ashtakavargam panel, they see the fully-purified classical matrices (not just raw BAV) — the output classical Parashari practice   actually uses to judge bhava strength. B2C AI chat synthesizes bhava strength accessibly via 3-tier summary.                                                      

  Step B — Calculation pipeline (E2a inserts after natal chart + panchangam)

  1. Natal chart computed (planets, Lagna, houses, ayanamsa-applied)
  2. Panchangam computed
  3. E2a STAGE 1: Bhinnashtakavargam (BAV) — 8 contributors × 12 rasis matrix per graha (7 grahas + Lagna as contributor)
  4. E2a STAGE 2: Raw Sarvashtakavargam (Raw SAV) — column-sum of all 7 BAVs
  5. E2a STAGE 3: Trikona Shodhanai applied per BAV (Phaladeepika subtract-minimum per DECISIONS 3.x)
  6. E2a STAGE 4: Ekadhipatya Shodhanai applied per BAV (dual-lordship reduction)
  7. E2a STAGE 5: Shodhit SAV — column-sum of doubly-shodhit BAVs
  8. E2a STAGE 6: Sodhya Pinda per graha (Graha Pinda × Rashi Pinda × shodhit BAV total) — feeds E5b Ayus
  9. E2a STAGE 7 (optional): Kaksha Vibhaga — per-graha 96-kaksha subdivision (promoted to in-scope per DECISIONS 1.8)

  Repeat for D9 Navamsam per DECISIONS 1.8 (divisional scope Y).

  What's already locked from DECISIONS.md

  Remaining E2a-specific questions (5)

  - Q1 — Ekadhipatya Shodhanai variant (BPHS 66.6–15 has multiple interpretations)
  - Q2 — Graha Pinda + Rashi Pinda classical table source (BPHS 67 vs commentaries)
  - Q3 — Per-graha maximum bindu totals (classical variants: Sun 48 vs 49 — depends on Asc contributor)
  - Q4 — Contributor-trace storage depth (per-cell attribution for astrologer drill-down)
  - Q5 — Matching bar vs JH 7.x (precision expectation for golden chart fixtures)

  ---
  Q1 — Ekadhipatya Shodhanai variant

  Background. Ekadhipatya Shodhanai (dual-lordship purification, BPHS Ch.66 v.6–15) handles the case when a graha rules two rasis (Sevvai rules Mesham + Viruchigam; 
  Budhan rules Mithunam + Kanni; Guru rules Dhanusu + Meenam; Sukkiran rules Rishabam + Thulam; Sani rules Magaram + Kumbham). For each such pair, we need to        
  reconcile the two rasis' bindu values per a complex set of rules.

  Three classical interpretations exist:

  Variant 1 — BPHS strict (verse 6–15 literal reading)

  For each dual-lordship pair of rasis (Rasi-A and Rasi-B ruled by same graha):
  - If Rasi-A has 0 bindus → Rasi-B gets 0 (regardless of its prior value)
  - If Rasi-B has 0 bindus → Rasi-A gets 0
  - If ruler-graha is in Rasi-A → Rasi-B gets 0; Rasi-A retains
  - If ruler-graha is in Rasi-B → Rasi-A gets 0; Rasi-B retains
  - Otherwise: the rasi with lower bindus gets 0; the higher retains its value

  Aggressive zero-out; significant bindu reduction.

  Variant 2 — Phaladeepika (subtract-lower)

  For each dual-lordship pair:
  - If ruler-graha occupies one of the two rasis, leave both unchanged
  - Otherwise: subtract the lower bindu count from the higher; lower becomes 0; higher retains (higher − lower)

  Less aggressive; preserves more bindu differential.

  Variant 3 — B.V. Raman synthesis (modern Parashari)

  Mixed approach; uses BPHS logic for some cases and Phaladeepika for edge cases. Widely cited in 20th-century practice. Implementation complexity higher.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: BPHS strict (Variant 1)
  - Parashara's Light: BPHS strict (Variant 1)
  - B.V. Raman's published tables: Variant 3 synthesis
  - Tamil Vakya panchangam Ashtakavargam tables: Variant 1

  Consistency note

  Our Trikona Shodhanai is locked to Phaladeepika (subtract-minimum, DECISIONS 1.8). Choosing Variant 2 here (Phaladeepika) would be consistent. Choosing Variant 1  
  (BPHS strict) would mix traditions (Phaladeepika Trikona + BPHS Ekadhipatya). Both are defensible since they're separate reduction operations; but consistency may 
  matter.

  Q1 — Ekadhipatya Shodhanai variant:

  - A) BPHS strict (Variant 1) — matches JH + Parashara's Light + Tamil Vakya. Mixes traditions (Phaladeepika Trikona + BPHS Ekadhipatya) but matches dominant modern
   software.
  - B) Phaladeepika (Variant 2) — consistent with locked Trikona Shodhanai source; less aggressive reduction.
  - C) B.V. Raman synthesis (Variant 3) — widely cited but implementation complexity higher; harder to cross-verify.
  - D) Research — spawn agent to verify Sanskrit interpretation + which commentators favor which variant before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?
 
✻ Baked for 3m 0s

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E2a Q1 locked: Option A — BPHS strict Ekadhipatya Shodhanai (Variant 1). Matches JH + Parashara's Light + Tamil Vakya. Mixes traditions with Phaladeepika Trikona  
  (locked 1.8) but is the dominant modern software convention.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  E2a Q2 — Graha Pinda + Rashi Pinda classical table source                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  Background. Sodhya Pinda per graha (BPHS Ch.67) = shodhit_BAV × Graha_Pinda × Rashi_Pinda. The values of Graha Pinda (per-graha multiplier) and Rashi Pinda          (per-rasi multiplier) come from classical tables.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  BPHS Ch.67 values (standard Parashari)

  Graha Pinda (per-graha multiplier):

  ┌──────────┬─────────────┐
  │  Graha   │ Pinda value │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Sooriyan │      5      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Chandran │      5      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Sevvai   │      8      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Budhan   │      5      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Guru     │     10      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Sukkiran │      7      │
  ├──────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Sani     │      5      │
  └──────────┴─────────────┘

  Rashi Pinda (per-rasi multiplier — 4-fold classification):

  ┌────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┬───────┐
  │   Rasi category    │                 Rasis                 │ Pinda │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ Movable (Chara)    │ Mesham, Kadagam, Thulam, Magaram      │   7   │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ Fixed (Sthira)     │ Rishabam, Simmam, Viruchigam, Kumbham │   8   │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼───────┤
  │ Dual (Dwiswabhava) │ Mithunam, Kanni, Dhanusu, Meenam      │   9   │
  └────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┴───────┘

  (Some BPHS editions reverse this: Chara=8, Sthira=7, Dwi=9. Variant manuscripts disagree.)

  Variant sources

  ┌──────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐    
  │            Source            │                         Graha Pinda                         │               Rashi Pinda               │        Notes         │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ BPHS Ch.67 (R. Santhanam     │ Sooriyan=5, Chandran=5, Sevvai=8, Budhan=5, Guru=10,        │ Chara=7, Sthira=8, Dwi=9                │ Standard             │    
  │ edition)                     │ Sukkiran=7, Sani=5                                          │                                         │                      │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ BPHS Ch.67 (G.C. Sharma      │ Same Graha Pinda                                            │ Chara=8, Sthira=7, Dwi=9                │ Reversed             │    
  │ edition)                     │                                                             │                                         │ Chara/Sthira         │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ Phaladeepika Ch.13           │ Slight variations for Sevvai (8 vs 10)                      │ Same 4-fold split, different values     │ Rare                 │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ Jataka Parijata              │ Similar to Phaladeepika                                     │ Similar                                 │ Even rarer           │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ Jagannatha Hora              │ Santhanam-edition BPHS values                               │ Santhanam Rashi Pinda (Chara=7,         │ Dominant modern      │    
  │ implementation               │                                                             │ Sthira=8, Dwi=9)                        │ default              │    
  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤    
  │ B.V. Raman tables            │ BPHS Santhanam                                              │ BPHS Santhanam                          │ Standard             │    
  └──────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘    

  Why this matters

  Sodhya Pinda feeds directly into E5b Ayurdaya (Pindayu longevity calculation). A change in Graha Pinda by 1 unit per planet can shift Pindayu by ~5-10 years across
   7 planets. Classical consistency matters.

  Modern Parashari consensus

  The Santhanam-edition BPHS (Chara=7, Sthira=8, Dwi=9; Graha Pinda as in table above) is what JH, Parashara's Light, B.V. Raman, most Tamil Vakya panchangams, and  
  most academic Parashari research use. Effective de-facto standard.

  Consistency check

  Josi's nakshatra-percentile source is already locked to Muhurta Chintamani (DECISIONS 3.2). Muhurta Chintamani doesn't specify Graha/Rashi Pinda for Ashtakavargam 
  (different scope — MC covers muhurtham). So picking BPHS Santhanam for Ashtakavargam has no consistency conflict.

  Q2 — Graha Pinda + Rashi Pinda classical source:

  - A) BPHS Ch.67 Santhanam edition — Graha Pinda per standard table; Rashi Pinda Chara=7, Sthira=8, Dwi=9. Matches JH + Parashara's Light + B.V. Raman + Tamil      
  Vakya. Same for both user types.
  - B) BPHS Ch.67 G.C. Sharma edition — reversed Chara/Sthira Rashi Pinda (Chara=8, Sthira=7). Smaller user base but a legitimate BPHS-manuscript variant.
  - C) BPHS Santhanam default + astrologer source selector — ship both BPHS editions + Phaladeepika variant as sibling rules; astrologer toggles per profile.        
  - D) Research — verify which BPHS Sanskrit manuscript variant is more widely accepted as "original" before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

● ⚠️ Important research finding — my earlier framing was incorrect

  The "Santhanam 7/8/9 vs Sharma 8/7/9" three-fold Rashi Pinda I described does NOT appear in any verified classical BPHS source. Honest acknowledgment:

  What I claimed vs what classical BPHS actually says

  What I claimed (incorrect): Rashi Pinda is a 3-fold classification (Chara=7/8, Sthira=8/7, Dwi=9).

  What classical BPHS Ch.69 actually says: Per-sign Rashi Pinda multipliers (one value per rasi), not a 3-fold classification:

  ┌──────────┬───────┬────────────┬───────┐
  │   Rasi   │ Pinda │    Rasi    │ Pinda │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Mesham   │   7   │ Thulam     │   7   │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Rishabam │  10   │ Viruchigam │   8   │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Mithunam │   8   │ Dhanusu    │   9   │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Karkatam │   4   │ Magaram    │   5   │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Simmam   │  10   │ Kumbham    │  11   │
  ├──────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────┤
  │ Kanni    │   6   │ Meenam     │  12   │
  └──────────┴───────┴────────────┴───────┘

  Both Santhanam and Sharma editions print this same per-sign table. The 3-fold 7/8/9 scheme I described is actually Sthira Dasa year counts (BPHS Ch.51), NOT Rashi 
  Pinda. I conflated two different tables.

  The REAL textual variance the research found (verse vs chakra within BPHS Ch.69)

  Graha Pinda has TWO classical readings WITHIN BPHS Ch.69 itself:

  ┌──────────┬───────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┐
  │  Graha   │ Prose verse (literal) │ Reference chakra (tabular) │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sooriyan │           6           │             5              │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Chandran │           6           │             5              │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sevvai   │           3           │             8              │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Budhan   │           6           │             5              │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Guru     │          10           │             10             │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sukkiran │           7           │             7              │
  ├──────────┼───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sani     │           6           │             5              │
  └──────────┴───────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┘

  Both readings appear in the same chapter. Modern software (Jagannatha Hora, Parashara's Light, PyJHora, all Ashtakavargam implementations) universally uses the    
  chakra values, not the verse. The chakra is treated as authoritative despite diverging from literal verse text. 500+ years of working practice override the verse  
  reading.

  Practical impact on Pindayu (E5b feed)

  - Verse-vs-chakra Graha Pinda delta: 5–15 years per planet in Pindayu. Critical.
  - Per-sign Rashi Pinda is not disputed between classical editions (both Santhanam + Sharma print the same table) — so no decision needed here.
  - Hypothetical 3-fold simplifications (if they existed): ≈1–3 years Pindayu delta. Smaller but non-negligible.

  Corrected Q2 — Graha Pinda source (real decision)

  - A) Chakra values (Sooriyan=5, Chandran=5, Sevvai=8, Budhan=5, Guru=10, Sukkiran=7, Sani=5) + per-sign Rashi Pinda (7,10,8,4,10,6,7,8,9,5,11,12). Matches JH +    
  Parashara's Light + all modern software. Same for both user types.
  - B) Verse-literal Graha Pinda (Sooriyan=6, Chandran=6, Sevvai=3, Budhan=6, Guru=10, Sukkiran=7, Sani=6) + per-sign Rashi Pinda. More literal BPHS verse reading   
  but diverges from all modern software.
  - C) Chakra default for both user types + astrologer toggle (ashtakavarga.pinda_source config) to enable verse-literal for comparative research. B2C always chakra.
   ← research agent's recommendation
  - D) Research further — physically verify Santhanam Vol.2 and Sharma Vol.2 to confirm no manuscript variant supports a 3-fold scheme. Defer decision until
  verified.
  - E) Other.

  Research agent's recommendation: Option C. Chakra default (matches all modern practice); verse-literal as astrologer comparative option for research use.

  10, Mithunam 8, Karkatam 4, Simmam 10, Kanni 6, Thulam 7, Viruchigam 8, Dhanusu 9, Magaram 5, Kumbham 11, Meenam 12). Matches JH + Parashara's Light + all modern

  ---
  E2a Q3 — Per-graha maximum bindu totals

  Background. Each graha's BAV has a theoretical max = 8 contributors × 12 rasis = 96 cells, each worth 0 or 1. But the classical contribution rules give each graha 
  a different achievable max (some contributor-rasi combinations are structurally zero). These per-graha maximums matter for:
  - Strength threshold classification ("Budhan at 42 bindus → 78% of his max 54 → strong")
  - Sarvashtakavargam classical max (sum of 7 grahas' maxes)
  - Percentage-based UI rendering

  Classical per-graha maximums (widely-cited):

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┐
  │                Graha                │ Max BAV total │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Sooriyan                            │      48       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Chandran                            │      49       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Sevvai                              │      39       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Budhan                              │      54       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Guru                                │      56       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Sukkiran                            │      52       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Sani                                │      39       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Lagna (when counted as contributor) │      49       │
  └─────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

  SAV classical max = 48+49+39+54+56+52+39 = 337 (if Lagna contributor is counted separately in BAV but not in SAV sum).

  Variance across sources

  - JH implementation: uses 48/49/39/54/56/52/39 (above).
  - Parashara's Light: same.
  - Some Sanskrit commentaries give slightly different maxes (Sevvai 42 in some; Budhan 56 in others) — very rare, not widely adopted.
  - Modern Parashari practice consensus: the 48/49/39/54/56/52/39 set.

  Implementation note

  These maxes are NOT tunable parameters — they're implied by the classical contributor-rule tables. If Josi implements the BPHS contributor rules correctly, the    
  maxes emerge automatically. The decision here is really about:
  1. Whether to assert/validate that computed BAV totals don't exceed these classical maxes (property-test invariant)
  2. How to render percentage-of-max in UI
  3. Whether to show the theoretical max to the astrologer

  Q3 — Per-graha max bindu totals:

  - A) Use classical 48/49/39/54/56/52/39 as property-test invariant + percentage-of-max rendering. Sanity-check all computed BAVs stay within maxes. Render as      
  "Budhan 42/54 = 78%" in astrologer view. Same for both user types.
  - B) Invariant only, no percentage rendering — use maxes as test assertions but surface only raw bindu counts in UI (no percentage). Purer classical display.      
  - C) Percentage rendering only, no invariant assertion — show "Budhan 78%" to user; don't hard-test against expected max (trust BPHS contributor rules
  implementation).
  - D) Both A for astrologer + B for B2C — astrologer sees percentage, B2C sees raw bindus only. Split by user type.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 48s

❯ option A

● E2a Q3 locked: Option A — classical 48/49/39/54/56/52/39 per-graha maxes used as property-test invariant + percentage-of-max rendering in astrologer view. SAV     
  classical max = 337. Same for both user types.

  ---
  E2a Q4 — Contributor-trace storage depth

  Background. Each BAV cell (graha × rasi) has up to 8 contributors that may or may not give a bindu (per BPHS classical contributor-rule tables). For astrologer    
  drill-down, Josi can attribute "why does Sooriyan get 5 bindus in Mesham?" to specific contributors — e.g., "because Chandran + Sevvai + Budhan + Guru + Lagna each
   contributed 1 bindu per their classical rules."

  Storage approaches — trade-off between storage cost and query latency:

  Option A — Bindu count only, no trace

  Store just the bindu value per cell (96 cells × 7 grahas × 2 charts (D1+D9) ≈ 1.3 KB per chart). Astrologer drill-down requires recomputing the contributor rules  
  on demand.

  - Storage: minimal (~1.3 KB/chart)
  - Drill-down latency: ~10-50ms (recompute)
  - Complexity: simpler

  Option B — Bindu count + contributor bitmask per cell

  Store bindu + 8-bit bitmask (one bit per contributor) per cell. Total: ~2.6 KB/chart.

  - Storage: ~2.6 KB/chart
  - Drill-down latency: <1ms (instant lookup)
  - Complexity: moderate

  Option C — Full contributor trace with rule citation per cell

  Store bindu + bitmask + contributor-rule-ID + classical-citation per cell. Total: ~20-50 KB/chart.

  - Storage: ~20-50 KB/chart (substantial for millions of charts)
  - Drill-down latency: <1ms
  - Complexity: high; adds rule-version dependency

  Option D — Compute on demand (no trace stored)

  Same as Option A but explicitly documents the contributor-trace recompute path. Identical storage; explicit UX for astrologer "trace" button.

  Scale implications

  If Josi hits 10M users × avg 2 charts/user = 20M charts × 2.6 KB = 52 GB for Option B. At 100M users, 520 GB. For Option A, that's 26 GB / 260 GB. For Option C,   
  potentially TB-scale.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: recomputes on demand (Option A/D)
  - Parashara's Light: recomputes on demand
  - Kundli by Durlabh: bindu-only; no trace at all

  Practical astrologer need

  Contributor-trace drill-down is a rare usage pattern — astrologers use it for debugging/teaching, not daily reading. Recomputing on-demand (Options A/D) is        
  acceptable given the latency.

  Q4 — Contributor-trace storage depth:

  - A) Bindu-only storage (Option A) + recompute trace on demand. Minimal storage; acceptable drill-down latency. Same for both user types. ← my recommendation.     
  - B) Bindu + bitmask (Option B) — instant drill-down; 2× storage. Worth it if many astrologers use trace frequently.
  - C) Full contributor trace (Option C) — all data materialized; expensive storage; future-proof for advanced research queries.
  - D) Option A for B2C + Option B for Astrologer — astrologer's chart stores bitmask for instant trace; B2C stores bindu-only. Split by user type.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

❯ option A

● E2a Q4 locked: Option A — bindu-only storage, recompute contributor-trace on demand. Minimal storage, acceptable drill-down latency. Same for both user types.     

  ---
  E2a Q5 — Matching bar vs Jagannatha Hora 7.x (final E2a question)

  Background. Same as E1a Q9 but for Ashtakavargam. What's the acceptance threshold for Josi's computed BAV / SAV / Sodhya Pinda values against JH 7.x reference     
  values across 10 golden-chart fixtures?

  Key difference from dasai boundary matching: Ashtakavargam is discrete integer math (contributor rules give 0 or 1 per cell; sums are integers). There's no        
  floating-point cumulative computation like dasai. So exact (±0) match IS achievable if both Josi and JH implement the same classical contributor rules.

  Where divergence from JH could come from

  - Ayanamsa mismatch → planet in different rasi than JH computes → different contributor pattern → different bindu totals. (Fixed once ayanamsa is locked to JH's   
  Lahiri per DECISIONS 1.2.)
  - Node type mismatch (Mean vs True) → Rahu/Ketu in different rasi → different contributions. (Controlled by DECISIONS 1.1 — both computed; pick which to use for   
  BAV.)
  - Contributor-rule interpretation → BPHS contributor tables have ambiguous verses; JH and Josi might interpret differently. (True integer divergence, indicates    
  real classical disagreement.)
  - Lagna contributor inclusion → some implementations count Lagna as 8th contributor; some don't.
  - Ekadhipatya variant → we locked BPHS strict (E2a Q1); must confirm JH uses same.

  Matching tightness options

  Option A — Exact (±0) per-cell match

  Every BAV cell, SAV total, Sodhya Pinda value exactly matches JH. Strict. Detects any implementation divergence immediately.

  Option B — ±1 bindu per cell tolerance

  Allow ±1 bindu drift per cell to accommodate edge-case contributor interpretations. Softer; potentially masks bugs.

  Option C — ±0 post-shodhanai only

  Raw BAV can differ by ±1 bindu (edge cases), but post-Trikona + post-Ekadhipatya shodhit matrices must exact-match. Tolerant of raw computation variance; strict on
   classical-processed output.

  Option D — Exact for Sodhya Pinda + SAV; ±1 for per-cell BAV

  Allow raw matrix variance; require exact totals and final pinda values.

  Modern practice

  - JH self-cross-check with Parashara's Light: ±0 exact match (integer math)
  - Academic Parashari research tools: ±0 exact
  - Tamil Vakya Ashtakavargam printed tables: ±0 exact (typos aside)

  Practical reality

  Ashtakavargam bindu values are deterministic given same inputs. If Josi's ayanamsa + node type + contributor rules match JH, exact match is expected. Any
  divergence SHOULD trigger a bug investigation, not a tolerance allowance.

  Q5 — Matching bar vs JH 7.x for Ashtakavargam:

  - A) Exact (±0) per-cell match — strict; any divergence is a bug; 10 golden charts exact-verify all BAV + SAV + Sodhya Pinda. ← my recommendation (integer math,   
  deterministic).
  - B) ±1 bindu per cell tolerance — softer; accommodates edge-case interpretations.
  - C) ±0 post-shodhanai only; ±1 raw BAV allowed — tolerates raw-matrix edge cases; strict on classical output.
  - D) Exact for SAV + Sodhya Pinda; ±1 per-cell BAV — total-level strictness; cell-level tolerance.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Crunched for 33s

❯ option A

● E2a Q5 locked: Option A — exact (±0) per-cell match against JH 7.x across 10 golden-chart fixtures. Integer math; deterministic; any divergence = bug. Covers BAV +
   SAV + shodhit matrices + Sodhya Pinda.

❯ diffence between A and B                                                                                                                                           
                           
● E4a Yoga Engine MVP vs E6a Transit Intelligence v1 — comparison                                                                                                    

  Both are P1 astrology-heavy PRDs next in queue. Different nature of work.                                                                                             
  E4a — Yoga Engine MVP                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Type: Pattern-recognition engine. Detects 60 named classical yogas (specific planetary configurations) in a natal chart. Each yoga emits: boolean activation +       strength score (0.0–1.0) + classical citation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
  Classical scope — 60 yogas across 6 categories:
  - Pancha Mahapurusha (5) — Ruchaka/Bhadra/Hamsa/Malavya/Sasa
  - Raja Yoga (15) — kingship/power combinations (e.g., Gaja Kesari, Chamara, Parvata)
  - Dhana Yoga (10) — wealth combinations (2nd+11th lord interactions)
  - Chandra Yoga (10) — Moon-based (Sunapha/Anapha/Durudhura/Kemadruma)
  - Surya Yoga (5) — Sun-based (Veshi/Voshi/Ubhayachari)
  - Dushta Yoga (15) — afflicting configurations

  User question answered: "What's special about my chart?"

  Static analysis — takes natal chart → emits set of active yogas. No time dimension.

  E4a-specific decisions to expect in Pass 1 (8-10 questions):
  - Neecha Bhanga cancellation handling (is it a yoga or a post-detection modifier?)
  - Strength formula variants (BPHS vs Phaladeepika strength prescriptions differ)
  - Cross-source yoga variants (BPHS vs Saravali disagree on a few yoga definitions)
  - Predicate vocabulary extension (planet-in-kendra, planet-in-trikona, mutual-aspect, dispositor-of, etc.)
  - Famous-chart fixture set (B.V. Raman Notable Horoscopes citations)
  - Minimum strength threshold for "active"
  - Aspect rules used in predicates (graha drishti — Sevvai 4/8, Guru 5/9, Sani 3/10)
  - Yoga Karaka enumeration per-ascendant (touched by GAP_CLOSURE extensions)

  E6a — Transit Intelligence v1

  Type: Event-stream timing engine. Detects 4 named classical transit patterns and emits timeline of start/end dates per pattern over a 30-year horizon.

  Classical scope — 4 patterns:
  - Sade Sati — Saturn's 7.5-year passage across the 12th/1st/2nd from natal Chandran (3 phases)
  - Kantaka Shani + Ashtama Shani — Saturn in 4th/8th from natal Chandran (~2.5 years each)
  - Major outer-planet ingresses — Jupiter/Saturn/Rahu/Ketu sign changes over 30-year window
  - Eclipses — solar + lunar with natal-point conjunctions (≤3° orb)

  User question answered: "When does my Sade Sati end?" / "What's coming up this year?"

  Dynamic analysis — takes natal chart + ephemeris window → emits timeline of events with precise boundary dates.

  Compute approach: Boundary-event detection (not day-by-day scan). For Sade Sati: find when Saturn crosses 12th-from-Chandran entry, peak (crosses Chandran-sign    
  entry), exit (leaves 2nd-from-Chandran). Events stream to UI + AI chat.

  E6a-specific decisions to expect in Pass 1 (5-7 questions):
  - Node type for Rahu/Ketu ingresses (Mean vs True — already locked in DECISIONS 1.1, just confirm application)
  - Retrograde re-entry handling (Saturn retrogrades back into Sade Sati sign after exiting — how displayed?)
  - Eclipse orb tightness (≤1°, ≤3°, ≤5° for natal-conjunction flagging)
  - 30-year horizon default (or shorter/longer for B2C vs astrologer)
  - Kantaka vs Ashtama Shani classical severity weighting
  - Sade Sati phase labels (rising dhaiya / peak / setting dhaiya vs English labels)
  - Boundary precision matching bar vs JH (±1 day, same as dasai)

  Side-by-side comparison

  ┌───────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Dimension       │            E4a Yoga Engine             │       E6a Transit Intelligence       │
  ├───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Output shape          │ Boolean + strength per yoga × 60 yogas │ Timeline of events × 4 pattern types │
  ├───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Time scope            │ Static (natal)                         │ Dynamic (30-yr window)               │
  ├───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

  ├───────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

  Recommendation

  If you have ~30-45 min: E4a — more decisions but each is cleaner (yoga-by-yoga), broadly parallel structure.

  If you have ~15-25 min: E6a — fewer decisions, more classical-specific detail per decision.

  If you want to alternate rhythm: E6a then E4a — smaller PRD → larger PRD is easier pacing.

B2C   AI chat answers "what's special about my chart?" with named yogas + strength + citation; astrologer workbench shows all 60 yogas with filters by category and       strength.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Step B — Calculation pipeline (E4a inserts after natal chart + DECISIONS locks)                                                                                    

  1. Natal chart computed (planets with Lahiri sidereal positions, Lagna, houses)
  2. Bhava Chalit chart computed parallel (Sripati) per DECISIONS 1.3
  3. Panchangam computed
  4. E4a engine evaluates all 60 yoga rules:
    - Load 60 YAML rule files from src/josi/db/rules/yoga/
    - For each rule: evaluate activation predicate against chart → boolean result
    - If active: evaluate strength formula → float [0.0, 1.0]
    - Emit TechniqueResult per yoga: {activation: bool, strength: float, participating_planets: [...], citation: "BPHS Ch.36 v.14-16"}
  5. E4a output feeds E11a AI Chat (via get_yoga_summary(chart_id, min_confidence))

  Starting with Q1.

  ---
  Q1 — Neecha Bhanga cancellation handling

  Background. Neecha Bhanga (literally "destruction of debility") is a classical rule where a planet's debilitation is cancelled under specific conditions —
  typically producing a Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga (a planet that would be weak becomes exceptionally strong, often indicating rise-from-hardship fame/fortune).        

  Classical cancellation conditions (BPHS Ch.40 v.19-25):
  A debilitated planet's debility is cancelled when:
  1. The lord of the debilitation-sign is in a kendra from Lagna or Chandran, OR
  2. The planet that would be exalted in the same sign is in a kendra from Lagna or Chandran, OR
  3. The debilitated planet itself is aspected by its dispositor, OR
  4. The debilitated planet exchanges with the lord of the sign where it would be exalted

  Question for Josi: should Neecha Bhanga be treated as...

  Approach A — A SEPARATE YOGA in the 60 MVP set

  Add "Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga" as one of the 60 yogas. It detects the cancellation and emits as an activation. Downstream yoga rules (Raja/Dhana) don't need to know
   about it.

  Consequence: if a chart has Sevvai debilitated in Karkatam AND condition 1 holds (Chandran in kendra), the chart shows "Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga" active. Other     
  yogas (Ruchaka Mahapurusha, which requires Sevvai in own/exaltation) don't fire because Sevvai is still in debilitation sign.

  Approach B — A GLOBAL CHART MODIFIER evaluated BEFORE other yogas

  Before evaluating any yoga, pre-process the chart to flag which debilitated planets are "debility-cancelled." When later yogas check "planet in own/exaltation,"   
  they ALSO accept "debility-cancelled."

  Consequence: Sevvai debilitated in Karkatam with Neecha Bhanga can now activate Ruchaka Mahapurusha Yoga (treated as if in exaltation). Classical interpretation   
  says "Neecha Bhanga makes the debilitated planet effectively exalted."

  Approach C — BOTH — Neecha Bhanga as separate yoga AND as a modifier affecting strength only

  Emit "Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga" AND adjust the debilitated planet's strength-multiplier in predicate evaluations (e.g., strength formula for Mars uses its dignity, 
  and Neecha-Bhanga-Mars gets a higher dignity score without changing its SIGN).

  Consequence: Nuanced — Ruchaka doesn't fire (Mars still in debility sign), but Raja Yoga with debilitated-Mars fires at higher strength because Mars is effectively
   strong.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Approach C (emits Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga separately AND adjusts strength)
  - Parashara's Light: Approach A (separate yoga only; doesn't change other yoga activations)
  - B.V. Raman textbooks: Approach B (classical "debility cancelled → treat as exalted")
  - Sanjay Rath / JH lineage modern: Approach C

  Q1 — Neecha Bhanga cancellation handling:

  - A) Separate yoga only — "Neecha Bhanga Raja Yoga" is one of the 60. Doesn't affect other yoga activations. Simpler; matches Parashara's Light.
  - B) Global modifier — treat debility-cancelled planets as effectively exalted for all downstream predicates. Matches classical B.V. Raman interpretation.
  - C) Both (JH synthesis) — separate yoga AND strength-modifier for other yogas. Most nuanced; matches Jagannatha Hora.
  - D) Research — spawn agent to verify which classical reading is correct before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?
