                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q3 — Kaal Sarpa 12 variants implementation detail                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Background. GAP_CLOSURE locks 12 Kaal Sarpa variants for E4b. Each variant is named based on Rahu's house placement (Ketu always opposite). All 12 require the       additional condition: all 7 visible grahas fall between Rahu and Ketu (one hemisphere of the zodiac).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  The 12 variants (classical naming)

  ┌─────┬─────────────┬────────────┬────────────┐
  │  #  │   Variant   │ Rahu house │ Ketu house │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  1  │ Anant       │     1      │     7      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  2  │ Kulik       │     2      │     8      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  3  │ Vasuki      │     3      │     9      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  4  │ Shankapal   │     4      │     10     │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  5  │ Padma       │     5      │     11     │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  6  │ Mahapadma   │     6      │     12     │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  7  │ Takshak     │     7      │     1      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  8  │ Karkotak    │     8      │     2      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │  9  │ Shankhachur │     9      │     3      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │ 10  │ Ghatak      │     10     │     4      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │ 11  │ Vishdhar    │     11     │     5      │
  ├─────┼─────────────┼────────────┼────────────┤
  │ 12  │ Sheshnag    │     12     │     6      │
  └─────┴─────────────┴────────────┴────────────┘

  Key classical question — "all 7 between" condition strictness

  Variant A — Strict (ALL 7 grahas must be between Rahu-Ketu)

  Zero grahas outside the axis. Classical BPHS-adjacent interpretation. Activates on ~15-20% of charts.

  Variant B — Lenient (6 or 7 grahas between; 1 outside allowed)

  1 graha outside axis still counts. ~30% of charts.

  Variant C — Partial Kaal Sarpa / Kaal Amrit (reverse)

  If even ONE graha is outside Rahu-Ketu axis, the yoga is broken (Kaal Amrit — "nectar" not poison). Variants on partiality:
  - Classical strict: only activate if all 7 between
  - Strict + partial flag: detect "Kaal Sarpa active" with flag when 1 graha outside ("partial Kaal Sarpa")

  Variant D — Classical post-BPHS debate (Raman skeptical)

  B.V. Raman and some traditionalists consider Kaal Sarpa post-classical folk astrology, not in BPHS originally. Some Parashari lineages decline to emit Kaal Sarpa. 
  K.N. Rao, PVR Narasimha Rao, Sanjay Rath all DO emit it.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: 12 variants, strict condition (all 7 between); astrologer can see "partial" flag
  - Parashara's Light: 12 variants, lenient condition
  - Astrosage: 12 variants, strict
  - K.N. Rao published treatment: strict (all 7 between)

  Direction convention

  Additional detail — "between Rahu and Ketu" has two possible hemispheres:
  - Savya (forward) direction: counting from Rahu forward to Ketu
  - Apasavya (reverse) direction: counting backward

  Some charts have grahas in BOTH hemispheres; neither is clean Kaal Sarpa. Classical rule: use the hemisphere where grahas are MORE concentrated (majority-side).   

  Q3 — Kaal Sarpa 12 variants implementation:

  - A) Strict "all 7 between" + hemisphere-majority rule + emit named variant (Anant/Kulik/...) + severity label. Matches JH + K.N. Rao + Astrosage. Same for both   
  user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Lenient "6+ between" — 1 graha outside allowed; matches PL.
  - C) Strict + partial-Kaal-Sarpa flag — strict for activation; flag if near-miss (6 grahas between + 1 outside) for astrologer-awareness only.
  - D) Partial/Kaal Amrit logic — emit "Kaal Amrit" reverse yoga when grahas break the axis.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Brewed for 35s

  panel).

  ---
  Q5 — Ship-wave release strategy for 174 E4b yogas

  Background. PRD §2.1 suggested "4 waves of ~50 yogas, each wave golden-green before promotion." With 174 (not 190), waves recalculate to ~43-44 yogas each.        
  Decision: lock the staged release or go big-bang?

  Release options

  Option A — 4 waves of ~43-44 yogas (PRD pattern)

  Each wave passes 3-tier golden fixtures (tier1 synthesized + tier2 public-figure + tier3 Raman 300C) before promotion to production. Failure in wave N blocks waves
   N+1..N+3.

  Wave 1 (Raja + Dhana): 30 + 20 = 50 — highest-value yogas first (most user questions about career/wealth)
  Wave 2 (Chandra + Surya + Dushta): 10 + 5 + 20 = 35 — balancing good + difficult
  Wave 3 (Nabhasa): 32 — structural count-based yogas; unique predicate validation
  Wave 4 (Parivartana + Kaal Sarpa + Dignity + Misc): 15 + 12 + 20 + 10 = 57 — remaining

  Pros: Each wave validates before next; early feedback; progressive rollout. Cons: Longer total timeline.

  Option B — Single big-bang release

  All 174 yogas ship together. Comprehensive but risky — any fixture failure blocks everything.

  Pros: Fast to full coverage. Cons: Regression risk; hard to isolate wave-specific issues.

  Option C — 2 big waves

  Wave 1 (high-value): Raja + Dhana + Chandra + Surya + Dignity = 85
  Wave 2 (remaining): Dushta + Nabhasa + Parivartana + Kaal Sarpa + Misc = 89

  Faster than 4 waves, safer than single release.

  Option D — Category-grouped (10 small waves)

  Each category = one wave. Very granular but operationally heavy.

  Option E — Category-value-ordered (3 value waves)

  Wave 1 "User asks most": Raja + Dhana + Gaja-Kesari-type yogas = ~50
  Wave 2 "Classical breadth": Chandra + Surya + Nabhasa + Parivartana = ~62
  Wave 3 "Advanced/niche": Dushta + Kaal Sarpa + Dignity + Misc = ~62

  Value-weighted; fewer waves than Option A; more grouped than Option C.

  Modern software development practice

  - Jagannatha Hora: Version releases group ~20-50 yogas per version
  - Parashara's Light: Bigger releases (~100 yogas per version)
  - Open-source Parashari tools: Often 1-by-1 contributions

  Q5 — Ship-wave strategy for 174 E4b yogas:

  - A) 4 waves of ~43-44 (PRD pattern) — gradual validation, early feedback.
  - B) Single big-bang — all 174 at once; risky but fast.
  - C) 2 big waves (85 + 89) — compromise.
  - D) Category-grouped (10 small waves) — very granular.
  - E) 3 value-ordered waves — user-intent prioritized.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Cooked for 37s
❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E4b Q5 locked: Option A — 4 waves of ~43-44 yogas. Wave 1: Raja + Dhana (50 yogas, highest-value first). Wave 2: Chandra + Surya + Dushta (35). Wave 3: Nabhasa    
  (32). Wave 4: Parivartana + Kaal Sarpa + Dignity + Misc (57). Each wave passes 3-tier golden fixtures before promotion.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q6 — Authoring workflow for 154 non-fully-specified yogas (final E4b question)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
  Background. E4b PRD §2.1 commits to "full YAML authoring for 20 representative yogas (one per new predicate) + brief rule summaries for remaining ~154." These 154   need full YAML specifications before shipping. How are they authored?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
  Authoring options

  Option A — Incremental authoring during 4 waves (PRD pattern)

  20 reference-implementation yogas authored in E4b implementation phase. Remaining 154 authored incrementally during Wave 1-4 releases. Each wave's specific yogas  
  get full YAML + classical citation + test fixture before wave ships. Astrologer reviewer sign-off per yoga.

  Pros: Staged investment; each wave validated before next; practical SLA. Cons: Authoring spread across 4 release cycles.

  Option B — Upfront full authoring (all 174 before wave 1)

  All 174 YAMLs authored before wave 1 ships. Longer upfront investment; zero authoring during release cycles.

  Pros: All rules reviewed holistically; cross-yoga consistency. Cons: Big upfront time investment; blocks wave 1 on 174 completions.

  Option C — Community contribution workflow

  20 reference yogas authored in-house. Remaining 154 opened for community contribution (astrology enthusiasts / Parashari scholars submit PRs). Josi astrologer     
  reviewer + engineer review each contribution.

  Pros: Scales authoring capacity; engages community. Cons: Quality control overhead; slower; community may not materialize.

  Option D — AI-assisted authoring with astrologer review

  LLM (Claude/GPT-4) drafts full YAML from brief summary + classical citation. Astrologer reviews + corrects. Engineer validates against golden chart fixtures.      

  Pros: 10× faster than hand-authoring; LLM handles boilerplate consistently. Cons: Requires astrologer-review SLA; LLM errors need careful catching.

  Option E — Hybrid: upfront 40 critical + incremental 134

  40 highest-value yogas (top 20% by "astrologer-expected-mention" weight) authored upfront in-house. Remaining 134 via community + AI-assisted during waves 2-4.    

  Operational implications

  - Option A: SLA per wave becomes authoring velocity × 43 yogas. At 1 yoga/day per author, ~2 months per wave. Total: 8 months for waves 1-4.
  - Option D: With AI assistance, can compress to 1-2 weeks per wave if astrologer-reviewer is available.
  - Option C: Depends entirely on community response; unpredictable.

  Modern software practice

  - Jagannatha Hora: Options A + community contribution via forums (P.V.R. Narasimha Rao reviews)
  - Parashara's Light: Option B (upfront authoring in-house)
  - Open-source Parashari: Mostly Option C

  Quality gates

  Regardless of authoring mode, each yoga needs:
  1. Full YAML with activation predicate + strength formula
  2. Classical citation (BPHS chapter/verse OR Saravali OR Phaladeepika)
  3. Tamil phonetic + Sanskrit-IAST names (DECISIONS 1.5)
  4. Test fixture (3-tier per E4a Q7)
  5. Astrologer review sign-off

  Q6 — Authoring workflow for 154 non-fully-specified yogas:

  - A) Incremental authoring during 4 waves (PRD pattern) — 20 upfront + 43 per wave × 4 waves. In-house authoring. Same quality gates per yoga. ← my recommendation 
  (matches PRD; staged investment).
  - B) Upfront full authoring (all 174 before wave 1) — big investment; holistic review.
  - C) Community contribution workflow — 20 in-house + 154 community PRs.
  - D) AI-assisted + astrologer review — LLM drafts, astrologer corrects.
  - E) Hybrid: 40 critical upfront + 134 via AI/community during waves 2-4.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Cooked for 33s

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5 Varshaphala (Tajaka) — Pass 1 kick-off                                                                                                                          
                             
  Step A — User story (context only)                                                                                                                                    
  On user's birthday (or near it), Josi proactively offers: "Your new annual chart just started. Want a read?" Astrologer workbench has a dedicated Varshaphala page   — annual chart + natal chart side-by-side, Muntha position marked, Varsheswara highlighted, 16 Tajaka yogas active, 30 Sahams listed, Mudda Dasha timeline for the   year. Persian-Sanskrit synthesis tradition — distinct machinery from Parashari natal apparatus.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           Step B — Calculation pipeline (E5 inserts as annual-chart-specific engine)                                                                                         

  1. Natal chart computed (natal Sun's exact sidereal longitude recorded)
  2. Panchangam computed
  3. E5 annual chart construction:
    - Find exact moment of solar return (natal Sun longitude reached in target year)
    - Cast full chart at solar-return moment for chosen location
  4. E5 Muntha computation: starting at natal Lagna, progress 1 rasi per elapsed year. Identify Muntha rasi + Muntha lord for this year.
  5. E5 Varsheswara selection: 5-candidate strength contest per Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 — winner = Year Lord.
  6. E5 Tajaka aspect grammar: compute applying/separating aspects between planets in annual chart with per-planet deeptamsha orbs (NOT Parashari V1 boolean).       
  7. E5 Tajaka yogas (16) (moved from E4b per E4b Q4): Ithasala, Isharaaph, Nakta, Yamya, Mama, Manau, Kamboola, Gairi-Kamboola, Khallasara, Radda, Duphali-Kuttha,  
  Dutthotha-Davira, Tambira, Kuttha, Ikkabala, Induvara.
  8. E5 Sahams (30 of 50+): classical Arabic-Parts-style derived points (Punya, Vidya, Vivaha, Jeeva, Mrityu, etc.).
  9. E5 Mudda Dasha: compress 120-year Vimshottari into 1 solar year; starting lord = solar-return Moon's nakshatra mod 9.
  10. E5 Tripathaka Chakra: hourly Tajaka sub-chart for day-of-week-level predictions.
  11. Output: annual_chart_summary shape (year, muntha_sign, muntha_house, year_lord, year_lord_strength, active_sahams, active_yogas, mudda_periods).

  Remaining E5-specific questions (~7)

  - Q1 — Annual chart location (birthplace-default vs current-location vs astrologer-configurable)
  - Q2 — Varsheswara 5-candidate method (strict Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 vs variants)
  - Q3 — Deeptamsha orb table source (Tajaka Neelakanthi vs Varshatantra)
  - Q4 — 30 Sahams selection — which 30 of 50+ ship MVP
  - Q5 — Mudda Dasha variant (Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda vs Patayini/Drig/Trikona-Mudda)
  - Q6 — Tripathaka Chakra scope
  - Q7 — Cross-source aggregation (BPHS Ch.5 vs Tajaka Neelakanthi vs Varshatantra for Tajaka system)

  Starting with Q1.

  ---
  Q1 — Annual chart location

  Background. Varshaphala annual chart is cast at the moment of solar return (Sun returning to natal longitude). Location matters — different locations = different  
  Lagna at same solar-return moment. Classical tradition varies.

  Three location options

  Option A — Birthplace default (classical convention)

  Solar-return chart always cast at birthplace coordinates. Native born in Chennai → annual chart for this year uses Chennai coordinates regardless of where native  
  currently lives.

  Classical rationale: Native's karmic anchor is birthplace. Annual chart runs on birth-locale cosmic coordinates.

  Option B — Current-location (modern Western / some Indian schools)

  Solar-return chart cast at native's current residence coordinates. Annual chart moves with the native.

  Rationale: Native's daily life is at current location; annual events anchor there.

  Option C — Astrologer-configurable per chart

  Astrologer picks per chart based on interpretive context. Different schools use different conventions.

  Option D — Both computed; astrologer picks display

  Compute both; expose both to astrologer; B2C sees birthplace default.

  Classical positions

  - Tajaka Neelakanthi (Neelakantha 16th c): Birthplace default (classical convention)
  - BPHS Ch.5 Varsha: Birthplace
  - Modern Western Solar Return (Sakoian, Jansky): Current location — not classical Vedic
  - Sanjay Rath SJVC: Birthplace default; current-location as experimental toggle
  - K.N. Rao: Birthplace strict
  - B.V. Raman: Birthplace strict

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Birthplace default + astrologer toggle to current location
  - Parashara's Light: Birthplace only
  - Astrosage: Birthplace
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams: Birthplace

  Practical impact

  Native's current location vs birthplace can differ significantly for diaspora users (Chennai-born, London-resident). Annual Lagna can differ by several signs.     
  Changes Muntha, Varsheswara contest results, Saham positions.

  Q1 — Annual chart location:

  - A) Birthplace default — classical Tajaka Neelakanthi convention. Matches JH + PL + K.N. Rao + Raman + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya. Same for both user types.
  - B) Current-location — Western Solar Return convention; breaks classical tradition.
  - C) Astrologer-configurable per chart — astrologer picks per interpretive context.
  - D) Birthplace default + astrologer toggle to current location — classical default; astrologer can experiment. ← my recommendation (matches JH; classically       
  grounded; diaspora flexibility for astrologer).
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 3s

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5 Q1 locked: Option A — Birthplace default. Solar-return annual chart always cast at native's birthplace coordinates regardless of current residence. Matches     
  Tajaka Neelakanthi classical convention + BPHS Ch.5 + JH + PL + K.N. Rao + Raman + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya. Same for both user types. No current-location toggle
  for MVP.                                                                                                                                                              
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q2 — Varsheswara (Year Lord) 5-candidate selection method                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Background. Varsheswara is THE most important planet for the year — determines primary theme, timing of events, and overall year quality. Classical Tajaka           Neelakanthi Ch.2 prescribes a 5-candidate strength contest. Winner = Year Lord.                                                                                    

  The 5 candidates (Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 standard)

  1. Lord of the Muntha rasi (Muntha-dharmaka) — year-point significator
  2. Lord of the annual Lagna (annual-lagna-lord) — solar-return Lagna ruler
  3. Lord of the natal Lagna (natal-lagna-lord) — native's core identity
  4. Sooriyan (representative of the year — since Varshaphala is solar-return based)
  5. Muni — Lord of the trirasipa (lord of the rasi sign occupied by sub-divisor in annual chart) — specific Tajaka calculation

  Some sources substitute #5 with variants. The 5-member contest is classical standard.

  Strength-scoring method variants

  Variant A — Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 strict (scoring table)

  Each candidate scored on 5 strength factors (Uccha/Own-sign/Mooltrikona/Kendra-Trikona/Aspects) per specific Tajaka Neelakanthi verse table. Highest sum wins.     
  Tie-break: classical hierarchy (Sooriyan > natal-lagna-lord > annual-lagna-lord > Muntha-lord).

  Variant B — Shadbala-based scoring

  Use each candidate's SHADBALA (from E19 Shadbalam engine per GAP_CLOSURE) as the scoring input. Highest Shadbala wins.

  Pros: Consistent with strength formula locked for yogas (E4a Q2 Source D = Shadbala-based).
  Cons: Classically a different scoring system; Tajaka uses its OWN strength factors different from Shadbala.

  Variant C — Dignity-only scoring

  Simplified — score = 4 if exalted, 3 if own-sign, 2 if mooltrikona, 1 if friend, 0 if neutral, -1 if enemy, -2 if debilitated. Plus +1 for kendra, +1 for trikona. 

  Pros: Easy to implement; transparent.
  Cons: Loses Tajaka-specific nuance.

  Variant D — Ernst Wilhelm / modern Western-Vedic synthesis

  Western synthesis using modern analytical framework. Not classical Tajaka.

  Variant E — Full Tajaka Neelakanthi weighted sum with tie-break variants

  Option A + additional tie-break rules for edge cases (e.g., when 2 candidates tie, check secondary factors like combustion, retrograde, etc.).

  Classical source weighting

  - Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 (Neelakantha, 16th c) — primary canon
  - Varshatantra (Tajaka commentary) — adds nuance
  - Tajaka Shastra / Tajaka Sara — alternative detailed treatments

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (Tajaka Neelakanthi scoring table) with explicit tie-break
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - Astrosage Varshaphala module: Variant A
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams (where Varshaphala shown): Variant A

  Q2 — Varsheswara selection method:

  - A) Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 strict (5-candidate contest with specific scoring table + classical hierarchy tie-break). Matches JH + PL + Astrosage. Same for both  
  user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Shadbala-based scoring — consistent with E4a Q2 yoga strength framework.
  - C) Dignity-only simplified — easy, transparent, classically less nuanced.
  - D) Ernst Wilhelm Western-Vedic synthesis — not classical Tajaka.
  - E) Option A + extended tie-break variants — Tajaka Neelakanthi core + Varshatantra edge-case tie-breaks.
  - F) Other.
Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 3s

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5 Q1 locked: Option A — Birthplace default. Solar-return annual chart always cast at native's birthplace coordinates regardless of current residence. Matches     
  Tajaka Neelakanthi classical convention + BPHS Ch.5 + JH + PL + K.N. Rao + Raman + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya. Same for both user types. No current-location toggle
  for MVP.                                                                                                                                                              
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q2 — Varsheswara (Year Lord) 5-candidate selection method                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Background. Varsheswara is THE most important planet for the year — determines primary theme, timing of events, and overall year quality. Classical Tajaka           Neelakanthi Ch.2 prescribes a 5-candidate strength contest. Winner = Year Lord.                                                                                    

  The 5 candidates (Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 standard)

  1. Lord of the Muntha rasi (Muntha-dharmaka) — year-point significator
  2. Lord of the annual Lagna (annual-lagna-lord) — solar-return Lagna ruler
  3. Lord of the natal Lagna (natal-lagna-lord) — native's core identity
  4. Sooriyan (representative of the year — since Varshaphala is solar-return based)
  5. Muni — Lord of the trirasipa (lord of the rasi sign occupied by sub-divisor in annual chart) — specific Tajaka calculation

  Some sources substitute #5 with variants. The 5-member contest is classical standard.

  Strength-scoring method variants

  Variant A — Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 strict (scoring table)

  Each candidate scored on 5 strength factors (Uccha/Own-sign/Mooltrikona/Kendra-Trikona/Aspects) per specific Tajaka Neelakanthi verse table. Highest sum wins.     
  Tie-break: classical hierarchy (Sooriyan > natal-lagna-lord > annual-lagna-lord > Muntha-lord).

  Variant B — Shadbala-based scoring

  Use each candidate's SHADBALA (from E19 Shadbalam engine per GAP_CLOSURE) as the scoring input. Highest Shadbala wins.

  Pros: Consistent with strength formula locked for yogas (E4a Q2 Source D = Shadbala-based).
  Cons: Classically a different scoring system; Tajaka uses its OWN strength factors different from Shadbala.

  Variant C — Dignity-only scoring

  Simplified — score = 4 if exalted, 3 if own-sign, 2 if mooltrikona, 1 if friend, 0 if neutral, -1 if enemy, -2 if debilitated. Plus +1 for kendra, +1 for trikona. 

  Pros: Easy to implement; transparent.
  Cons: Loses Tajaka-specific nuance.

  Variant D — Ernst Wilhelm / modern Western-Vedic synthesis

  Western synthesis using modern analytical framework. Not classical Tajaka.

  Variant E — Full Tajaka Neelakanthi weighted sum with tie-break variants

  Option A + additional tie-break rules for edge cases (e.g., when 2 candidates tie, check secondary factors like combustion, retrograde, etc.).

  Classical source weighting

  - Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 (Neelakantha, 16th c) — primary canon
  - Varshatantra (Tajaka commentary) — adds nuance
  - Tajaka Shastra / Tajaka Sara — alternative detailed treatments

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (Tajaka Neelakanthi scoring table) with explicit tie-break
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - Astrosage Varshaphala module: Variant A
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams (where Varshaphala shown): Variant A

  Q2 — Varsheswara selection method:

  - A) Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 strict (5-candidate contest with specific scoring table + classical hierarchy tie-break). Matches JH + PL + Astrosage. Same for both  
  user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Shadbala-based scoring — consistent with E4a Q2 yoga strength framework.
  - C) Dignity-only simplified — easy, transparent, classically less nuanced.
  - D) Ernst Wilhelm Western-Vedic synthesis — not classical Tajaka.
  - E) Option A + extended tie-break variants — Tajaka Neelakanthi core + Varshatantra edge-case tie-breaks.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Worked for 48s

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5 Q2 locked: Option A — Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 strict 5-candidate contest. Candidates: Muntha-rasi lord, annual-Lagna lord, natal-Lagna lord, Sooriyan, Muni     
  (trirasipa lord). Scoring per classical table (Uccha/Own-sign/Mooltrikona/Kendra-Trikona/Aspects). Tie-break hierarchy: Sooriyan > natal-lagna-lord >
  annual-lagna-lord > Muntha-lord. Matches JH + PL + Astrosage. Same for both user types.                                                                               
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q3 — Deeptamsha (Tajaka aspect orb) table source                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Background. Tajaka aspects (applying/separating — tajaka_applying_aspect and tajaka_separating_aspect predicates per E4b Q2) use per-planet deeptamsha orbs — the    "fullness orb" within which an aspect is considered operative. Each graha has a DIFFERENT orb value (unlike Parashari's boolean 7th-or-nothing). This is distinct  
  machinery from Parashari graha drishti.

  Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.3 standard deeptamsha table

  ┌──────────┬────────────────┐
  │  Graha   │ Deeptamsha orb │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Sooriyan │      15°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Chandran │      12°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Sevvai   │       8°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Budhan   │       7°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Guru     │       9°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Sukkiran │       7°       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────┤
  │ Sani     │       9°       │
  └──────────┴────────────────┘

  Tajaka orb applies to both applying and separating aspect computations. Rahu/Ketu typically get no Tajaka-specific orb (use 7° conventionally).

  Variants

  Variant A — Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.3 standard (above table)

  Classical canon. Widely adopted.

  Variant B — Varshatantra variant

  Slightly different for Sani and Sevvai:
  - Sevvai: 9° (instead of 8°)
  - Sani: 10° (instead of 9°)

  Minor differences; used by some commentators.

  Variant C — Modern synthesis (Ernst Wilhelm / Rath)

  Western-Vedic synthesis adjusts some values based on observed precision:
  - Outer planets (Guru, Sani): 10° (slightly larger)
  - Inner planets (Sevvai, Budhan, Sukkiran): 6° (tighter)

  Not classical; modern empirical adjustment.

  Variant D — Persian/Arabic origin table

  Original Persian Tajaka tradition (Al-Biruni era) had different orb values:
  - Sooriyan: 17°
  - Chandran: 12.5°
  - Others similar

  Historical; not used in Indian Tajaka practice since Neelakanthi's 16th-c synthesis.

  Classical position

  Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.3 is THE canonical source for Indian Tajaka deeptamsha. All modern Indian Tajaka software uses this table.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (Tajaka Neelakanthi standard)
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - Astrosage Varshaphala: Variant A
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams: Variant A

  Practical impact

  Variant A vs B differs for only 2 grahas by 1°. Affects 5-10% of aspect activations near orb boundaries. Small but real.

  Q3 — Deeptamsha orb table source:

  - A) Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.3 standard — Sooriyan 15°, Chandran 12°, Sevvai 8°, Budhan 7°, Guru 9°, Sukkiran 7°, Sani 9°. Rahu/Ketu 7° convention. Matches JH + PL +
   Astrosage. Same for both user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Variant B — Varshatantra (Sevvai 9°, Sani 10°) — minor commentary variant.
  - C) Variant A default + astrologer toggle to Varshatantra (Variant B) — per E1b Q7 convention.
  - D) Modern synthesis (Ernst Wilhelm outer=10°, inner=6°) — non-classical empirical.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Cogitated for 31s
  Q4 — 30 Sahams selection (of 50+)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Background. Sahams are classical Tajaka's Arabic-Parts-style derived points — computed from specific planet+house formula combinations (e.g., Punya Saham =          Chandran + Lagna − Sooriyan). 50+ Sahams exist in classical Tajaka Neelakanthi. PRD commits to 30 MVP covering "≥95% of astrologer citations."                                                                                                                                                                                          
  Proposed 30 MVP Sahams (PRD §2.1 list)

  Per E5 PRD: Punya, Vidya, Yasas, Mitra, Mahatmya, Guru, Karma, Aishwarya, Vivaha, Putra, Jeeva, Marana, Shatru, Jaya, Karya, Roga, Vrana, Bandhu, Mrityu, Paradesh,
   Artha, Paradara, Karmagaha, Vanik, Vanija, Gaja, Ashwa, Bhratru, Punar, Satya.

  Coverage breakdown by theme

  ┌───────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Theme       │                  Sahams                  │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Virtue/dharma     │ Punya, Yasas, Mahatmya, Satya            │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Education         │ Vidya, Guru                              │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Career/profession │ Karma, Karmagaha, Vanik, Vanija          │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Wealth            │ Aishwarya, Artha                         │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Family            │ Vivaha, Putra, Bhratru, Bandhu, Paradara │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Health/death      │ Jeeva, Roga, Vrana, Marana, Mrityu       │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Conflict          │ Shatru, Jaya, Karya                      │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Transport/travel  │ Gaja, Ashwa, Paradesh                    │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Misc/timing       │ Mitra, Punar                             │
  └───────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Variants

  Option A — PRD-proposed 30

  Balanced across 9 themes. Covers most-cited Sahams per classical Tajaka Neelakanthi.

  Option B — Alternative 30 (different selection)

  Drop some less-used (e.g., Vanik, Vanija, Gaja, Ashwa — commerce/transport specialized) and add more widely-cited (e.g., Dukha Saham, Mula Saham, Sheela Saham,    
  Labha Saham).

  Option C — Ship ALL 50+ for comprehensive coverage

  No 30-item selection decision. Full Tajaka Saham corpus. More compute; more UI load; complete.

  Option D — Minimal 15 Sahams MVP + expand to 30 in E5b

  Tighter MVP: drop niche Sahams (Gaja, Ashwa, Vanik, Vanija, Karmagaha, Bhratru, Paradara, Punar, Satya, Paradesh, Mrityu, Vrana, Mahatmya, Bandhu, Yasas). Ship 15 
  core. Extend later.

  Option E — Research which Sahams most cited by modern Parashari-Tajaka authorities

  Survey B.V. Raman, K.N. Rao, Sanjay Rath, P.V.R. Narasimha Rao, Ernst Wilhelm classical-chart treatments to identify the actual 30 most-used.

  Modern software coverage

  - Jagannatha Hora: 30-50 Sahams
  - Parashara's Light: 30 Sahams (similar to PRD proposed)
  - Astrosage: 15 Sahams (lighter)

  Q4 — 30 Sahams selection:

  - A) PRD-proposed 30 — balanced across 9 themes, matches JH + PL standard. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Alternative 30 — tell me which to swap in/out.
  - C) Ship all 50+ — comprehensive coverage.
  - D) Minimal 15 MVP — tighter scope.
  - E) Research — verify with modern authorities before locking.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Churned for 33s

❯ option A
Q5 — Mudda Dasha variant                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  Background. Mudda Dasha compresses the 120-year Vimshottari cycle into 1 solar year for intra-year timing. Provides Mahadasa → Antardasa granularity WITHIN the      annual Varshaphala chart. Multiple classical variants exist for how the starting lord is selected and how sub-periods are distributed.                             

  Four classical Mudda Dasha variants

  Variant A — Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda (PRD current)

  - Starting lord determined by solar-return Chandran's nakshatra lord modulo 9
  - Sub-periods follow Vimshottari proportions (Ketu 7/120 of year, Sukkiran 20/120 of year, etc.)
  - Most widely used — standard in modern Indian Tajaka practice
  - Source: Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.7

  Variant B — Patayini-Mudda

  - Starting lord determined by the lord of the Patayini sub-division (a specific year-point computation)
  - Different sub-period proportions
  - Used in some North Indian Tajaka lineages
  - Source: Varshatantra commentary

  Variant C — Drig-Mudda (aspect-based)

  - Starting lord determined by which graha aspects the annual Lagna most strongly
  - Used for aspect-driven timing questions within the year
  - Source: Some Tajaka commentaries

  Variant D — Trikona-Mudda (trine-based)

  - Starting lord based on trinal house placements from annual Lagna
  - Rare; used in specific lineage research

  PRD scope

  E5 PRD §2.2 out-of-scope note: "Additional Mudda variants — only Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda (most widely used) ships in E5. Patayini-Mudda, Drig-Mudda, Trikona-Mudda  
  deferred."

  Options

  Option A — Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda only (PRD current)

  Ship 1 variant. Covers 95%+ of Tajaka astrologer usage.

  Option B — Ship all 4 variants

  Full Mudda coverage; astrologer picks per question-context. More compute + UI complexity.

  Option C — Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda default + Patayini-Mudda as astrologer toggle

  Per E1b Q7 convention (default + 1 alternate). Mid-scope.

  Option D — Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda default + ALL 3 others deferred to E5 v2 or future Tajaka enhancement PRD

  Formalize the deferral path (since E5b is Ayus, not Tajaka). Create E5c or future E5-enhancement for Patayini/Drig/Trikona.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda (Variant A) primary; Patayini as toggle
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A only
  - Astrosage: Variant A only
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams (where Varshaphala shown): Variant A only

  Q5 — Mudda Dasha variant scope:

  - A) Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda only — PRD current. Matches PL + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya. Same for both user types.
  - B) All 4 variants — full coverage.
  - C) Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda default + Patayini-Mudda astrologer toggle — matches JH convention + E1b Q7 pattern. ← my recommendation (astrologer flexibility;      
  classically grounded).
  - D) Variant A only + formal E5-enhancement PRD for future Patayini/Drig/Trikona — defers other 3 variants cleanly.
  - E) Other.

  Which?
✻ Brewed for 35s

❯ option C

● E5 Q5 locked: Option C — Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda default + Patayini-Mudda astrologer-profile toggle. Matches JH convention + E1b Q7 pattern. B2C always sees        
  Yoga-Vimshottari-Mudda; astrologer can switch to Patayini. Drig-Mudda and Trikona-Mudda deferred (future enhancement PRD).

  ---
  Q6 — Tripathaka Chakra scope

  Background. Tripathaka Chakra is an hourly Tajaka sub-chart — a mini-Varshaphala computed for each hour of the day within the annual chart context. Used for       
  day-of-week-level predictions and fine-grained timing within the Varshaphala year.

  Classical source: Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.8.

  How it works

  For each hour in the year, compute a "mini chart" anchored on the hour's boundary. Identify:
  - Hourly Muntha position
  - Hourly Varsheswara (different from annual Varsheswara)
  - Hourly Tajaka yogas active in the micro-window

  Data volume: 365 days × 24 hours = 8,760 hourly charts per year. Compute-intensive.

  Scope options

  Option A — Full Tripathaka Chakra per PRD scope (hourly)

  8,760 hourly charts computed on-demand. Astrologer queries "Tripathaka for this specific hour" and gets full analysis. Used in muhurta-style precise timing.       

  Pros: Classical completeness.
  Cons: Compute-intensive; ~95% of users never query; storage cost.

  Option B — Skip Tripathaka for MVP

  Defer to future E5-enhancement PRD. Tajaka MVP ships without hourly-precision Tripathaka. Annual + Mudda Dasha timing suffice for most Varshaphala reading.        

  Pros: Tighter MVP scope; matches typical PL/Astrosage treatment (neither ships full Tripathaka).
  Cons: Classical completeness gap; research-astrologers may note absence.

  Option C — Daily (not hourly) Tripathaka — simplified

  365 daily charts per year (not hourly). 24× less compute than full. Captures day-level patterns (Tuesday vs Friday annual-context differences) without hour-level  
  granularity.

  Pros: Partial classical coverage; manageable compute.
  Cons: Not strictly classical (Tripathaka IS hourly by classical definition).

  Option D — Astrologer-only + on-demand compute

  Ship full hourly Tripathaka infrastructure but compute ONLY when astrologer specifically requests it. Lazy evaluation. B2C never sees Tripathaka.

  Pros: Classical completeness available; zero compute cost when unused.
  Cons: Implementation complexity.

  Option E — Defer Tripathaka to future PRD (clean scope)

  Formally defer. E5 MVP = annual chart + Muntha + Varsheswara + Tajaka yogas + 30 Sahams + Mudda Dasha (Option A locked). Tripathaka in E5c or future enhancement.  

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Full hourly Tripathaka available; on-demand compute
  - Parashara's Light: NOT implemented
  - Astrosage Varshaphala: NOT implemented
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams: NOT implemented (too rare in daily use)

  Classical usage frequency

  Tripathaka Chakra is highly niche. Even among Tajaka specialists, ~5% of charts use Tripathaka. Most annual readings end at Mudda Dasha level granularity.

  Q6 — Tripathaka Chakra scope:

  - A) Full hourly Tripathaka (PRD scope) — classical completeness; 8,760 hourly charts/year; on-demand compute.
  - B) Skip for MVP — defer to E5-enhancement PRD; matches PL + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya (none ship it). ← my recommendation (tight MVP; rare classical usage; can add
   later).
  - C) Daily simplified — 365 daily charts; not strictly classical but manageable.
  - D) Astrologer-only + on-demand compute — full classical infrastructure, lazy evaluation.
  - E) Formally defer to future PRD — clean scope boundary; same practical effect as B.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Brewed for 30s

❯ option B

● E5 Q6 locked: Option B — Skip Tripathaka Chakra for MVP. Defer to E5-enhancement PRD. Matches PL + Astrosage + Tamil Vakya (none ship hourly Tripathaka). Classical
   usage frequency ~5% even among Tajaka specialists; tight MVP scope acceptable.

  ---
  Q7 — Cross-source aggregation for E5 (final E5 question)

  Background. Classical Tajaka has multiple authoritative sources with minor commentary variations:

  - Tajaka Neelakanthi (Neelakantha, 16th c) — primary Sanskrit Tajaka canon
  - BPHS Ch.5 Varshaphala — Parashari-lineage Tajaka treatment (briefer)
  - Varshatantra — Tajaka commentary with edge-case nuances
  - Persian/Arabic origin texts — historical precursors (Al-Biruni, etc.) — not used in modern Indian Tajaka

  Variations surface in E5 techniques

  - Muntha progression: 1 sign/year (all sources agree) — no variance
  - Varsheswara 5-candidate contest: Tajaka Neelakanthi Ch.2 canonical (locked Q2) — BPHS Ch.5 barely mentions; Varshatantra has extended tie-break rules
  - Sahams formulas: ~5-7 Sahams have minor formula variants between Tajaka Neelakanthi and Varshatantra (~10% of the 30)
  - 16 Tajaka yogas: ~2-3 yogas have commentary variants between TN and Varshatantra

  Options (matching E1b Q7 + E3 Q6 convention)

  Option A — Tajaka Neelakanthi canonical only

  Single-source Tajaka. No astrologer toggles for Varshatantra. Simplest.

  Option B — Tajaka Neelakanthi default + Varshatantra commentary variant per technique, astrologer toggle

  Matches E1b Q7 + E3 Q6 convention. Each Tajaka technique (Sahams, yogas, tie-breaks) ships TN canonical as default + Varshatantra variant as astrologer-profile    
  toggle (where classical variance exists).

  Option C — Tajaka Neelakanthi + BPHS Ch.5 Parashari-synthesis

  Use TN for Tajaka-specific machinery (aspects, yogas, Sahams); BPHS Ch.5 framework for overall Varshaphala structure. Per-technique curation.

  Option D — Full F8 aggregation with confidence indicator

  All variants as sibling rules; confidence-weighted aggregation for B2C; astrologer sees all with confidence. Higher complexity.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Option B (Tajaka Neelakanthi default + Varshatantra variant toggle) — matches E1b Q7 convention
  - Parashara's Light: Option A (TN only)
  - Astrosage Varshaphala: Option A
  - Sanjay Rath SJVC: Option B

  Consistency with E1b Q7 + E3 Q6 convention

  Both E1b and E3 locked "canonical default + 1 variant per technique with astrologer toggle." Extending same convention to E5 gives parallel astrologer experience  
  across Jaimini (E1b/E3) and Tajaka (E5) tradition layers.

  Q7 — Cross-source aggregation for E5:

  - A) Tajaka Neelakanthi canonical only — simplest; matches PL + Astrosage.
  - B) Tajaka Neelakanthi default + Varshatantra variant per technique, astrologer toggle — matches E1b Q7 + E3 Q6 + JH convention + Sanjay Rath SJVC. ← my
  recommendation (consistency across tradition layers).
  - C) TN + BPHS Ch.5 Parashari-synthesis — per-technique curation.
  - D) Full F8 aggregation with confidence indicator — maximum tooling.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

❯ option B

● E5 Q7 locked: Option B — Tajaka Neelakanthi canonical default + Varshatantra commentary variant per technique as astrologer-profile toggle. Matches E1b Q7 + E3 Q6 
  convention + JH + Sanjay Rath SJVC. Consistency across tradition layers (Parashari canonical defaults + classical commentary variants throughout).

      109 +annual_chart_summary = {                                                                                                                                  
      110 +    "year": int,                         # Solar-return year (e.g., 2026)                                                                                 
      111 +    "solar_return_moment_utc": datetime, # Exact moment Sun reached natal longitude

      114 +    "year_lord": str,                    # Varsheswara (e.g., "Sooriyan")                                                                                 
      115 +    "year_lord_strength": float,         # 0.0-1.0 strength score from 5-candidate contest

      117 +    "active_yogas": list[TajakaYoga],    # 16 Tajaka yogas with activation + strength                                                                     
      118 +    "mudda_periods": list[TemporalRange],# Mudda Dasha MD/AD/PD periods for this year                                                                     
      119 +    "chart_context": "varshaphala",      # Discriminator for yoga engine gating                                                                           
      120 +    "ayanamsa_used": "lahiri",

❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5b Full Ayus (Longevity) Suite — Pass 1 kick-off                                                                                                                  
                             
  Step A — User story (context only)                                                                                                                                    
  Critical ethical framing: Longevity predictions are gated behind professional astrologer workbench. B2C AI chat declines to answer "how long will I live?" and       routes users to marketplace astrologer consultations. On the astrologer workbench, all 4 classical methods (Pindayu, Amshayu, Nisargayu, Jaimini Ayur-dasha)         compute together for ayur-nirnaya (longevity determination) — the classical practice of cross-reading multiple methods to assess agreement before any                interpretation. Astrologer uses ayus for framing (ayus-window bounds for dasha relevance), not for direct prediction to client.                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  Step B — Calculation pipeline (E5b inserts after E2a)

  1. Natal chart + panchangam computed
  2. E2a Ashtakavargam + Sodhya Pinda (per graha) completed — E5b depends on this
  3. E5b Pindayu engine (BPHS Ch.72):
    - Per-planet planet_ayus from strength in sign (exaltation/debilitation modifiers)
    - Apply 5 classical harana (subtraction) factors: Chakra, Kroorasthana, Astangata, Shatrukshetra, Sheersha-odaya
    - Final: pindayu = Σ (planet_ayus_i × Π harana_factor_i_j)
  4. E5b Amshayu engine (BPHS Ch.73):
    - Per-planet contribution from Navamsa (D9) position relative to natal sign
    - Same 5 haranas applied
  5. E5b Nisargayu engine (BPHS Ch.74):
    - Fixed per-planet natural lifespan (Sooriyan 19, Chandran 25, Sevvai 15, Budhan 12, Guru 15, Sukkiran 21, Sani 20 = ~120 yr total)
    - Reduced by 5 haranas
  6. E5b Jaimini Ayur-dasha (Jaimini Upadesha Sutras Book 2):
    - 3-category determination: Alpa (short) / Madhya (medium) / Deergha (long)
    - Based on lord-of-8th placement, Chara Karaka placements, AL strength
  7. Output: longevity_summary with all 4 method outputs + ayur-nirnaya consensus flag
  8. Gated surfacing: B2C AI chat sees scripted refusal; astrologer workbench sees full data

  Starting with Q1.

  ---
  Q1 — Pindayu 5 harana factors source

  Background. Pindayu (BPHS Ch.72) uses 5 harana (subtraction) factors to reduce each planet's base ayus contribution before summing. The 5 haranas:

  ┌───────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
  │        Harana         │                              What reduces ayus                               │  Classical source  │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │ Chakra harana         │ Debility-based — planets in 6/8/12 houses lose a fraction                    │ BPHS Ch.72 v.6-9   │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │ Kroorasthana harana   │ Further subtraction for malefics in "trouble positions" (6/8/12 + afflicted) │ BPHS Ch.72 v.10-13 │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │ Astangata harana      │ Combustion — planets within 6° of Sooriyan lose portion                      │ BPHS Ch.72 v.14-17 │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │ Shatrukshetra harana  │ Planets in enemy signs lose fraction                                         │ BPHS Ch.72 v.18-20 │
  ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │ Sheersha-odaya harana │ Based on Lagna rising/non-rising sign classification                         │ BPHS Ch.72 v.21-23 │
  └───────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┘

  Classical source variants

  Variant A — BPHS Ch.72 5-harana standard

  All 5 haranas as listed above. Standard modern Parashari implementation. Matches JH + PL + B.V. Raman.

  Variant B — Phaladeepika modified 3-harana

  Mantreswara's Phaladeepika Ch.7 uses only 3 haranas (Chakra, Kroorasthana, Astangata). Drops Shatrukshetra and Sheersha-odaya as "disputed."

  Pros: Simpler; less aggressive reduction.
  Cons: Classically incomplete; breaks from BPHS canonical.

  Variant C — BPHS + Phaladeepika synthesis (modern)

  Use all 5 BPHS haranas; for ambiguous edge cases (e.g., Shatrukshetra boundary cases), apply Phaladeepika's softer interpretation.

  Variant D — Jataka Parijata extended (7 haranas)

  Some commentaries add 2 more haranas: Retrograde-affliction harana + Bhava-affliction harana. Rare; mostly research-only.

  Practical impact

  - Variant A (BPHS 5): Pindayu typically ~60-80 years (moderate reduction)
  - Variant B (Phaladeepika 3): Pindayu ~75-95 years (less reduction)
  - Variant D (extended 7): Pindayu ~50-70 years (more reduction)

  Delta between variants A and B: ~10-15 years per chart. Significant for ayur-nirnaya cross-validation.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (BPHS 5-harana)
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - B.V. Raman's treatment: Variant A (canonical BPHS)
  - Astrosage: Variant A
  - K.N. Rao: Variant A primary + mentions Phaladeepika variant

  Q1 — Pindayu harana factors source:

  - A) BPHS Ch.72 5-harana standard (Chakra, Kroorasthana, Astangata, Shatrukshetra, Sheersha-odaya) — matches JH + PL + Raman + Astrosage + K.N. Rao. Same for both 
  user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Phaladeepika 3-harana — simpler, less aggressive.
  - C) BPHS 5-harana default + Phaladeepika synthesis astrologer toggle — matches E1b Q7 convention.
  - D) Jataka Parijata extended 7-harana — rare, research-only.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

● E5b Q1 locked: Option A — BPHS Ch.72 5-harana standard (Chakra, Kroorasthana, Astangata, Shatrukshetra, Sheersha-odaya). Matches JH + PL + Raman + Astrosage + K.N.
   Rao. Same for both user types.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q2 — Amshayu harana application                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  Background. Amshayu (BPHS Ch.73) uses Navamsa (D9) positions to compute per-planet ayus contribution — distinct from Pindayu's rasi-based computation. Question:     which haranas apply to Amshayu's base values?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  Classical variants

  Variant A — Same 5 haranas as Pindayu (BPHS Ch.73 standard)

  BPHS Ch.73 v.2 states haranas apply to Amshayu "as in Pindayu." Same 5 subtraction factors (Chakra, Kroorasthana, Astangata, Shatrukshetra, Sheersha-odaya) applied
   to Amshayu base values.

  Classical rationale: Amshayu differs from Pindayu only in base computation method (Navamsa vs rasi); harana reductions conceptually apply equally.

  Variant B — Reduced 2-3 haranas (Phaladeepika variant)

  Phaladeepika Ch.7 argues Amshayu already captures some nuance via Navamsa positions; therefore apply only:
  - Chakra harana (debility-based)
  - Astangata harana (combustion)

  Skip Kroorasthana, Shatrukshetra, Sheersha-odaya as "redundant given Navamsa dignity."

  Variant C — Different set (Amshayu-specific)

  Some commentaries prescribe:
  - Navamsa-Chakra harana (Navamsa-specific debility)
  - Navamsa-Astangata harana (combustion in Navamsa context)
  - Standard Sheersha-odaya harana

  Classically rare; used by specific lineages.

  Variant D — All 5 haranas + additional Navamsa-specific adjustment

  BPHS 5 haranas + 1 additional Navamsa-dignity harana. Most aggressive reduction.

  Practical impact

  - Variant A (5 haranas): Amshayu typically ~50-75 years (aggressive reduction)
  - Variant B (2 haranas): Amshayu ~80-100 years (mild reduction)

  Delta: ~15-25 years between A and B. Major difference in ayur-nirnaya cross-validation.

  Classical source weighting

  - BPHS Ch.73 v.2 explicitly says "as in Pindayu" → endorses Variant A
  - Phaladeepika Ch.7 argues for Variant B
  - Modern consensus follows BPHS literal reading (Variant A)

  Consistency with Q1

  Q1 locked BPHS 5-harana for Pindayu. Applying same 5 to Amshayu (Variant A) is the consistent classical choice. Variant B would introduce asymmetric treatment     
  between Pindayu and Amshayu.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (same 5 haranas)
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - B.V. Raman's treatment: Variant A
  - K.N. Rao: Variant A primary
  - Astrosage: Variant A

  Q2 — Amshayu harana application:

  - A) Same 5 haranas as Pindayu (BPHS Ch.73 standard) — consistent with Q1 lock; matches JH + PL + Raman + Astrosage + K.N. Rao. Same for both user types. ← my     
  recommendation.
  - B) Phaladeepika 2-harana reduced — Chakra + Astangata only.
  - C) Navamsa-specific haranas — classically rare variant.
  - D) All 5 + 1 Navamsa-dignity additional — most aggressive.
  - E) Variant A default + Phaladeepika variant astrologer toggle — per E1b Q7 convention.
  - F) Other.

  Which? 
  Which?

  Q3 — Jaimini Ayur-dasha 3-category year thresholds                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Background. Jaimini Ayur-dasha (Jaimini Upadesha Sutras Book 2) classifies longevity into 3 categories: Alpa (short), Madhya (medium), Deergha (long).               Classification based on lord-of-8th placement, Chara Karaka configurations, AL strength. The classification outputs a category, but the year-range for each          category depends on classical source.                                                                                                                              

  Classical threshold variants

  Variant A — BPHS/Jaimini Sutras standard thresholds

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────────┐
  │    Category     │  Year range  │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Alpa (short)    │ 0-32 years   │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Madhya (medium) │ 33-70 years  │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Deergha (long)  │ 71-100 years │
  └─────────────────┴──────────────┘

  Most widely cited. Matches Jaimini Upadesha Sutras commentary interpretations.

  Variant B — Alternative classical (Parashara-adjacent)

  ┌──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Category │             Year range              │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Alpa     │ 0-30 years                          │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Madhya   │ 30-60 years                         │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Deergha  │ 60+ years (often capped at 100-108) │
  └──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘

  BPHS Ch.74 Nisargayu-influenced interpretation (since 120-year natural total divided roughly into thirds).

  Variant C — Irangati Rangacharya extended

  4-category refinement adding "Ati-Deergha" (extra-long, 100+ years). Modern adjustment for contemporary life expectancy.

  Variant D — Modern-adjusted thresholds

  Alpa: 0-40, Madhya: 40-75, Deergha: 75+ — reflecting modern life expectancy ~75-80 years where Madhya should be "reasonable lifespan."

  Pros: More realistic. Cons: Not classical.

  Variant E — Classical thresholds + B.V. Raman's "with-uncertainty" annotation

  Variant A thresholds + explicit uncertainty band per Raman: "Ayus classification is indicative within ±10 years; cross-validate with Pindayu/Amshayu/Nisargayu     
  before finalizing interpretation."

  Practical impact

  Classification is a SIGNAL for ayur-nirnaya, not a precise prediction. Category crossovers near boundaries (65-year-old chart classified Madhya but close to       
  Deergha boundary) need soft interpretation.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Variant A (classical BPHS)
  - Parashara's Light: Variant A
  - K.N. Rao's published treatment: Variant A with ±10 year soft annotation
  - Astrosage: Variant B
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams: Variant A

  Q3 — Jaimini Ayur-dasha thresholds:

  - A) BPHS/Jaimini Sutras standard — Alpa 0-32, Madhya 33-70, Deergha 71-100. Matches JH + PL + K.N. Rao + Tamil Vakya. Same for both user types.
  - B) Alternative classical — Alpa 0-30, Madhya 30-60, Deergha 60+. Matches Astrosage.
  - C) 4-category Rangacharya extended — adds Ati-Deergha 100+ for modern longevity.
  - D) Modern-adjusted — Alpa 0-40, Madhya 40-75, Deergha 75+. Non-classical.
  - E) Variant A + ±10 year soft annotation (B.V. Raman convention) — classical thresholds + uncertainty note. ← my recommendation (classical + honest).
  - F) Other.

  Which?

● E5b Q3 locked: Option A — BPHS/Jaimini Sutras standard thresholds: Alpa 0-32 years, Madhya 33-70 years, Deergha 71-100 years. Matches JH + PL + K.N. Rao + Tamil   
  Vakya. Same for both user types.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---                                                                                                                                                                
  Q4 — Ayur-nirnaya integration strategy (4-method cross-validation)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Background. Classical practice is ayur-nirnaya — "longevity determination" via cross-reading multiple methods. No single method is considered definitive.            Astrologers present all 4 together (Pindayu/Amshayu/Nisargayu numbers + Jaimini category) and assess AGREEMENT among them before any interpretation. Strong          agreement = higher confidence; divergence = flag for uncertainty.                                                                                                  

  Classical position (BPHS Ch.72-74 + Jaimini Upadesha Sutras)

  BPHS Ch.72 v.1 explicitly states: "A wise astrologer should not declare the lifespan from a single method; cross-read Pindayu, Amshayu, and Nisargayu; the correct 
  ayus is typically the one most consistent with the chart's overall dignity."

  Integration options

  Option A — Present all 4 side-by-side with consensus-agreement flag

  Pindayu: 71 years    ┐
  Amshayu: 76 years    ├── Agreement: HIGH (all within ±10 yr spread)
  Nisargayu: 74 years  ┘
  Jaimini: Madhya (33-70 yr range) — PARTIAL AGREEMENT (Jaimini points lower than P/A/N triple)

  Ayur-nirnaya consensus: 70-76 years (Madhya-Deergha boundary)

  Each method emits independently; UI shows all with agreement badge. Classical rigor; respects "no single method" principle.

  Option B — Numerical-only consensus (drop Jaimini category)

  Show Pindayu/Amshayu/Nisargayu as numbers with average + spread. Jaimini category reported separately but not integrated into numeric consensus.

  Pros: Simpler math. Cons: Drops Jaimini from integration.

  Option C — Ensemble average + confidence band

  Single number = weighted average of Pindayu/Amshayu/Nisargayu. Jaimini category provides confidence band (Madhya = wide band, Deergha = narrower). One headline    
  number + band.

  Pros: Simple user-facing display. Cons: Loses per-method visibility; not classically pure.

  Option D — Each method independent; no cross-method synthesis

  Show 4 methods; no integration. Astrologer synthesizes manually.

  Pros: Classical purity (tradition doesn't formally synthesize). Cons: Forces manual analysis; no UI guidance.

  Option E — Classical ayur-nirnaya decision tree

  Apply classical precedence rules:
  - If all 4 agree (within ±10 yr range or same category) → high confidence
  - If 3 of 4 agree → medium confidence, flag the outlier
  - If methods split 2-2 → low confidence, astrologer must decide

  Structured classical rigor with decision logic.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Option A (side-by-side + agreement badge)
  - Parashara's Light: Option D (independent methods, no integration)
  - B.V. Raman's published treatment: Option E (decision tree approach)
  - K.N. Rao: Option A + Option E hybrid
  - Astrosage: Option C (ensemble average)

  Astrologer-only context

  Per E5b's ethical gating, this output is astrologer-workbench-only. Astrologers expect classical rigor; synthesized/averaged outputs without per-method visibility 
  are rejected.

  Q4 — Ayur-nirnaya integration:

  - A) All 4 side-by-side + consensus-agreement flag — classical rigor; matches JH + K.N. Rao astrologer expectation. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Numerical-only consensus — simpler but drops Jaimini integration.
  - C) Ensemble average + confidence band — simple display; loses per-method visibility.
  - D) Independent methods, no synthesis — classical purity; matches PL.
  - E) Classical decision tree — structured classical rigor with precedence logic.
  - F) A + E hybrid — side-by-side display + decision-tree agreement logic.
  - G) Other.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 33s
   ❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                                         
● E5b Q4 locked: Option A — All 4 longevity methods presented side-by-side on astrologer workbench with consensus-agreement flag (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW based on spread and
   category alignment). Classical rigor per BPHS Ch.72 v.1. Matches JH + K.N. Rao astrologer expectation.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q5 — Ethical gating strictness for B2C                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  Background. E5b's ethical guardrail per PRD §1 — longevity predictions gated behind professional astrologer workbench. B2C AI chat declines direct ayus questions.   Classical + modern rationale:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  - Classical: Phaladeepika Ch.7 v.2 explicitly states "do not state the year of death to the native" — industry-wide ethical norm.
  - Modern: Legal liability, user distress, potential self-harm, astrologer code-of-conduct violation.

  Question: how strict is the B2C refusal?

  Gating strictness options

  Option A — Hard refusal (PRD current)

  Scripted refusal response: "This is a question I'm not set up to answer directly — classical astrology has strong ethical traditions against stating specific      
  lifespan to the native, and I follow that tradition. If longevity context is important for a life-planning decision you're facing, I can connect you with a        
  professional astrologer from our network." No numeric/categorical data surfaced. AI chat routes to marketplace consultation.

  Pros: Clean ethical stance; matches Phaladeepika Ch.7 v.2 literal; legally defensible. Cons: User frustration; doesn't acknowledge legitimate curiosity.

  Option B — Soft refusal with classical + legal context

  Explain WHY before declining: "Classical astrology (Phaladeepika Ch.7 v.2) and modern ethics advise against direct lifespan predictions. Your chart does have      
  longevity indicators that a professional astrologer can interpret IN THE CONTEXT of a life question you're facing (e.g., 'should I start this long project now?'). 
  I can connect you with someone in our network."

  Pros: Educates user; preserves ethical stance. Cons: More complex response; some users may still push.

  Option C — Partial disclosure (range-only, no specific years)

  Share general category without specific numbers: "Your chart indicates generally long longevity (Deergha category per Jaimini)" OR "generally moderate longevity   
  (Madhya category)." No specific year or decade.

  Pros: Acknowledges user's question. Cons: Violates Phaladeepika principle; opens liability door; category boundary edge cases (70-year-old chart in "Madhya 33-70" 
  feels like death-threat).

  Option D — Age-adaptive

  - Users <30: hard refusal (Option A)
  - Users 30-60: soft refusal (Option B)
  - Users >60: partial disclosure (Option C) rationale: "the elderly already know they're in late life; withholding is paternalistic"

  Pros: Culturally adapted. Cons: Complex logic; still breaks Phaladeepika principle for elderly.

  Option E — Astrologer-workbench override

  B2C default: hard refusal. BUT if user's chart is annotated by their paid astrologer-consultant who has explicitly authorized disclosure (e.g., for
  terminal-illness-planning context), AI chat can surface the astrologer's framed message.

  Pros: Respects context; astrologer mediates. Cons: Complex UX; rare usage.

  Modern software + industry convention

  - Jagannatha Hora: No B2C layer (astrologer tool only); all data exposed to astrologer.
  - Parashara's Light: Same, astrologer-only.
  - Astrosage (B2C): Usually partial category disclosure (Option C) — controversial within industry.
  - Drik Panchang + mypanchang: No longevity surfaced (Option A hard refusal).
  - B.V. Raman's published ethical stance: Option A strict — never surface to native.

  Josi's positioning

  Josi is both B2C AND professional astrologer platform. The astrologer-route acknowledges classical tradition while respecting user autonomy. Option A aligns with  
  classical ethics + matches conservative industry practice.

  Q5 — B2C ethical gating strictness:

  - A) Hard refusal (PRD current) — scripted decline + marketplace route. Matches classical Phaladeepika + conservative industry + B.V. Raman stance. Same scripted  
  response for all B2C users. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Soft refusal with context — educates user; preserves ethics.
  - C) Partial disclosure (category only) — violates classical principle; opens liability.
  - D) Age-adaptive — complex logic; cultural nuance.
  - E) Astrologer-workbench override — astrologer mediates; rare UX.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

✻ Churned for 37s
❯ option A                                                                                                                                                                      
● E5b Q5 locked: Option A — Hard refusal scripted response for B2C. AI chat declines longevity questions + routes to marketplace astrologer consultation. Matches    
  classical Phaladeepika Ch.7 v.2 + Raman ethical stance + conservative industry + Drik/mypanchang convention.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Q6 — Uncertainty band display for astrologer workbench                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  Background. Even on astrologer workbench, classical tradition emphasizes ayus is NOT precise. BPHS Ch.72 v.1 explicitly warns: "A wise astrologer should not         declare the lifespan from a single method." Modern research (from E2a Graha Pinda agent report) recommends ±10 year uncertainty band per B.V. Raman's convention.                                                                                                                                                                       
  Classical position

  - BPHS Ch.72 v.1: Multi-method cross-validation required; no single number should be stated
  - B.V. Raman in Hindu Predictive Astrology: "Ayurdaya should never be presented as a precise age-of-death; always display with harana reductions visible and a     
  ±10-year uncertainty band"
  - K.N. Rao: Teaches "ayus is indicative, not definitive"

  Options

  Option A — Numerical values only, no uncertainty band

  Astrologer sees raw numbers: "Pindayu: 71 years." Astrologer interprets precision themselves. Classical purity; trusts astrologer expertise.

  Option B — ±10 year uncertainty band on each method (Raman convention)

  Display: "Pindayu: 71 years (± 10 yr uncertainty per B.V. Raman)." Each of 4 methods shows its own band. Ayur-nirnaya consensus view also shows combined band.     

  Pros: Classical modesty; matches Raman published convention; reinforces "not definitive" principle.
  Cons: Might feel condescending to experienced astrologers.

  Option C — ±5 year band (tighter)

  Narrower band. More precise-feeling but less classically humble.

  Option D — Configurable uncertainty band per astrologer profile

  Astrologer sets preferred uncertainty (none / ±5 / ±10 / ±15) per profile. Default ±10.

  Option E — Classical "cross-validate before finalizing" annotation

  No numeric band on individual methods. BUT consensus view has classical reminder: "Per BPHS Ch.72 v.1, cross-validate with multiple methods before finalizing. Ayus
   is indicative, not definitive." Reminder only, not numeric band.

  Option F — A + E hybrid

  Raw numbers shown + classical reminder text on consensus view. No numeric uncertainty band per method.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Option A (raw numbers + astrologer interprets)
  - Parashara's Light: Option A
  - B.V. Raman's published books: Option B (±10 yr band explicit)
  - K.N. Rao's teachings: Option E (cross-validate reminder)
  - Astrosage: Option A

  Practical impact

  Uncertainty band display affects astrologer reading stance:
  - Option A: astrologer free to interpret at their own rigor level
  - Option B: forces classical modesty; reduces over-confident interpretations
  - Option E: gentle classical reminder without numeric imposition

  Q6 — Uncertainty band display:

  - A) Raw numerical values only — astrologer interprets precision themselves. Matches JH + PL + Astrosage.
  - B) ±10 year uncertainty band per method (B.V. Raman convention) — classical modesty.
  - C) ±5 year band — tighter.
  - D) Configurable per astrologer profile — default ±10; astrologer can adjust.
  - E) Classical "cross-validate" reminder on consensus view only — matches K.N. Rao.
  - F) A + E hybrid — raw numbers + consensus reminder. ← my recommendation (no numeric imposition; honest classical framing where it matters).
  - G) Other.

  Which?

   Astrosage. Same on astrologer workbench for all astrologers.

  Q7 — Cross-source aggregation for E5b (final E5b question)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  Background. Same pattern as E1b Q7, E3 Q6, E5 Q7 — classical Parashari canonical default + 1 commentary variant per technique with astrologer-profile toggle.                                                                                                                                                                             For E5b, the four methods and their canonical sources:                                                                                                             

  ┌────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Method       │                      Canonical default                       │              Commentary variant              │
  ├────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Pindayu            │ BPHS Ch.72 (5-harana, locked Q1)                             │ Phaladeepika Ch.7 (3-harana variant)         │
  ├────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Amshayu            │ BPHS Ch.73 (same 5-harana, locked Q2)                        │ Phaladeepika Ch.7 (2-harana variant)         │
  ├────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Nisargayu          │ BPHS Ch.74 (standard)                                        │ Varaha Mihira Brihat Jataka commentary       │
  ├────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Jaimini Ayur-dasha │ Jaimini Upadesha Sutras Book 2 + BPHS (thresholds locked Q3) │ Rangacharya Kalpalatha commentary extensions │
  └────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Options

  Option A — Canonical defaults only, no astrologer toggles

  Single-source per method. No variant selectability. Simplest.

  Option B — Canonical default + 1 commentary variant per method, astrologer toggle

  Matches E1b Q7 / E3 Q6 / E5 Q7 convention. Each method ships canonical + alternate with astrologer-profile toggle via F2 astrologer_source_preference.

  Option C — Full F8 aggregation (all variants as sibling rules with confidence indicator)

  Higher complexity; matches master design §1.3 Strategy D.

  Option D — Per-method curation (pick best for each)

  Pindayu: BPHS only (Phaladeepika 3-harana too aggressive departure)
  Amshayu: BPHS only
  Nisargayu: BPHS + Varaha Mihira toggle
  Jaimini: JUS + Rangacharya toggle

  Per-method decision rather than uniform.

  Consistency with prior PRDs

  E1b Q7 = Option B (BPHS + Rangacharya variant per Jaimini dasai)
  E3 Q6 = Option B (JUS + Rangacharya variant per Jaimini technique)
  E5 Q7 = Option B (Tajaka Neelakanthi + Varshatantra variant per Tajaka technique)

  Extending Option B to E5b gives uniform astrologer experience across Jaimini (E1b/E3) + Tajaka (E5) + Longevity (E5b) tradition layers.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: Option B (canonical + Phaladeepika/Rangacharya toggles)
  - Parashara's Light: Option A (BPHS only, no variants)
  - B.V. Raman: Option A primary (BPHS canonical)
  - K.N. Rao: Option B (canonical + commentary variants per method)
  - Astrosage: Option A

  Q7 — Cross-source aggregation for E5b:

  - A) Canonical defaults only, no toggles — simplest; matches PL + Astrosage + Raman.
  - B) Canonical default + 1 commentary variant per method, astrologer toggle — matches E1b Q7 / E3 Q6 / E5 Q7 convention + JH + K.N. Rao. Uniform consistency across
   Jaimini/Tajaka/Longevity tradition layers. ← my recommendation.
  - C) Full F8 aggregation — maximum tooling; higher complexity.
  - D) Per-method curation — mixed approach per method.
  - E) Other.
