  Q1 — Dasa-pack scope (which dasas ship MVP)

  Background. Per GAP_CLOSURE, E1c covers Jaimini extensions + Tara Dasa + minor kalpas. Which ship MVP?

  Per GAP_CLOSURE proposed scope

  Tier 1 — Critical (must-ship):
  - Tara Dasa (Tamil Parashari) — CRITICAL for Tamil users
  - Shoola Dasa (Jaimini) — important maraka-timing tool

  Tier 2 — Important (should-ship):
  - Sthira Dasa (Jaimini) — fixed-sign dasa
  - Drig Dasa (Jaimini) — 9th-house dharma focus

  Tier 3 — Nice-to-have:
  - Kendra Dasa, Trikona Dasa, Brahma Dasa, Niryana Shoola Dasa (Jaimini variants)

  Tier 4 — Niche kalpas (research-tier):
  - Dwadashottari (112yr), Panchottari (105yr), Shataabdika (100yr), Chathurseethi-sama (84yr), Dwisaptati-sama (72yr)

  Scope options

  Option A — All tiers (12+ dasas)

  Comprehensive. Heavy MVP scope.

  Option B — Tier 1 + 2 (4 dasas: Tara/Shoola/Sthira/Drig)

  Critical + important. Tighter MVP. Tier 3+4 deferred.

  Option C — Tier 1 only (Tara + Shoola)

  Minimum critical. Defer everything else to E1d.

  Option D — Tiered feature flags (Tier 1 always-on; Tier 2 default-on; Tier 3 astrologer-toggle; Tier 4 research-flag)

  Matches uniform tiered convention (E8b Q1, E22 Q1, etc.).

  Option E — Custom — tell me which specific dasas

  Q1 — Dasa-pack scope:

  - A) All 12+ dasas — comprehensive.
  - B) Tier 1+2 (4 dasas: Tara/Shoola/Sthira/Drig) — critical + important.
  - C) Tier 1 only (Tara + Shoola) — minimum critical.
  - D) Tiered feature flags (T1 always · T2 default · T3 astrologer-toggle · T4 research-flag) — matches uniform tiered convention. ← my recommendation.
  - E) Custom — tell me.

  Which?

  Q2 — Tara Dasa Tamil classical source

  Background. Tara Dasa is Tamil Parashari standard — based on 9-Tara cycle (from E24). Each Tara period mapped to a dasa-period with specific year allocations.     

  Classical Tamil sources

  Source A — Tamil Vakya Panchangam canonical

  Universal Tamil tradition. Year allocations per Tara per Tamil Vakya. Standard for Tamil matchmaking + daily practice.

  Source B — Agathiyar Tamil Siddhar lineage

  Specific Tamil Siddhar text-tradition. Slight allocation variants.

  Source C — Modern Tamil published synthesis

  Modern Tamil jyotish books. Synthesis of Vakya + Siddhar.

  Source D — Pan-Tamil (with North Indian Parashari overlay)

  Combines Tamil Vakya base + influences from North Indian Parashari.

  Classical position

  Tamil Vakya Panchangam is the dominant Tara Dasa source. All Tamil matchmaking + daily-practice software use this.

  Modern software

  - Tamil-specific matchmaking apps: Tamil Vakya
  - Drik Panchang Tamil: Tamil Vakya
  - Astrosage Tamil: Tamil Vakya

  Q2 — Tara Dasa Tamil source:

  - A) Tamil Vakya Panchangam canonical — universal Tamil tradition. Same for both user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Agathiyar Tamil Siddhar lineage — niche.
  - C) Modern Tamil published synthesis — modern.
  - D) Pan-Tamil + North Indian overlay — combined.
  - E) Vakya default + Siddhar/Modern variant toggle — uniform variant convention.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

❯ A

● E1c Q2 locked: Option A — Tamil Vakya Panchangam canonical for Tara Dasa. Universal Tamil tradition; matches all Tamil matchmaking + daily-practice software. Same 
  for both user types.

  Q3 — Sthira/Shoola/Drig Dasa Jaimini source

  Background. These three Jaimini sign-based dasas trace to:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Source               │                                   Coverage                                    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Jaimini Upadesha Sutras           │ Primary Jaimini canon; defines all 4 Jaimini dasas (Chara/Sthira/Shoola/Drig) │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ BPHS Ch.50                        │ Parashari-lineage Jaimini treatment                                           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rangacharya Kalpalatha commentary │ Modern interpretive extensions                                                │
  ├───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Sanjay Rath SJVC                  │ Contemporary synthesis                                                        │
  └───────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Options

  Option A — BPHS Ch.50 canonical (consistent with E1b Q7)

  Matches E1b Q7 lock for Chara/Narayana/Kalachakra (BPHS canonical default). Cross-PRD consistency.

  Option B — Jaimini Upadesha Sutras canonical

  Sthira/Shoola/Drig are PRIMARILY JUS-defined. JUS-primary is more classically accurate for these specific dasas.

  Option C — BPHS + JUS synthesis (per-dasa best-source curation)

  Picks JUS or BPHS per dasa based on which source treats it most thoroughly.

  Option D — JUS canonical default + Rangacharya variant astrologer toggle

  Per uniform variant convention. JUS-primary (more accurate for Jaimini dasas) + commentary toggle.

  Classical consensus

  JUS is THE Jaimini canon. BPHS Ch.50 references JUS for these dasas. JUS-primary is the more classically defensible choice.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: JUS-primary + Rangacharya toggle
  - Sanjay Rath SJVC: JUS-primary
  - Parashara's Light: BPHS Ch.50 reading

  Q3 — Sthira/Shoola/Drig source:

  - A) BPHS Ch.50 canonical — consistent with E1b Q7 lock.
  - B) JUS canonical — primary for Jaimini dasas.
  - C) BPHS + JUS per-dasa curation — picks best per dasa.
  - D) JUS canonical default + Rangacharya variant astrologer toggle — matches JH + uniform variant convention. ← my recommendation (more accurate for Jaimini dasas 
  + consistent with E1b Q7 toggle pattern).
  - E) Other.

  Which?

    - Transit Ashtakavargam: SAV + Kaksha Vibhaga at transit moment (per E2a)

    - Chandra Bala: Moon's transit rasi position from natal Moon
    - Lattha: retrograde-back-into-natchathiram rules
    - Graha-Karaka: dasa-lord transit over natal-Karaka triggers
    - Kal Sarpa transit activations

  Background. Vedha = natchathiram-pair obstruction rules in transit. When a planet transits natchathiram X, it can be obstructed by another planet transiting paired   natchathiram Y. Classical sources differ on specific Vedha pairs.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Classical sources

  Source A — BPHS Ch.40 canonical (transit chapter)

  Standard Parashari treatment of gochara phala including Vedha. Most-cited.

  Source B — Phaladeepika Ch.26 (Mantreswara extensions)

  Slight variations in some natchathiram-pair definitions.

  Source C — Muhurta Chintamani Ch.4 synthesis

  Integrates BPHS + Phaladeepika.

  Source D — Tamil Vakya panchangam canonical

  Tamil daily-practice application — usually follows Phaladeepika.

  Modern software

  - Jagannatha Hora: BPHS Ch.40
  - Parashara's Light: BPHS Ch.40
  - Astrosage: BPHS Ch.40
  - Tamil Vakya panchangams: BPHS Ch.40 + Phaladeepika synthesis

  Q2 — Vedha source:

  - A) BPHS Ch.40 canonical — matches all modern software. Same for both user types. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Phaladeepika Ch.26 variant — Mantreswara extensions.
  - C) Muhurta Chintamani synthesis — combined.
  - D) Tamil Vakya canonical — Tamil practice.
  - E) BPHS default + variant astrologer toggle — uniform convention.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

A

● E17 Chart Rectification — Pass 1 kick-off (final PRD!)

  Step A — User story (context only)

  User onboarding detects "approximate birth time" (e.g., "around 5-6 AM"). Josi prompts: "Tell me 4-6 major life events with dates — marriage, career change, child 
  birth, parental death, etc. — and I'll estimate your birth time." Engine searches the candidate window, scores each candidate using 4 independent classical+modern 
  techniques, returns top 5 candidate times with confidence + per-method reasoning. Differentiating consumer feature vs competitors who require user to input exact  
  time.

  Step B — Calculation pipeline (E17 = compute-heavy)

  1. User submits: approximate birth date + candidate window (default ±4h) + K life events (date + category)
  2. E17 candidate search: 1-min resolution over window (default ±4h = 480 candidates; coarse-to-fine optimization)
  3. E17 4-method scoring per candidate:
    - Method 1 — Vimshottari Dasa-AD timing (uses E1a): for each event date, identify active MD/AD lords; score significations vs event category
    - Method 2 — Slow-transit natal-house matching (uses E6a/E6b): Jupiter/Saturn/Rahu/Ketu transits to natal houses on event date; score house-matter alignment     
    - Method 3 — Secondary progressions (Western technique): progressed planets at event date; score angular contacts
    - Method 4 — Varga consistency: Lagna lord in D9 + D10 vs reported life pattern; score
  4. Combine scores with configurable weights → composite score
  5. Return top 5 candidates with per-method breakdown
  6. 3 operating modes per PRD: Pure AI / Expert-assisted (astrologer) / Hybrid (AI shortlist + astrologer final pick)

  ---
  Q1 — 4-method scoring weights

  Background. 4 methods produce independent scores per candidate. How are they combined?

  Weighting options

  Option A — Equal weights (25% each)

  Vimshottari + Transit + Progressions + Varga each contribute 25% to composite. Simplest.

  Option B — Parashari-weighted (Vimshottari/Transit/Varga emphasized; Progressions reduced)

  - Vimshottari Dasa: 35%
  - Slow Transit: 30%
  - Varga Consistency: 25%
  - Secondary Progressions: 10% (Western technique — reduced for Parashari-primary chart)

  Aligns with Parashari classical emphasis.

  Option C — User-configurable weights per profile

  Astrologer adjusts weights per their methodology preference.

  Option D — AI-learned weights

  Train weights from historical chart-event pairs. Modern data-driven.

  Option E — Tradition-adaptive

  For Parashari-primary chart: Option B (Vimshottari emphasized). For Western chart: equal-weighted with Progressions higher.

  Modern software

  - Solar Fire Rectification: Configurable weights default Western-equal
  - Sanjay Rath SJVC Rectification: Vimshottari + Transit + Varga weighted heavier
  - Astrosage Rectification: Vimshottari emphasized

  Q1 — 4-method scoring weights:

  - A) Equal weights (25% each) — simplest.
  - B) Parashari-weighted (Vimshottari 35% + Transit 30% + Varga 25% + Progressions 10%) — matches Parashari classical emphasis. ← my recommendation (Josi is        
  Parashari-primary).
  - C) User-configurable per profile — astrologer flexibility.
  - D) AI-learned weights — data-driven.
  - E) Tradition-adaptive — Parashari-weighted for Vedic; equal for Western.
  - F) Other.

  Which?

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  E17 Q2 — Search window default (how wide to scan around reported birth time)
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Rectification needs a time window to sweep. Too narrow misses real birth time; too wide multiplies compute cost + false candidates.

  Classical lineages vary:
  - KP rectification (Krishnamurti): tight ±15–30 min, relies on cuspal sub-lord precision.
  - Parashari/traditional Tamil: ±2–4 hours is common when mother's recollection is "morning" or "around sunset."
  - Jaimini Chara Dasa rectification: often ±4–8 hours when only date is known.
  - Unknown-time charts (adopted children, old records): ±12h or full-day scan.

  Options:

  - A) ±2 hours default — assumes reported time is fairly accurate (hospital record, clock-noted). Fast compute. Fails for "morning/evening" recollections.
  - B) ±4 hours default — matches traditional Tamil astrologer practice for family-recollected births. Balanced compute. ← my recommendation (covers 80% of Indian
  diaspora cases where birth time is "pulgalkaalam" / "maalai" rather than clock-exact).
  - C) ±8 hours default — wide sweep for poorly-remembered times. Heavy compute, more false positives, but catches edge cases.
  - D) Tiered: user picks "known/approximate/unknown" → system maps to ±30min / ±4h / ±12h. Best UX but adds a UI step. B2C default = "approximate" (±4h); astrologer
   can override per case.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 56s
 B

● Q2 locked → B (±4 hours default).

  ---
  E17 Q3 — Operating modes scope (which rectification modes to ship)

  The PRD lists 3 modes. Need to lock which are in-scope for v1 and which are astrologer-only vs B2C-exposed.

  Three modes:
  1. Event-based rectification — user enters 3–10 life events (marriage, job change, parent death, childbirth) with dates; system reverse-fits birth time so
  Vimshottari dasha + transits match those events. Most accurate. Needs user data entry.
  2. Physical/personality-based — user answers questionnaire (body type, temperament, health tendencies); system matches to Lagna/Moon sign indicators. Weaker       
  signal, no dates needed.
  3. Prashna-assisted — astrologer casts a Prashna chart at time of consultation; uses Prashna lagna + querent's cue to cross-check birth time. Astrologer-only      
  workflow.

  Options:

  - A) All 3 modes, all users — maximum feature surface. Risk: B2C users abandon Mode 1 (too many events to enter) and Mode 3 requires Prashna skill.
  - B) Mode 1 + Mode 2 for B2C; all 3 for astrologers. Prashna mode gated to verified astrologers only. ← my recommendation — matches the two-user-type framework;   
  Prashna is genuinely an astrologer skill.
  - C) Mode 1 only (events) — ship the highest-accuracy mode first; defer 2 & 3 to v2.
  - D) Mode 1 + Mode 3 only — skip personality (weak signal). B2C uses events; astrologers add Prashna.

  Which?

❯ B                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q3 locked → B (Modes 1+2 for B2C; all 3 for astrologers, Prashna gated to verified astrologers).                                                                   
  
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  E17 Q4 — Confidence threshold for result acceptance
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Rectification produces a ranked list of candidate birth times with scores. Need to decide when to present a result vs say "inconclusive, need more events."

  Options:

  - A) Top candidate wins (no threshold) — always return the highest-scoring time even if score is weak. Risk: users trust a bad result.
  - B) Absolute threshold ≥70% match score — only return if top candidate passes. Below threshold → "insufficient data, please add more events." ← my recommendation 
  for B2C (protects users from false certainty).
  - C) Relative threshold (top candidate ≥15% above #2) — returns a result only when it's clearly separated from alternatives. Stricter; good for astrologer mode.   
  - D) Tiered: B2C = absolute ≥70%; Astrologer = show top 3 candidates with scores + allow manual pick. Astrologer judges ambiguity themselves.

  Which?

❯ B                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q4 locked → B (absolute threshold ≥70% match score; below → "insufficient data, add more events").                                                                 
  
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  E17 Q5 — Cross-source aggregation (which classical texts inform rectification rules)                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Rectification combines signals from multiple classical methods. Need to lock the source stack + default vs toggle.

  Options:

  - A) BPHS-only — Vimshottari + Varga + Nisheka (conception-based) per Parashara Ch. 4. Pure Parashari, but ignores Jaimini Chara-dasa signals which many Tamil     
  astrologers use.
  - B) BPHS canonical default + Jaimini/KP as astrologer toggles — B2C gets pure Parashari (Vimshottari + Varga + transits). Astrologer can enable Jaimini Chara or  
  KP cuspal sub-lord as additional scoring dimensions. ← my recommendation (matches cross-cutting pattern 1.x + two-user framework).
  - C) All methods weighted together — BPHS + Jaimini + KP + Tajaka all fire always. Risk: methods disagree, scores muddy.
  - D) User picks tradition upfront — user selects "Parashari" or "KP" at start of rectification; system uses only that tradition's method.

  Which?

❯ B                                                                                                                                                                      
● Q5 locked → B (BPHS canonical default; Jaimini/KP as astrologer toggles).

      138  ### 3.1 Classical rectification techniques

❯ what is Badhaka Shadbalam-weighted activation

  Searched for 1 pattern, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

● Here's the explanation.

  ---
  What "Badhaka Shadbalam-weighted activation" means

  The classical concept — Badhaka

  Badhaka (பாதகன் / obstructor) is a house-lord classically defined per BPHS Ch.44 as the planet that specifically obstructs, blocks, or causes hindrances for a giveen
  chart. It's separate from Marakas (death-dealers = 2nd/7th lords). Badhaka is more about chronic frustration, obstacles, delays — not mortality.

  Which house is the Badhaka depends on your Lagna type (locked in §6.5 E27 Q2, no controversy):

  ┌────────────────────┬───────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Lagna type     │ Badhaka house │           Example Lagnas           │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Movable (Chara)    │ 11th          │ Mesha, Karka, Thula, Makara        │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fixed (Sthira)     │ 9th           │ Rishabha, Simha, Vrischika, Kumbha │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Dual (Dwisvabhava) │ 7th           │ Mithuna, Kanni, Dhanus, Meena      │
  └────────────────────┴───────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘

  The lord of that house = Badhakesha (the Badhaka planet).

  The question: when does the Badhaka "activate"?

  Having a Badhaka planet doesn't mean obstacles are firing all the time. The question is: under what conditions does the Badhaka actually cause trouble in someone's
   life?

  Two philosophical camps on this:

  Camp 1 — Classical strict (BPHS literal):
  Badhaka "activates" via specific structural conditions from BPHS Ch.44 v.20-25:
  - Badhaka lord in its own Badhaka house (self-placement)
  - Badhaka lord in a dusthana (6/8/12)
  - Badhaka lord afflicted by malefics
  - Badhaka lord's dasha/antardasha running
  - Badhaka lord conjunct with Maraka

  This is binary — either the structural trigger is present (Badhaka fires) or not (dormant). No gradient.

  Camp 2 — Rath-school modern hybrid:
  Pandit Sanjay Rath and modern Parashari commentators (Ernst Wilhelm, Freedom Cole) add a strength modulation layer on top of the binary triggers: even when a      
  trigger condition is met, the severity of obstruction scales with the Badhaka planet's Shadbalam (six-fold strength).

  - Weak Badhaka (Shadbalam well below threshold) → mild, nagging obstacles even when triggered
  - Average Badhaka (Shadbalam near threshold) → expected classical effects
  - Strong Badhaka (Shadbalam well above threshold) → severe, life-phase-defining obstruction

  This is a gradient — the output is a severity score (0-1) not a boolean.

  What Josi locked

  §6.5 E27 Q3: "Classical strict (BPHS Ch.44 v.20-25) + Shadbalam-strength weighting (hybrid)."

  Translation: Josi uses Camp 2. Triggers are classical (BPHS); severity is weighted by the Badhaka planet's Shadbalam total.

  What the quality agent flagged

  Two concerns:

  1. Attribution. The lock calls it "Classical strict" but the Shadbalam-weighting layer is not actually classical — BPHS doesn't modulate Badhaka by Shadbalam. It's
   a modern commentary extension from the Rath school (20th-21st century). Calling it "classical strict" is mildly misleading — engineering might implement it       
  thinking BPHS backs the weighting; it doesn't.
  2. No harm to the decision itself. The weighting is a defensible modern refinement — many Parashari astrologers use it, it's classically plausible (Shadbalam      
  measures general planetary potency; it's reasonable to think a strong planet does what it does more strongly). The agent isn't asking you to remove it.

  Agent's recommendation: label it as "BPHS classical triggers + Rath-school Shadbalam severity extension" rather than "Classical strict + weighting" so engineering,
   astrologers, and future reviewers understand that the weighting layer is modern commentary, not BPHS literal.

  Your options

  - A) Keep as-is — lock stands, no edit. You're comfortable with the "Classical strict + weighting" phrasing.
  - B) Relabel per agent suggestion — clarify that weighting is Rath-school/modern, not BPHS. No functional change, just accuracy in attribution.
  - C) Drop the weighting — go pure binary classical-strict per BPHS literal, no severity gradient.
  - D) Make weighting astrologer-toggleable — B2C gets pure binary classical; astrologer can enable Shadbalam weighting for severity gradient.

  Which?

❯ what  are maraka planets

● Maraka planets (மாரகம் / "death-dealers")

  Maraka = classical Parashari term for planets that can act as death-inflictors during their dasha/antardasha periods. Not "causers of death" mystically — rather,  
  they're the structural markers for mortality-timing windows in a chart. From mru (Sanskrit root for "to die").

  BPHS Ch.44 canonical definition (locked in Josi §6.5 E27 Q1 as "Strict 2nd + 7th"):

  Primary Marakas — by house lordship

  ┌────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │  Maraka    │                                                                   Definition                                                                    │   
  │    type    │                                                                                                                                                 │   
  ├────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ 2nd-lord   │ Lord of the 2nd house from Lagna — "maraka-sthana" (house of family/speech/wealth; classical death-house by counting 7 from 8, i.e., 8 =        │   
  │            │ longevity, 8+1 mod = 2)                                                                                                                         │   
  ├────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ 7th-lord   │ Lord of the 7th house from Lagna — "maraka-sthana" (house of partnership; death-house by direct count)                                          │   
  └────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Why 2nd and 7th? Classical logic: the 8th house signifies longevity itself. The 12th from 8th = 7th (loss of longevity). The 2nd is the 7th from 8th (mutual       
  aspect/opposition to longevity). So 2nd and 7th are the two "maraka-sthanas" structurally tied to the 8th.

  Secondary Marakas — by occupation or association

  1. Planets placed in 2nd or 7th house (occupation)
  2. Planets conjunct with the 2nd-lord or 7th-lord (association)
  3. Planets aspected by 2nd-lord or 7th-lord (some lineages)

  Key classical nuances

  - Natural benefics become malefic when they turn Maraka — this is the strongest classical rule. Guru as 2nd-lord for a Makara Lagna becomes a Maraka despite being 
  a universal benefic. The role overrides the intrinsic nature.
  - Functional vs natural: Maraka status is purely functional (by lordship in your specific chart), not natural. Sevvai is a natural malefic but only becomes your   
  Maraka if it happens to rule your 2nd or 7th.
  - Activation timing: Maraka effects fire during the Maraka planet's Mahadasha or Antardasha, intensified if:
    - Maraka transits over sensitive points (Lagna, Moon, 8th house)
    - Maraka dasa + transit over Maraka house coincide
    - Ashtakavargam strength at 8th house drops during Maraka period

  Maraka vs Badhaka — the distinction

  People confuse these two. Classical separation:

  ┌────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                            │              Maraka              │                       Badhaka                       │
  ├────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ What it does               │ Death / mortality timing         │ Obstruction / hindrance / chronic blockage          │
  ├────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Scope                      │ End-of-life window               │ Life-phase-recurring obstacles                      │
  ├────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Houses                     │ 2nd + 7th (universal)            │ 11th (Chara) / 9th (Sthira) / 7th (Dwisvabhava)     │
  ├────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Activation                 │ Dasha-period + transit           │ Dasha + structural triggers                         │
  ├────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Severity modulation (Josi) │ Binary BPHS strict (§6.5 E27 Q1) │ Classical + Shadbalam-weighted hybrid (§6.5 E27 Q3) │
  └────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  How Josi will use Maraka data

  Per §6.5 E27 locks:
  - Engine identifies primary Marakas (2nd + 7th lords) + secondary Marakas (occupants + conjuncts) for every chart
  - Feeds into E5b Ayus longevity computation as "critical Maraka dasha periods"
  - Ethical gating inherited from E5b Q5: B2C gets hard refusal for direct longevity-timing queries — no showing "you will die in X year" even if the engine computes
   it. Astrologers see full Maraka/dasa timing data in the workbench.
  - Same Maraka logic for both user types at the computation layer; gating happens only at the display layer.

  Classical sources cited in lock: BPHS Ch.44 primary; Phaladeepika Ch.14 reinforces the 2nd-lord/7th-lord rule; Jataka Parijata Ch.14 adds the
  "benefic-turns-malefic-when-Maraka" clarification.

❯ maraka planet by KP

  Searched for 1 pattern (ctrl+o to expand)

● Maraka planets in KP (Krishnamurti Paddhati)

  KP's approach to Maraka is structurally different from classical Parashari — it rejects the "2nd + 7th lord = Maraka" house-lord rule as too coarse and replaces it
   with sub-lord theory applied to death-signifying houses.

  KP's core difference

  KP (established by K.S. Krishnamurti, 1963–1972 publications) argues that classical house-lordship is insufficient because:
  - A single planet can be the lord of two houses
  - Houses have fractional affinity (a planet placed at 28° of Scorpio is barely in Scorpio and strongly "tending toward" Sagittarius)
  - Actual event-timing requires the sub-lord (5th level of Vimshottari nesting) — not just the sign-lord

  So KP identifies Marakas via sub-lord analysis of death-signifying houses, not via house-lordship.

  KP's Maraka houses (the "death" house set)

  KP doesn't use just 2nd + 7th. Per KP Reader Vol. 4 Ch. 18 (Krishnamurti's Death & Its Timing), the classical KP set is:

  ┌───────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ House │                                                 KP role                                                 │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 2nd   │ Maraka (classical retained) — family, wealth, end-of-embodiment                                         │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 7th   │ Maraka (classical retained) — partnership, negation of Lagna                                            │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 12th  │ Moksha/loss — departure from body, dissolution                                                          │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 11th  │ Sometimes included — gains from 12th (inverted death-indicator)                                         │
  ├───────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 8th   │ Explicitly NOT Maraka in KP — 8th is longevity, not death. Death comes from houses that negate the 8th. │
  └───────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  This is KP's signature move: treating 8th as longevity-preserving and 12th as the actual "exit" house. Classical Parashari lumps 8th into mortality analysis; KP   
  separates them.

  The KP Maraka identification procedure

  Per KPR Vol. 4 Ch. 18:

  1. Identify significators of 2, 7, and 12 using the 5-level significator hierarchy (from E9 Q2 lock — KPR-II Ch.4 v.23 ordering: level 2 > 4 > 1 > 3 > 5).
    - Each house gets a set of 5-15 planets that "signify" it across the 5 levels.
  2. Filter for Maraka candidates — a planet is a Maraka candidate if it significates house 2, 7, or 12 at ANY level.
  3. Find the strongest signficator via:
    - Whether the planet is the sub-lord of the 2nd, 7th, or 12th cusp (strongest claim)
    - Whether it's in a nakshatra of a significator of these houses
    - Whether it's in the sub of a significator (deeper nesting)
  4. Cross-check with Ruling Planets (RP) at the moment of question/event — the Maraka must also appear in the RP set during its activation period.

  How the "Maraka dasha" fires in KP

  In Vimshottari dasha, a KP Maraka doesn't just "activate" because it's a Maraka — it activates when:
  - The planet's MD/AD/PD is running
  - The sub-lord of the running sub-period significates house 2, 7, or 12
  - Transit of Sun over sensitive KP points (natal MD lord, Badhaka cusp) coincides
  - Ruling Planets of the date match the Maraka significator set

  This is a 4-way convergence, much stricter than Parashari's "Maraka dasha is running" rule. KP practitioners claim this is why KP death-timing predictions are more
   accurate — classical Parashari over-predicts.

  KP-specific Maraka nuances

  a) Sub-lord override. A planet that is classically NOT a Maraka (not lord of 2/7/12) can still act as a Maraka in KP if it happens to be the sub-lord of the 2nd,  
  7th, or 12th cusp. The sub-lord layer overrides sign-lordship for event-significance.

  b) Contrary-house rejection. If a planet significates house 2/7/12 at a low level (4 or 5) but strongly significates contrary houses (1st = self, 5th = vitality,  
  9th = dharma/longevity, 10th = status/continuity) at a higher level (2 or 1), it's rejected as a Maraka — KP's "contrary significator" exclusion rule.

  c) Rahu/Ketu special rule. Nodes act as "agents" of the planets they conjunct or whose sign they occupy. In KP, Rahu in a Maraka-significator's sign becomes a     
  stronger Maraka than that planet itself, because nodes carry "acceleration" in KP theory.

  Key classical KP sources

  - K.S. Krishnamurti, KP Reader Vol. 4 (1971) — Death & Its Timing, Chapters 18–22
  - KP Reader Vol. 2 Ch. 4 — significator hierarchy (basis for Maraka identification; locked in E9 Q2)
  - KP Reader Vol. 6 — longevity and Maraka case studies
  - Modern: K. Subramaniam commentaries; Kanak Bosmia, Tin Win for practical KP death-timing

  Josi's KP Maraka scope

  Currently not explicitly locked in E9 — E9 Pass 1 locked significators, cuspal sub-lords, 5-element Ruling Planets, but not Maraka rules specifically. The Maraka  
  engine in §6.5 E27 was locked as Parashari-only (BPHS Ch.44 canonical).

  This is a gap — if you want Josi to support KP Maraka analysis, the options are:

  - A) Extend E27 with a KP branch — add a KP-variant Maraka identifier that uses significators of 2/7/12 + sub-lord override + contrary-significator rejection.     
  Astrologer picks tradition (Parashari/KP) per chart.
  - B) New E27-KP PRD — separate KP Maraka engine as its own PRD under P2 or P3.
  - C) Keep E27 Parashari-only for v1 — defer KP Maraka to P3 or later. Josi's current KP scope (E9) is cuspal/significator analysis for general prediction; Maraka  
  is a specialized use case.
  - D) Bundle KP Maraka into E9 itself — add Maraka identification as a sub-section of E9 since the building blocks (significators, cuspal sub-lords, RPs) already   
  exist there.

  My recommendation: D — KP Maraka is mechanically just a specific application of existing E9 primitives (5-level significators + cuspal sub-lord analysis + RP      
  matching). No new engine needed; just a new endpoint on E9: GET /api/v1/charts/{chart_id}/kp/maraka-analysis that composes existing E9 outputs with the KP
  death-house filter.

  Want to lock this as a new E9 sub-decision? Or keep the scope as-is and treat KP Maraka as a future P3 addition?

❯ what are the sanskrit names of nakshra in english

● 27 Nakshatras — Sanskrit names in English

  Plain English transliteration (no IAST diacritics):

  ┌─────┬────────────────────┬───────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │ Sanskrit (English) │ Ruling Planet │              Zodiac span              │
  ├─────┼────────────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 1   │ Ashwini            │ Ketu          │ 00°00′ – 13°20′ Aries                 │
  ├─────┼────────────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 2   │ Bharani            │ Venus         │ 13°20′ – 26°40′ Aries                 │
  ├─────┼────────────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3   │ Krittika           │ Sun           │ 26°40′ Aries – 10°00′ Taurus          │
  ├─────┼────────────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤

  | 28 | Abhijit | — (sometimes Sun) | 06°40′ – 10°53′20″ Capricorn (carved out of Uttara Ashadha / Shravana boundary) |

  default, 28 for SBC only.

  Notes on Tamil supersession

  Six nakshatras are classically referred to in Tamil by their Tamil name rather than a Sanskrit transliteration, because the Tamil term is ingrained in lineage     
  usage (Vakya panchangam, agathiyar texts, Tamil Jyotisha literature):

  - Thiruvathirai (not Ardra) — #6
  - Ayilyam (not Ashlesha) — #9
  - Anusham (not Anuradha) — #17
  - Kettai (not Jyeshtha) — #18
  - Thiruvonam (not Shravana) — #22
  - Avittam (not Dhanishta) — #23

  For these, the Tamil name is the canonical user-facing form in Josi's B2C; the Sanskrit is kept internally as sa_iast_english for API/cross-reference.

  Published-variant ambiguities flagged in the audit

  - Mirugasirsham (#5) — also seen as Mrigasheersham / Mirugasirisham
  - Punarpoosam (#7) — also seen as the pure Sanskrit Punarvasu in some Tamil texts
  - Hastham (#13) — also seen as Astham / Atham
  - Visakam (#16) — also Vishakam
  - Sathayam (#24) — also Sadayam

  Josi locks the first spelling as primary display; alternates are stored as ta_phonetic_variants for search/alias matching but not rendered in UI.

✻ Cooked for 36s

❯ will josi support multi language output

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)       

● Yes — Josi supports multi-language display per §1.5 lock                                                                                                           
                     
  The locked policy                                                                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                       Display format = Sanskrit-IAST (English transliteration) + user's regional language, side-by-side.                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                     
  - English side: always transliteration, never translation. E.g., users see "Mṛgaśīrā" not "Orion"; "Sūrya" not "Sun"; "Guru" not "Jupiter". Classical terms remain
  classical; we don't dumb them down.
  - Regional side: user picks one from their profile.
  - Same policy for B2C and astrologers — only the regional-side language differs by user.

  8 regional languages in scope

  ┌─────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          Language           │    Script field    │          Example (Mrigashira nakshatra)          │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tamil                       │ ta                 │ மிருகசீரிடம்                                          │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Hindi / Sanskrit-Devanagari │ sa_devanagari + hi │ मृगशीर्ष                                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Telugu                      │ te                 │ మృగశిర                                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Kannada                     │ kn                 │ ಮೃಗಶಿರ                                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Malayalam                   │ ml                 │ മൃഗശിരസ്                                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Bengali                     │ bn                 │ মৃগশিরা                                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Gujarati                    │ gu                 │ મૃગશીર્ષ                                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Marathi                     │ mr                 │ मृगशीर्ष (often shares Devanagari forms with Hindi) │
  └─────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The schema (what engineering will build)

  Each canonical entity gets a classical_names YAML block with fields:
  classical_names:
    sa_iast: "Mṛgaśīrā"
    sa_devanagari: "मृगशीर्ष"
    ta: "மிருகசீரிடம்"
    ta_phonetic: "Mirugasirsham"
    hi: "मृगशीर्ष"
    te: "మృగశిర"
    kn: "ಮೃಗಶಿರ"
    ml: "മൃഗശിരസ്"
    bn: "মৃগশিরা"
    gu: "મૃગશીર્ષ"
    mr: "मृगशीर्ष"
    en: "DEPRECATED — do not use for display"  # English gloss was for early dev only

  Scope of entities to translate

  Per §1.5 action items, the full list of canonical entities that need multi-language fill:
  - 27 nakshatras + Abhijit
  - 12 rasis
  - 9 grahas
  - 16 vargas (divisional charts)
  - 60 Samvatsaras
  - 30 tithis
  - 11 karanas
  - 27 yogas (panchanga)
  - 5 panchaka subtypes
  - 30 muhurtas + auspicious/inauspicious labels (Kuligai, Emakandam, Rahu Kalam, etc.)
  - Yoga names (Raja Yoga, Gaja Kesari, Neecha Bhanga, etc.)
  - 16 Tajaka yogas
  - Dasha system names
  - Shadbalam sub-component names
  - Kuta names (Nadi, Gana, Varna, etc.)

  Regional nuance: auto-adapt pattern (§2.1)

  Some content is region-gated, not just translated — because classical astrology has region-specific practice:

  - Tamil users see Pongal, Karthigai Deepam, Aadi Perukku, Thirumana Porutham (10-porutham)
  - Malayalam users see Vishu, Onam, Kerala Prasna (via E10)
  - Telugu/Kannada users see Ugadi
  - Bengali users see Poila Boishakh, Durga Puja

  This isn't translation — it's showing different content based on region. Locked in §2.1 (Panchangam B2C) and §3.12 (Festival calendar).

  AI interpretation language

  Separate from UI labels: AI-generated interpretations (chart readings, neural pathway questions, consultation summaries) can be generated in the user's regional   
  language directly via GPT-4/Claude — this is a P1 scope item via the AI interpretation service. Not covered by §1.5 (which is label/term display).

  Important limitations

  - Calculation-layer is language-agnostic. All computation uses internal canonical English/IAST. Translation happens only at the UI display layer.
  - API responses (B2B) return classical_names as a dict — consumer picks which field to render. Default is sa_iast.
  - en English-gloss field is deprecated for display. It exists in current YAML for legacy reasons but should never be user-facing. "Mṛgaśīrā" stays Mṛgaśīrā; we    
  don't show "Orion" to users.

  What's not yet filled in

  The schema supports it; the YAML data files don't yet have all 8 regional scripts populated. Engineering action item: audit and fill every canonical entity across 
  all 8 regional scripts. This is Govind/engineering Pass 2 scope — explicitly called out in §1.5 action items and already on his plate.

  So: yes, designed in at the lock level; partially built; full data-fill is an engineering Pass 2 task.

✻ Churned for 35s                        

❯ will it support foreign languages                                                                                                                                                            
● Foreign languages — not currently locked in §1.5                                                                                                                   

  The current lock covers 8 Indian regional languages + Sanskrit-IAST English transliteration. Foreign languages are not in scope. Worth deciding now, because this    is a significant product-scope question that affects multiple PRDs.
                                                                                                                                                                       Why it matters (product vision tension)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   PRODUCT_VISION.md says "Global users across all traditions" + "Indian diaspora" + "B2B API for developers." The six traditions Josi supports (Vedic, Western,        Chinese, Hellenistic, Mayan, Celtic) aren't all Indian. So there's a genuine mismatch:                                                                             

  - Vedic/Tamil user in India → covered by §1.5 (Sanskrit IAST + Tamil)
  - Vedic user in US diaspora → covered (English IAST + chosen regional)
  - Western tradition user in France → NOT covered — they need French labels, and "always transliteration, never translation" rule means "Sūrya" when they expect    
  "Soleil"
  - Chinese tradition user in Singapore → not covered — they'd want 太阳 (tàiyáng) for Sun, not "Sūrya"
  - B2B API consumer building a Portuguese astrology app → needs Portuguese glosses for their UI

  What "foreign language" actually means here — 3 sub-questions

  1. For Vedic-tradition terms, should we add foreign-language transliterations? E.g., Spanish "Sūrya" rendered as "Suria" (Spanish phonetic) like Tamil is rendered 
  "Sooriyan"?
    - Plausible, but low-value. Spanish speakers who want Vedic astrology already accept Sanskrit terms.
  2. For non-Vedic traditions, should labels use the tradition's native-language canonical terms? E.g., Western tradition user sees "Sun / Mercury / Venus"
  (English); Chinese tradition user sees "太阳 / 水星 / 金星"; Hellenistic user sees "Hēlios / Hermēs / Aphroditē" (Greek).
    - High-value. This is proper multi-tradition rendering — each tradition uses its own canonical names.
  3. For AI-generated interpretations (chart readings, guidance), should output language match user's UI language even if it's French/Spanish/etc.?
    - Probably yes via GPT-4/Claude prompting. Low engineering cost.

  Options for the lock

  - A) Indian-only (current lock, no change). §1.5 stays as-is. Foreign languages out of scope indefinitely. Global users who aren't South Asian simply use the      
  English-IAST side. Simplest.
  - B) Add foreign language UI labels for 5-8 major languages (Spanish, French, Portuguese, German, Italian, Chinese, Arabic, Russian) — but only as UI chrome (menu,
   buttons, errors). Astrological terms stay in Sanskrit-IAST for Vedic tradition; switch to tradition-native terms for non-Vedic. Matches actual classical practice.
  - C) Full foreign language support — translate astrological terms. Add "Sun/Moon/Mercury" as English translation alongside IAST when user picks English as
  regional; similarly Spanish, French, Chinese, etc. This breaks the "always transliteration" rule in §1.5 but maximizes accessibility.
  - D) Tradition-native rendering (my recommendation). Per-tradition canonical labels:
    - Vedic tradition → Sanskrit-IAST + selected Indian regional (current §1.5 behavior preserved)
    - Western tradition → English common names ("Sun/Mars/Venus") — no IAST
    - Chinese tradition → Chinese canonical terms (太阳, 水星, etc.) with pinyin gloss option
    - Hellenistic tradition → Greek canonical (Hēlios, Hermēs) with English gloss
    - Mayan tradition → Yucatec Mayan names (K'in, U, Chak) with English gloss
    - Celtic tradition → Ogham/Irish-Gaelic + English
    - Plus foreign UI chrome in Spanish/French/Portuguese/German/Chinese/etc. for non-astrological UI elements

  This is what real multi-tradition practice looks like — no tradition's terms get mangled into another's.
  - E) Defer to P4 or P5. Not a P1/P2 concern. Vedic-only for initial launch. Add foreign-language support after core platform proven.

  My concrete recommendation

  D, deferred to P3 (not P1/P2). Here's why:

  - §1.5 as locked is correct for P1/P2 — Vedic is the primary engine and Indian regional languages are the primary market.
  - Options A/B/C all create compromises (A too narrow; B inconsistent; C breaks classical term integrity).
  - Option D is the right eventual architecture because it respects each tradition's classical term heritage, but it requires meaningful work in non-Vedic engines   
  (E8 Western Depth, Chinese/Mayan/Celtic PRDs in P5–P6) that haven't been specced yet.
  - Shipping D in P1/P2 would over-engineer; shipping A and then pivoting later would be backwards-compatible.

  What to lock now

  Minimal lock for v1:
  - §1.5 remains as-is for Vedic tradition (the only fully-specced tradition).
  - New §1.5a lock: "Multi-tradition rendering — each tradition uses its own canonical term heritage. Vedic = Sanskrit-IAST. Western/Hellenistic/Chinese/Mayan/Celtic
   = tradition-native terms. Specification for non-Vedic traditions deferred to each tradition's PRD (E8 Western specced; E8b Asteroids specced; Chinese/Mayan/Celtic
   in P5–P6 backlog). Foreign-language UI chrome (Spanish/French/Chinese/etc.) deferred to P3 with separate i18n PRD."

  This commits to the principle (tradition-native), defers the work (P3+), and doesn't block anything P1/P2.

  Want me to lock this as §1.5a + add a P3 placeholder PRD entry for i18n?

✻ Worked for 43s

● Update(docs\markdown\prd\DECISIONS.md)                                                                                                                             
  ⎿  Added 2 lines    
      79  - **Conversational convention (this review process):** Use **Tamil phonetic transliteration ONLY** — no Tamil script, no IAST diacritics. Examples:        
          "Dasai" not "Daśā" or "தசை"; "Uthiradam" not "Uttara Āṣāḍhā"; "Nazhigai" not "ghati"; "Emakandam" not "Yamagaṇḍa"; "Kuligai" not "Gulika Kāla"; "Prad                                                                                                                                                                           
   ──     osham" not "Pradoṣa"; "Karthikai" not "Kṛttikā"; "Sooriyan" not "Sūrya"; "Mirugasirsham" not "Mṛgaśīrā". Where a Tamil-specific term exists (nazhiga───────
          i, emakandam, kuligai, pongal), use that over the Sanskrit term. Durable docs (PRDs, DECISIONS.md itself) continue to use Sanskrit-IAST as canonical       
           for cross-reference stability across multi-tradition use.
      80  - **Hora exception 🔒 (revised 2026-04-22):** Use **"Hora"** (Sanskrit-IAST form without macron) instead of Tamil phonetic "Horai" throughout — beca       
          use the same word refers to 3 distinct classical concepts that need disambiguating qualifiers: (a) **Hora Bala** — Kala Bala sub-component per BPHS        
          Ch.27; (b) **Hora Lagna** — Special Lagna per BPHS Ch.4; (c) **Hora** (standalone) — Chaldean planetary hour per §3.11. The qualifier ("Bala" / "Lag       
          na" / standalone) carries the disambiguation. Using "Horai" for all three creates ambiguity with no benefit. This is a targeted exception to the Tam       
          il-phonetic rule; other Tamil-phonetic terms (Dasai, Uthiradam, Nazhigai, Kuligai, etc.) remain unchanged.
      81
      82 +- **Foreign-language support scope 🔒 (locked 2026-04-23, Option A — Indian-only policy continued):** The 8 Indian regional languages listed above (       
         +Tamil, Hindi, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, Bengali, Gujarati, Marathi) + Sanskrit-IAST English transliteration remain the only supported display lang       
         +uages for **P1 / P2 / P3**. Foreign languages (Spanish, French, Portuguese, German, Chinese, Arabic, Russian, etc.) are **out of scope**. Rationale:       
         + (a) Vedic is the primary engine and Indian regional market is the primary audience for v1–v3; (b) non-Vedic traditions (Western, Chinese, Hellenist       
         +ic, Mayan, Celtic) are not yet fully specced and premature foreign-language work would lock in compromises; (c) global B2B API consumers can consume       
         + `sa_iast` canonical and localize in their own UI layer; (d) AI interpretation language (GPT/Claude output) can already adapt naturally without sche       
         +ma changes. Revisit at **P4 or later** if global non-diaspora demand materializes; at that point likely adopt tradition-native rendering per traditi       
         +on's classical canon (Western = English common names, Hellenistic = Greek, Chinese = Chinese, etc.) — deferred decision.

● Memory updated — you + Govind pair-review Pass 2. Diving into Area #10 first (meta-workflow), as that shapes how we consume the other 9.

  ---
  Area #10 — Agent-team workflow (Pass 2 architectural Q1)

  Before we decide what architectural patterns to lock, we need to decide how agent teams consume those decisions and produce implementation. This shapes how        
  detailed the subsequent decisions need to be.

  The question

  Q1 — Agent team unit of work. What's the granularity at which an agent team picks up work?

  Three industry patterns, each with tradeoffs:

  - A) Per-PRD agent teams. One agent team owns one PRD end-to-end (schema → migration → model → repository → service → controller → tests → docs). Example: "E19    
  Shadbalam agent team" owns everything E19. Clean boundaries; easy to track; each team can be scoped independently. Downside: cross-PRD consistency (shared
  patterns, response envelopes, error handling) tends to drift because each team re-invents locally.
  - B) Per-layer agent teams (horizontal). Agent teams are organized by architectural layer: DB-schema team, migrations team, service-layer team, controller team,   
  test team. Each team sweeps across all 89 PRDs at its layer. Upside: consistency is enforced by specialization. Downside: coordination overhead (team-B waits for  
  team-A's schema to land; sequential dependencies stall work).
  - C) Per-epic (vertical slice) teams. Agent teams own an epic (e.g., "Classical Yoga Engines" = E4a + E4b; "Dasa Systems" = E1a + E1b + E1c; "Compatibility" = E25 
  + E29; "Panchangam" = panchang + E10 + muhurtam). One team owns all PRDs within the epic end-to-end. Balances A and B — big enough to enforce consistency within a 
  domain, small enough to avoid end-to-end stalls. ← my recommendation.
  - D) Hybrid — per-epic for calculation engines + per-layer for cross-cutting concerns. Epic teams own engines (E1a, E4a, E19, etc.); cross-cutting teams own shared
   infrastructure (caching, auth, API envelope, observability, CI). Most expensive to coordinate but best for a complex product. Realistic for Josi given the        
  6-tradition scope.

  Note on "agent team": by this I mean either (a) a single sub-agent spawned with a specific PRD+scope brief, or (b) multiple specialized sub-agents (architect,     
  implementer, tester, reviewer) collaborating on a scoped chunk. You can decide that format separately — the question here is just the unit of work.

  Which granularity?

  ┌─────┬───────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

  │ 4   │ Audit-ready   │ Every calculation has source-trace (which classical rule, which input values, which code path). Immutable event log for user actions.   │  
  │     │               │ Versioned classical-rule registry. Reproducible from inputs+version.                                                                    │

  Proposed review order

  1. Foundation layer first — F1–F17 (rule registry, source_id, classical-source plumbing, response envelope, golden fixtures, feature flags, etc.). These are the

  Panchadha Maitri feeds Graha Maitri koota, etc.).

  6. P4–P6 — scale, differentiation, institution-level PRDs.

  research on what factors to consider for an application like this.

● Agent(Research: factors for AI-native astrology SaaS review)
  ⎿  Backgrounded agent (↓ to manage · ctrl+o to expand)

● Research agent running. Here's my structured list of clarifying questions — organized by leverage. We don't have to answer all at once; pick what's crispest for   
  you today.

  ---
  Meta-factors worth clarifying before spec-hardening starts

  1. Business context (shapes the quality bar)

  Q1.1 — Launch timeline. Are we hardening to ship v1 in 3 months, v1 in 9 months, or v1 in 18 months? Aggressive timelines mean "implementation-ready-enough,       
  refactor later." Long horizons mean "get it right on first pass."

  Q1.2 — User scale at launch. Designing for 10K B2C users + 100 astrologers, or 1M users + 10K astrologers? Shapes every architectural call — sharding, caching, LLM
   cost envelopes, payment providers.

  Q1.3 — Revenue pressure. Is there external funding runway (reviewer polish over speed), bootstrapped (revenue-first, ugly-but-working), or
  internal-stakeholder-funded (politics-aware)?

  Q1.4 — Geographic launch sequence. India-first → diaspora → global? Or global from day 1? Matters for data residency, payment rails (UPI for India; Stripe for     
  global), LLM region selection.

  2. Team & delivery (shapes spec detail level)

  Q2.1 — Agent-team composition. How many parallel agent teams? 2-3 means specs need to be terse-but-clear. 10-15 means specs need to be near-code-level to avoid    
  conflicts. Your mention of PM + coding + QA + frontend-design + frontend-dev suggests ~5 functional roles — how many instances of each running in parallel?        

  Q2.2 — Agent autonomy level. Are agents directed (humans review every PR), supervised (humans sample PRs), or autonomous (humans only review integration)? More    
  autonomy = more detailed specs needed.

  Q2.3 — Human-review cadence. Does cpselvam + Govind review each PRD's implementation output before merge? Or weekly batch?

  3. Scale, performance, cost (shapes architecture)

  Q3.1 — Chart calculation latency target. <200ms for "instant" UX? <500ms acceptable? <2s OK if backgrounded? Shapes cache design, precomputation, and whether we   
  need eager Ashtakavargam or lazy.

  Q3.2 — LLM cost envelope per user per month. $0.10/user (aggressive cache), $1/user (reasonable), $5/user (premium tier only)? Drives provider routing +
  context-packaging decisions.

  Q3.3 — Peak-traffic assumptions. Smooth load or spiky (e.g., auspicious-time muhurtam checks on festival mornings)? Affects autoscaling + Redis capacity.

  4. Regulatory & compliance (shapes data model + API design)

  Q4.1 — Data residency. India's DPDP Act increasingly demands India-resident data for India users. US/EU compliance for diaspora. Multi-region from day 1 or later? 

  Q4.2 — Audit-ready scope. Full calculation audit trail for every chart (every rule evaluated, every input, every version)? Or sampled? Audit-trail cost compounds  
  at scale.

  Q4.3 — Disclaimer discipline. Astrology + AI = regulatory risk. "This is guidance not advice" disclaimer in API response? Consent flow? Age gates (minor users)?   
  Mental-health crisis detection (user mentions self-harm during AI chat → escalation)?

  Q4.4 — Consultation recording + transcription. Video/audio consultations — retain? Delete after X days? Transcribe for AI summary and discard audio? HIPAA-adjacent
   concerns for mental-health-adjacent topics.

  5. AI-first investment profile (shapes AI architecture)

  Q5.1 — Primary LLM strategy. Single-provider (Claude or GPT) vs multi-provider routing? Fallback when primary is rate-limited — same-quality fallback or
  degraded-but-available?

  Q5.2 — Eval infrastructure priority. Build LLM-quality eval harness in P1 (catches regressions, enables A/B of prompts)? Or defer to P3? Most platforms defer and  
  regret it.

  Q5.3 — On-device / private-model strategy. Is there a future path to local inference for privacy-conscious users (running smaller models on user's device or       
  private cloud)?

  Q5.4 — Data-flywheel consent. Can user queries + chart data be used for model evaluation / fine-tuning (with consent)? Or opt-in only? Or never? Affects product   
  value over time.

  6. Extensibility horizon (shapes plugin architecture)

  Q6.1 — New traditions/lineages in 24 months. 0 (just the 6 + current specs)? 2-3 (e.g., Sri Lankan Sinhalese tradition, South African witchcraft, Japanese
  Onmyodo)? 10+? Changes how plugin-able the rule engine needs to be.

  Q6.2 — B2B customization depth. Can B2B customers add their own rules? Override thresholds? White-label branding? Full-tenant-themed UIs?

  7. Operational maturity (shapes infra PRDs)

  Q7.1 — On-call model. Govind + cpselvam as primary on-call, or hired SRE? Determines observability investment.

  Q7.2 — Deploy frequency target. Continuous (multiple daily), daily, weekly, monthly? Affects CI investment.

  Q7.3 — Security posture target for Year 1. Informal → formalized → SOC2 Type 1 → SOC2 Type 2 → HIPAA-adjacent? Affects logging, access controls, secret management.

  8. Risk tolerance / quality bar

  Q8.1 — MVP philosophy. "Ship ugly, iterate" vs "Ship polished, iterate less." Affects how aggressively we cut scope during Pass 2.

  Q8.2 — Known acceptable-imperfections. E.g., "KP system can be 80% accurate in v1"; "Chinese tradition can be stubbed"; "Sri Lagna can be computed but not
  displayed." Where are you willing to accept incomplete to ship?

  ---
  How I'd use your answers

  These factors cascade into different review decisions per PRD. Example: if Q3.1 says "<200ms P95," then every E-PRD needs a precomputation strategy locked. If Q4.2
   says "full audit trail," every F-PRD needs event-log schema locked. If Q6.1 says "10+ traditions in 24 months," the plugin pattern gets stricter.

  Suggested approach

  Tier 1 (answer first, highest leverage): Q1.1, Q1.2, Q2.1, Q3.1, Q3.2, Q4.2, Q6.1, Q8.1.
  Tier 2 (can defer): the rest — we'll ask in context when a PRD review surfaces them.

  Research agent should return shortly with additional factors I might be missing. Want to start answering Tier 1 now, or wait for the research report first?

● Agent "Research: factors for AI-native astrology SaaS review" completed

● Research back. It surfaced 8 material factors my initial list missed or understated. Consolidating.

  ---
  Additions from research (high-leverage, not in my initial list)

  A1. AI provenance / determinism contract (critical — affects every AI PRD)

  Every AI-generated artifact (chart interpretation, NPQ response, consult summary) needs prompt version + model version + context hash stamped for reproducibility, 
  audit, and regression detection. Retrofit is painful across 89 PRDs. Needs to be a foundation-layer decision before any AI PRD locks.

  A2. LLM eval harness as P1 blocker (not P3 deferred)

  Agent's strong claim: no AI PRD should ship without a golden-dataset eval + LLM-as-judge regression gate. If we defer this to P3, agent teams will ship untested   
  prompts by then and we'll be retrofitting. Should move from P3 → P1 foundation.

  A3. Crisis escalation & self-harm flow (regulatory + ethical)

  Concrete version of the "guidance vs advice" question: "If a user writes 'I want to end it all' in an NPQ reflection or AI chat, what must the system do?" This    
  determines whether we need a safety classifier as a foundation service, crisis handoff URLs per geography, and audit logs of these events.

  A4. Structured Outputs as canonical AI contract

  Lock early: every LLM ↔ service boundary uses Pydantic schema + Anthropic tool-use / OpenAI Structured Outputs — never JSON mode with regex parsing. Prevents      
  brittle integration. Affects E11a, E11b, all AI-tool-use interfaces.

  A5. Prompt-injection boundary (security)

  Formal "untrusted text → sanitized context" perimeter. Injection vectors: user chat, consult transcripts, astrologer-authored bio copy, any uploaded birth
  certificate scans. OWASP LLM01 is explicit. Needs a single canonical sanitization layer, not per-PRD handling.

  A6. Stripe-Connect gap for India payouts

  Stripe Connect is NOT available in India as payee. Astrologer marketplace needs dual-rail: Stripe for US/global astrologers, RazorpayX/Cashfree for India-resident 
  astrologers. Plus GST invoicing (India), 1099-K/1042-S (US), sanctions screening, KYC at onboarding. This is a concrete architecture-forcing constraint that       
  reshapes E-marketplace PRDs.

  A7. Vector embeddings as personal data

  Qdrant chart embeddings enable reverse-lookup identification of users. Under DPDP/GDPR, they're personal data — need tenant-namespacing, consent, and inclusion in 
  "right to be forgotten" deletion flow. Affects data model + deletion tooling design.

  A8. Cultural/religious editorial owner (governance role)

  Who signs off on Tamil/Hindi copy, deity/temple iconography, Upaya remedy wording, AI-generated religious imagery? Without a named editorial owner, this becomes   
  reputational PR risk. Should be a role/process lock, not a technical one.

  A9 (regulatory deadlines, concrete)

  - India DPDP Rules 2025 — 12-month consent-manager integration from notification (already running). Affects data model now.
  - EU AI Act Aug 2, 2026 — GPAI deployer transparency obligations (AI-generated content must be labeled). ~3 months away.
  - FTC AI rules (US) — AI-generated reviews/testimonials must be labeled. Affects astrologer review system.
  - India Consumer Protection Act — "guaranteed outcome" copy in Upaya PRDs = misleading-ad liability. Affects E28 copy.

  A10. Deletion surface is multi-system

  Right-to-erasure touches: Postgres soft-delete, Redis cache, Qdrant vectors, LLM provider logs (OpenAI/Anthropic retention). Deletion architecture is its own      
  sub-problem, not a one-line DB UPDATE.

  ---

  ---
  T1.1 — Launch geography + sequence

  Shapes data residency, payment rails, compliance timeline, and marketing focus. This is the single most cascading architectural question.

  Options:

  - A) India-first, hard. Launch India-only for first 6 months. Indian diaspora can use it via app stores but no active marketing outside India. US + EU expansion   
  Month 7–12. Data residency: India-only servers at launch; multi-region later. DPDP 2025 compliance hard-locked; CCPA/GDPR deferred until expansion. Payment:       
  RazorpayX/Cashfree primary; Stripe added for diaspora astrologer payouts Month 7. Simplest architecture; lowest compliance surface; lowest LLM cost (India
  inference cheaper).
  - B) India + Indian-diaspora simultaneous (US/UK). Launch Month 1 serves India + US/UK Indian diaspora. Multi-region data residency (India + US-East) from day 1.  
  DPDP + CCPA + UK GDPR at launch; EU-proper (Schengen zone) deferred. Dual payment rails (RazorpayX + Stripe). More compliance surface but captures the diaspora    
  audience that product-vision explicitly names. ← my recommendation (matches PRODUCT_VISION.md's stated primary audience).
  - C) Global simultaneous. India + US + EU + UK all launch Month 1. Full compliance stack: DPDP + CCPA + GDPR + EU AI Act readiness + UK Online Safety. Tri-region  
  data residency. Maximum addressable market but 3–4 extra months of compliance work upfront. Payment: triple rail (Razorpay + Stripe + SEPA).
  - D) Staged: India Q1 → US/UK/diaspora Q3 → EU Q5. Compromise between A and B. Ship India first, add diaspora 6 months later, EU 12 months out. Gives team
  breathing room but delays diaspora revenue (which is likely higher ARPU than India-resident revenue).

  Which?

  ---
  T1.2 — "Guidance vs advice" liability posture + crisis flow

  Where does Josi legally/ethically sit on the spectrum from entertainment → wellness guidance → medical/psychological advice? This shapes disclaimer discipline,    
  safety-classifier foundations, crisis-escalation flows, and copywriting guidance for all AI-generated content.

  Josi has 3 surfaces where this matters most:
  - AI chart interpretations (generic chart guidance)
  - Neural Pathway Questions (psychological self-reflection — users share personal thoughts)
  - Upaya / Remedies (specific "do X, get Y" suggestions)

  Plus the specific crisis-flow question: if a user writes something self-harm-adjacent during NPQ or AI chat, what must the system do?

  Options

  - A) "Entertainment only" posture. Josi is astrology, not advice — hard legal positioning. All outputs footer-disclaimed "entertainment only, not professional     
  advice." NPQ has no crisis classifier; standard "call a helpline" footer if triggered by regex keywords. Upaya copy says "tradition suggests" never "will cause."  
  Minimum legal surface, minimum engineering. Risk: doesn't match how users will actually consume the product (users will treat NPQ as therapy-adjacent regardless of
   disclaimers).
  - B) "Wellness guidance" posture + soft crisis flow. Josi positions itself as wellness/self-reflection tool (not entertainment, not medical). Disclaimers
  structural (in response envelope, not footer). NPQ has a soft-triggered safety classifier that detects distress signals → gentle redirect with geography-specific  
  crisis hotlines (iCall/Vandrevala/NIMHANS for India; 988 for US; Samaritans for UK; EU-country-specific). Upaya copy uses subjunctive language ("may support,"     
  "classical tradition associates with"). Middle-ground legal posture; moderate engineering investment. ← my recommendation.
  - C) "Medical-adjacent" posture + hard crisis flow. Josi is taken as seriously as a therapy-adjunct tool. Hard-triggered safety classifier on every user text input
   (NPQ + AI chat + consultation notes) → crisis-level responses block the AI from replying generatively and surface mandatory crisis resources + optional "connect  
  to emergency services." Strongest user safety; highest engineering cost + liability exposure (more regulatory scrutiny, not less). Also: astrologer marketplace    
  requires trained-counselor-adjacent onboarding vetting.
  - D) Split posture per surface. Chart interpretations = wellness (B-style). NPQ = medical-adjacent (C-style, hard crisis flow). Upaya = entertainment (A-style).   
  Astrologer consultations = professional (astrologers vetted for credentials + ethics, assumed responsible). Most nuanced; highest engineering investment. Matches  
  real risk distribution (NPQ is genuinely therapy-adjacent; chart interpretations aren't).

  Which?

✻ Cogitated for 1m 0s

❯ B

● T1.2 locked → B (Wellness guidance + soft crisis flow).

● Moving to T1.3.

  ---
  T1.3 — Launch timeline

  How long until v1 ships? This cascades into the quality bar for every downstream decision — aggressive timelines mean "implementation-ready-enough, polish in      
  v1.1"; long horizons mean "get it right on first pass."

  Context:
  - Global simultaneous launch (§0.1) implies 3–4 months of pure compliance work upfront before product work
  - Wellness posture (§0.2) requires foundation-service classifier + editorial copy review (~1 month)
  - 89 PRDs with ~60 meaningful implementation surfaces (engines + UI + infra)

  Options

  - A) Aggressive — v1 in 6 months. Compliance-first phase Month 1-3 (legal, payment rails, data residency, crisis classifier, editorial governance). Engine phase   
  Month 3-5 (P1 MVP engines only — E1a/E2a/E4a/E6a + AI chat + panchangam). UI + marketplace Month 5-6. Ship MVP with Western/Chinese/Hellenistic/Mayan/Celtic as    
  stub engines returning "coming soon." Vedic-only at depth. Ship fast, iterate. Risk: scope of 6-tradition promise underdelivered on launch.
  - B) Balanced — v1 in 9 months. Compliance Month 1-3 in parallel with P1 engines. Foundation (F1-F17) + P1 MVP Month 3-5. P2 depth + GAP_CLOSURE (E18-E29) Month   
  5-7. UI + marketplace + AI orchestration Month 7-8. Polish + soft launch Month 9. All 6 traditions have working engines; Vedic + Western at full depth, others at  
  "basic but functional." Reasonable scope, reasonable timeline. ← my recommendation.
  - C) Considered — v1 in 12-15 months. Compliance Month 1-3. Foundation Month 3-4. Engines (all P1 + P2 + GAP_CLOSURE) Month 4-9. UI + marketplace Month 9-11. AI   
  quality/eval harness + Neural Pathway Questions Month 11-13. Polish + beta Month 13-15. All 6 traditions at meaningful depth. Full eval harness + observability    
  before launch. Lower risk, higher polish, more runway burn.
  - D) Conservative — v1 in 18-24 months. Full Pass 2 review of every PRD before coding starts (~2 months). Foundation layer built with full observability + audit   
  trail before any engine work (~2 months). Phased engine rollout with eval gates at every step. Launch only after every GAP_CLOSURE PRD is built + every eval       
  harness passing + SOC2 Type 1 audit complete. Lowest risk, highest investment, best for raising Series A with "enterprise-ready" positioning.

  Which?

✻ Crunched for 1m 5s

  Smart synthesis — agents parallelize coding in ways human teams can't, so the bottleneck shifts from implementation time to spec quality. Investing ~2 months      
  upfront in hardened specs unlocks 4 months of parallel agent work.

● §0.2 = Launch timeline, §0.3 = Liability posture. Moving to T1.4.

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  T1.4 — User scale target at launch
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Drives architecture choices: sharding (P4 S7 decision now vs later), caching aggression, autoscaling, connection pooling, Qdrant sizing, LLM cost envelope
  multiplier.

  Context: global simultaneous launch (§0.1) + 6-month timeline (§0.2) + wellness positioning + astrologer marketplace.

  Options — Month-6 launch targets

  - A) Modest: 10K B2C users + 100 astrologers. Single-region Postgres per geography works; Redis single-node per region; Qdrant small cluster; LLM costs <$5K/mo. No
   sharding needed. Marketing footprint: organic + diaspora word-of-mouth. Conservative; matches bootstrapped-revenue cadence.
  - B) Moderate: 100K B2C users + 1K astrologers. Postgres with read-replicas per region (P3 S2 needed at launch); Redis cluster; Qdrant sharded; LLM costs ~$50K/mo.
   Marketing: paid acquisition + PR push at launch. Requires meaningful compute budget. ← my recommendation — matches "global simultaneous" ambition realistically.  
  - C) Aggressive: 1M B2C users + 10K astrologers. Multi-region active-active Postgres (P4 S7 sharding needed at launch); Redis multi-region; Qdrant large
  distributed cluster; LLM costs $500K/mo+. Marketing: heavy paid-acquisition + strategic partnerships. Requires significant pre-launch funding. Most of the P4 scale
   PRDs need to land at launch not later.
  - D) Variable: plan for 100K nominal, architect for graceful scale to 1M. Build sharding-ready schemas (tenant_id + user_id composite keys, region-aware routing)  
  even though only using one shard; Redis keys namespaced for future sharding; Qdrant tenant-partitioned. Costs track B. Upside: no architectural rewrite when we hit
   1M. Downside: ~10-15% extra engineering discipline throughout.

  Which?

✻ Cogitated for 2m 11s                     

❯ Let's do Option D, but let's provision resources in such a way that, we practically spend money based on traffic strictly. Let's try to also evaluate options to     run LLM using the most cheapest but capable models, or even self hosting open source models and using that for Inference. We can also layer in a PRD to train our    own LLM based on the data as we collect to make a proprietary SLM. If we have 1k users and 10 astrologers, we should spend accordingly. let's also factor in         finops aggresively since we want to make sure we provide the best customer experience with the cost we provide.
