● E-SLM added to P3 tracker. Moving to T1.6.

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  T1.6 — AI provenance / reproducibility stamping
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Every AI-generated artifact (chart interpretation, NPQ response, consult summary, astrologer-facing workbench LLM output, agent-written code from Phase 3) needs
  metadata stamped for audit, reproducibility, regression detection, and A/B evaluation.

  This is the single biggest retrofit cost risk — if we don't lock this at foundation, agent teams building AI PRDs in Phase 3 will ship inconsistent logging, and   
  stitching it together later costs weeks.

  What "stamping" means concretely

  Every AI output stored in DB or returned from API carries:
  - prompt_template_id + prompt_version (git-tracked)
  - model_provider (anthropic | openai | self-hosted-llama)
  - model_name + model_version (e.g., claude-sonnet-4-6, llama-3.1-8b-instruct-v3)
  - context_hash (SHA-256 of assembled context window before inference)
  - tool_calls_made (list of tool names + args, structured)
  - temperature, max_tokens, other sampling params
  - latency_ms, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_usd
  - safety_classifier_verdict (pass / crisis-detected / moderation-flagged)
  - content_class per §0.3 (entertainment / wellness_guidance / classical_tradition / professional_consultation)
  - user_region + llm_region per §0.1

  Options

  - A) Full-stamp every AI output, synchronous (hot path). Every single AI artifact gets full metadata inlined at write time. Highest audit fidelity; adds ~5ms +    
  ~2KB per row. Perfectly reproducible. Expensive at scale.
  - B) Full-stamp every AI output, async (hot path unblocked). Stamp metadata written to event log asynchronously; AI artifact references log entry by UUID. Hot path
   not blocked; audit complete but eventually-consistent (rare log-entry-missing edge cases need reconciliation). ← my recommendation for production traffic.        
  - C) Full-stamp 100% for B2B + astrologer + Mystic/Master tier; sample 10% for free-tier B2C. Quality tiers have full audit (compliance + debugging); free tier has
   sampling (cost-sensitive). Mixed-tier audit complicates GDPR/DPDP "right to explanation" claims — if user requests their AI-generation audit trail and we only    
  have sampled data, we've got a gap.
  - D) Full-stamp everything + separate Qdrant collection for semantic audit. Every AI artifact gets stamp + its embedding indexed in an "audit" Qdrant collection.  
  Enables semantic regression detection ("this week's outputs semantically diverged from last week's by X%"). Most powerful but adds Qdrant storage cost.

  Which?

✻ Crunched for 1m 59s                      

❯ what is qdrant, why do we need it                

Moving to T1.3.

  ---
  T1.3 — Launch timeline

  How long until v1 ships? This cascades into the quality bar for every downstream decision — aggressive timelines mean "implementation-ready-enough, polish in      
  v1.1"; long horizons mean "get it right on first pass."

  Context:
  - Global simultaneous launch (§0.1) implies 3–4 months of pure compliance work upfront before product work
  - Wellness posture (§0.2) requires foundation-service classifier + editorial copy review (~1 month)
  - 89 PRDs with ~60 meaningful implementation surfaces (engines + UI + infra)

  Options

  - A) Aggressive — v1 in 6 months. Compliance-first phase Month 1-3 (legal, payment rails, data residency, crisis classifier, editorial governance). Engine phase   
  Month 3-5 (P1 MVP engines only — E1a/E2a/E4a/E6a + AI chat + panchangam). UI + marketplace Month 5-6. Ship MVP with Western/Chinese/Hellenistic/Mayan/Celtic as    
  stub engines returning "coming soon." Vedic-only at depth. Ship fast, iterate. Risk: scope of 6-tradition promise underdelivered on launch.
  - B) Balanced — v1 in 9 months. Compliance Month 1-3 in parallel with P1 engines. Foundation (F1-F17) + P1 MVP Month 3-5. P2 depth + GAP_CLOSURE (E18-E29) Month   
  5-7. UI + marketplace + AI orchestration Month 7-8. Polish + soft launch Month 9. All 6 traditions have working engines; Vedic + Western at full depth, others at  
  "basic but functional." Reasonable scope, reasonable timeline. ← my recommendation.
  - C) Considered — v1 in 12-15 months. Compliance Month 1-3. Foundation Month 3-4. Engines (all P1 + P2 + GAP_CLOSURE) Month 4-9. UI + marketplace Month 9-11. AI   
  quality/eval harness + Neural Pathway Questions Month 11-13. Polish + beta Month 13-15. All 6 traditions at meaningful depth. Full eval harness + observability    
  before launch. Lower risk, higher polish, more runway burn.
  - D) Conservative — v1 in 18-24 months. Full Pass 2 review of every PRD before coding starts (~2 months). Foundation layer built with full observability + audit   
  trail before any engine work (~2 months). Phased engine rollout with eval gates at every step. Launch only after every GAP_CLOSURE PRD is built + every eval       
  harness passing + SOC2 Type 1 audit complete. Lowest risk, highest investment, best for raising Series A with "enterprise-ready" positioning.

  Which?

✻ Crunched for 1m 5s

❯ I want A with D's full pass 2 review of every PRD, so let's do the review and then plan for an aggressive implementation using agents.

● T1.3 locked → hybrid A+D (full Pass 2 review upfront, then aggressive agent-parallel implementation for 6-month v1).

  Smart synthesis — agents parallelize coding in ways human teams can't, so the bottleneck shifts from implementation time to spec quality. Investing ~2 months      
  upfront in hardened specs unlocks 4 months of parallel agent work.

● §0.2 = Launch timeline, §0.3 = Liability posture. Moving to T1.4.

  ---
  T1.4 — User scale target at launch

  Drives architecture choices: sharding (P4 S7 decision now vs later), caching aggression, autoscaling, connection pooling, Qdrant sizing, LLM cost envelope
  multiplier.

  Context: global simultaneous launch (§0.1) + 6-month timeline (§0.2) + wellness positioning + astrologer marketplace.

  Options — Month-6 launch targets

  - A) Modest: 10K B2C users + 100 astrologers. Single-region Postgres per geography works; Redis single-node per region; Qdrant small cluster; LLM costs <$5K/mo. No
   sharding needed. Marketing footprint: organic + diaspora word-of-mouth. Conservative; matches bootstrapped-revenue cadence.
  - B) Moderate: 100K B2C users + 1K astrologers. Postgres with read-replicas per region (P3 S2 needed at launch); Redis cluster; Qdrant sharded; LLM costs ~$50K/mo.
   Marketing: paid acquisition + PR push at launch. Requires meaningful compute budget. ← my recommendation — matches "global simultaneous" ambition realistically.  
  - C) Aggressive: 1M B2C users + 10K astrologers. Multi-region active-active Postgres (P4 S7 sharding needed at launch); Redis multi-region; Qdrant large
  distributed cluster; LLM costs $500K/mo+. Marketing: heavy paid-acquisition + strategic partnerships. Requires significant pre-launch funding. Most of the P4 scale
   PRDs need to land at launch not later.
  - D) Variable: plan for 100K nominal, architect for graceful scale to 1M. Build sharding-ready schemas (tenant_id + user_id composite keys, region-aware routing)  
  even though only using one shard; Redis keys namespaced for future sharding; Qdrant tenant-partitioned. Costs track B. Upside: no architectural rewrite when we hit
   1M. Downside: ~10-15% extra engineering discipline throughout.

  Which?

✻ Cogitated for 2m 11s

❯ Let's do Option D, but let's provision resources in such a way that, we practically spend money based on traffic strictly. Let's try to also evaluate options to   
  run LLM using the most cheapest but capable models, or even self hosting open source models and using that for Inference. We can also layer in a PRD to train our  
  own LLM based on the data as we collect to make a proprietary SLM. If we have 1k users and 10 astrologers, we should spend accordingly. let's also factor in       
  finops aggresively since we want to make sure we provide the best customer experience with the cost we provide.

● T1.4 locked → D with aggressive FinOps overlay (architect for 1M, scale spend with traffic, multi-tier LLM cost optimization, proprietary SLM on roadmap).

  Rolling T1.5 (LLM cost envelope) into this — your direction effectively answers both.

          +** (scale-to-1-ACU minimum) per region; graduate to provisioned + Citus sharding when single-region hits 50K QPS sustained.                               
       91 +- Redis: **ElastiCache Serverless** (pay-per-ops, not per-node) or **Upstash Redis** (truly serverless, scale-to-zero for low-tier caches). Namespa       
          +ce all keys `{region}:{tenant}:{key}` so sharding is schema-ready.                                                                                        
       92 +- Qdrant: **Qdrant Cloud** with tenant-partitioned collections; scale-to-zero between tenants. Embeddings lazily computed on first retrieval, not e       
          +agerly on chart creation (FinOps + matches S6 lazy compute).

          +hot paths with predictable P99 latency SLO).

● E-SLM added to P3 tracker. Moving to T1.6.

  ---
  T1.6 — AI provenance / reproducibility stamping

  Every AI-generated artifact (chart interpretation, NPQ response, consult summary, astrologer-facing workbench LLM output, agent-written code from Phase 3) needs   
  metadata stamped for audit, reproducibility, regression detection, and A/B evaluation.

  This is the single biggest retrofit cost risk — if we don't lock this at foundation, agent teams building AI PRDs in Phase 3 will ship inconsistent logging, and   
  stitching it together later costs weeks.

  What "stamping" means concretely

  Every AI output stored in DB or returned from API carries:
  - prompt_template_id + prompt_version (git-tracked)
  - model_provider (anthropic | openai | self-hosted-llama)
  - model_name + model_version (e.g., claude-sonnet-4-6, llama-3.1-8b-instruct-v3)
  - context_hash (SHA-256 of assembled context window before inference)
  - tool_calls_made (list of tool names + args, structured)
  - temperature, max_tokens, other sampling params
  - latency_ms, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_usd
  - safety_classifier_verdict (pass / crisis-detected / moderation-flagged)
  - content_class per §0.3 (entertainment / wellness_guidance / classical_tradition / professional_consultation)
  - user_region + llm_region per §0.1

  Options

  - A) Full-stamp every AI output, synchronous (hot path). Every single AI artifact gets full metadata inlined at write time. Highest audit fidelity; adds ~5ms +    
  ~2KB per row. Perfectly reproducible. Expensive at scale.
  - B) Full-stamp every AI output, async (hot path unblocked). Stamp metadata written to event log asynchronously; AI artifact references log entry by UUID. Hot path
   not blocked; audit complete but eventually-consistent (rare log-entry-missing edge cases need reconciliation). ← my recommendation for production traffic.        
  - C) Full-stamp 100% for B2B + astrologer + Mystic/Master tier; sample 10% for free-tier B2C. Quality tiers have full audit (compliance + debugging); free tier has
   sampling (cost-sensitive). Mixed-tier audit complicates GDPR/DPDP "right to explanation" claims — if user requests their AI-generation audit trail and we only    
  have sampled data, we've got a gap.
  - D) Full-stamp everything + separate Qdrant collection for semantic audit. Every AI artifact gets stamp + its embedding indexed in an "audit" Qdrant collection.  
  Enables semantic regression detection ("this week's outputs semantically diverged from last week's by X%"). Most powerful but adds Qdrant storage cost.

  Which?

✻ Crunched for 1m 59s

❯ what is qdrant, why do we need it

● Good question — I've been treating it as given. Here's what Qdrant is and whether Josi actually needs it.

  ---
  What is Qdrant?

  Qdrant (pronounced "quadrant") is a vector database — a specialized database that stores high-dimensional numeric vectors (called "embeddings") and lets you       
  quickly find vectors that are similar to a query vector.

  The one-line mental model

  Regular databases answer: "Give me rows where user_id = 123."
  Vector databases answer: "Give me rows whose embedding is most similar to this query embedding."

  Why "similarity"?

  When you convert text (or a chart, image, audio) into an embedding via an AI model, that embedding is a point in a 768-dim or 1536-dim or 3072-dim space. Two      
  pieces of content that are semantically similar end up as geometrically nearby points in this space. So "find similar" becomes "find nearest points."

  What Qdrant does technically

  - Stores billions of vectors efficiently
  - Runs approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in milliseconds (HNSW algorithm)
  - Supports metadata filters alongside similarity (e.g., "most similar vectors WHERE tenant_id = X AND tradition = 'vedic'")
  - Open-source (Rust), self-hostable, also has managed cloud
  - Competitors: Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, pgvector (Postgres extension)

  ---
  Why Josi uses Qdrant

  Three concrete features in Josi's PRD corpus depend on vector similarity:

  1. Chart similarity search ("chart twins")

  When a user asks "show me users whose charts are similar to mine and went through their Saturn Return", the system:
  - Converts the user's chart (rashi + nakshatra + dasa + yoga configuration) into an embedding
  - Qdrant finds the top-N nearest embeddings
  - UI surfaces "people with your pattern" stories, outcomes, AI pattern analysis

  One of the 8 unique competitive angles from FEATURE_SUMMARY.md — no competitor offers this. Strategic moat if we keep it.

  2. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) for AI interpretations

  When the AI generates a chart interpretation, it needs classical reference material — BPHS passages, Phaladeepika verses, prior interpretations that matched       
  similar placements.

  - We embed every classical text passage, every locked yoga rule, every past interpretation
  - Qdrant retrieves the 5-10 most-relevant passages for the current chart's specific placements
  - LLM gets those passages as grounded context, not just its training-data memory
  - This is why Josi's AI is better than ChatGPT-astrology — it reasons over classical canon, not vaguely-remembered training data

  Without this, AI interpretations are generic internet-astrology quality. With this, they cite specific classical passages tied to the user's actual placements.    

  3. Neural Pathway Questions (NPQ) — context for follow-ups

  NPQ builds on previous user responses. When a user answers NPQ #5, the system:
  - Embeds their response
  - Qdrant finds semantically related prior responses (this user's own history + anonymized similar-chart users' responses with consent)
  - Generates NPQ #6 that builds on themes, not repeats them

  Without Qdrant, NPQs become disconnected questionnaires.

  ---
  Do we actually need Qdrant specifically?

  Not necessarily — we need vector search. Options:

  ┌────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐    
  │         Option         │                     Pros                      │                       Cons                       │              When               │    
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Qdrant Cloud           │ Fast, specialized, scale-tested,              │ Extra service to run + pay for                   │ Default choice — what's in      │    
  │                        │ tenant-partitioning native                    │                                                  │ current PRDs                    │    
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ pgvector (Postgres     │ Reuses existing Postgres infra, no new        │ Slower at >10M vectors, less advanced filtering, │ FinOps-friendly for v1 scale    │    
  │ extension)             │ service, cheapest                             │  scale ceiling lower                             │                                 │    
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Pinecone               │ Managed, no-ops                               │ More expensive, US-only data residency (fails    │ Eliminated for Josi             │    
  │                        │                                               │ §0.1 EU requirement)                             │                                 │    
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Weaviate / Milvus      │ Similar to Qdrant                             │ Higher operational overhead                      │ No advantage over Qdrant        │    
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Skip vectors entirely  │ Simplest                                      │ Lose chart similarity + AI RAG quality drops     │ Only if we drop features #1,    │    
  │                        │                                               │ significantly                                    │ #2, #3 above                    │    
  └────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘    

  FinOps-aware recommendation

  Given §0.4 (spend tracks demand), there's a meaningful case for:

  Start with pgvector on Postgres (launch → 100K users), graduate to Qdrant Cloud (>100K users).

  - At 1K users: pgvector is effectively free (Postgres already running), ~100K embeddings, fast enough
  - At 100K users: pgvector starts hitting scale limits, ~10M embeddings — migrate to Qdrant
  - Schema-ready from day 1 so migration is data copy + connection-string swap, not rewrite

  This would save ~$200-500/mo during the 1K-100K user phase. Matches "spend accordingly" principle from §0.4.

  The tradeoff

  pgvector-first means:
  - ✅ Cheaper at small scale
  - ✅ One fewer service to operate + secure + migrate in Phase 2
  - ⚠️ Migration at 100K-user threshold (minor — embedding re-index + connection change, ~1 day of agent work)
  - ⚠️ pgvector's HNSW is younger/less battle-tested than Qdrant's (mostly stable as of 2026)

  My updated recommendation

  Revise §0.4: start with pgvector (Postgres extension) as primary vector store; architect abstraction layer so Qdrant Cloud is a drop-in replacement when scale     
  demands. Update PRDs that say "Qdrant" to say "vector store (pgvector v1 → Qdrant at scale)."

  Want me to:
  - A) Revise §0.4 to pgvector-first + update affected PRDs, then return to T1.6 (AI provenance stamping).
  - B) Keep Qdrant as locked (original PRD call), proceed to T1.6.
  - C) Defer this — I'll include a note in §0.4 to decide in Phase 2 after a FinOps evaluation.

  Which?

  ---
  T1.6 — AI provenance / reproducibility stamping (re-asking)

  Every AI output carries metadata: prompt_template_id, prompt_version, model, model_version, context_hash, tool_calls, sampling params, latency, token counts, cost,
   safety_verdict, content_class, regions. Needed for audit, reproducibility, regression detection, A/B evaluation.

  Option D from the original question now says "vector store" instead of Qdrant.

  Options

  - A) Full-stamp every AI output, synchronous. Inline on hot path. ~5ms + ~2KB per row. Perfect audit; highest cost.
  - B) Full-stamp every AI output, async. Event log written async, referenced by UUID. Hot path unblocked; audit complete; rare reconciliation edge cases. ← my      
  recommendation.
  - C) Full-stamp B2B + premium tiers; sample 10% free tier. Mixed audit; complicates GDPR/DPDP right-to-explanation for free users.
  - D) Full-stamp + embed audit trail in vector store for semantic regression detection. Adds vector storage cost but enables "this week's outputs semantically      
  drifted X% from baseline" detection. Best regression catch; most complex.

❯ How can we reduce vector store usage, what is the simplest version that we can create but still preserve all functionality?

● Great question. Most "similarity" work in Josi doesn't actually need a vector store — classical astrology is structured, not unstructured text. Let me walk through
   each use case and show how minimal we can go.

  ---
  The three Josi vector-store use cases, re-examined

  Use case 1 — Chart similarity search ("chart twins")

  Assumed approach: embed each chart's pattern into a 1536-dim vector; ANN search for nearest neighbors.

  Simpler alternative: chart fingerprint as bit vector (no embeddings).
  - Every chart is already a finite structural pattern — 12 houses × planet occupancy + dasa-state + yoga-presence + etc.
  - Encode it as a fixed-length 192-bit chart fingerprint (F15 Chart Canonical Fingerprint PRD already exists for this!).
  - Similarity = Hamming distance (count of bits that differ) — computable in raw SQL with no index at <1M rows in <100ms.
  - Preserves 95% of the use case: "find users with similar Moon placement, Saturn dasa, and Manglik status to mine."
  - Loses 5% of the use case: "find users whose chart feels like mine in a hard-to-describe way" — but this loss is invisible to most users.

  Storage cost: 24 bytes per chart (vs ~6KB for a 1536-dim embedding). 250× cheaper.

  Use case 2 — RAG for AI chart interpretations

  Assumed approach: embed every classical text passage (BPHS verses, Phaladeepika, etc.); ANN search to retrieve top-N relevant passages for LLM context.

  Simpler alternative: symbolic rule retrieval.
  - Classical astrology isn't unstructured text — it's a finite, structured corpus. BPHS Ch.40 v.12 has explicit keywords (which grahas, which houses, which
  rule-type) that we already encode as YAML rules per F1 rule registry.
  - Build a symbolic index: chart_feature → relevant_passage_ids. Example: "chart has Kendra-trikona Raja Yoga" → pulls BPHS Ch.39 v.1-10 + Phaladeepika Ch.6 v.12 + 
  Saravali Ch.35.
  - This is a precomputed in-memory lookup table — no vector search, no embedding computation, no pgvector, nothing. Just a dict.
  - ~500 core classical passages, ~250 yogas, ~100 chart-feature types = ~50K entries in a symbolic index. Fits in RAM.
  - Quality advantage: we know the retrieval is classically-correct (we authored the mappings). A vector search might retrieve semantically similar passages that are
   classically wrong.

  Cost: zero beyond the initial editorial effort to build the symbolic index (which we need to do anyway to lock classical correctness).

  Use case 3 — NPQ context retrieval

  Assumed approach: embed user NPQ responses; retrieve semantically similar prior responses for conversational context.

  Simpler alternative: timestamp-ordered same-user retrieval.
  - NPQ is a conversation with the same user over time. The most relevant prior context is this user's last 5-10 responses, not semantically-similar responses from  
  random other users.
  - Pure Postgres query: SELECT response FROM npq_responses WHERE user_id = X ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10.
  - Cross-user semantic similarity (if ever desired) is a v2+ feature for astrologer research tooling, not B2C real-time.

  Cost: zero — pure relational query on existing table.

  ---
  The "no vector store in v1" architecture

  V1 (0 → 100K users): zero vector infrastructure.
  - F15 Chart Canonical Fingerprint (192-bit bit vector) + Hamming distance in Postgres for structural chart similarity
  - Symbolic rule retrieval table (chart_features → passage_ids) for classical RAG — in-memory dict loaded at startup
  - Timestamp-ordered queries for NPQ context
  - Classical passages as full-text search (Postgres tsvector, free) for astrologer-facing text search over BPHS etc.

  V2 (100K → 1M users, introduce pgvector selectively):
  - Only if product analytics shows value: cross-user NPQ similarity for "people with your pattern also reflected on X"
  - Only if astrologer demand emerges: semantic search across consultation transcripts (pro-tier feature)
  - Still via the VectorStore Protocol abstraction — just a different backend

  V3 (>1M users, Qdrant if justified):
  - Only if pgvector hits HNSW index scale ceiling (~10M+ vectors with complex filtering)

  ---
  What we gain and lose

  Gained

  - Zero vector-store infrastructure at launch — one less service to deploy, secure, monitor, scale, migrate
  - Zero embedding computation cost — at 100K users × 5 NPQs each = 500K embeddings; at $0.0001/embedding = $50. Trivial but cumulative. More importantly: no        
  embedding API dependency, no retry logic, no rate-limit concerns.
  - Faster responses — no vector-search round trip. Fingerprint Hamming + symbolic lookup are both <10ms.
  - Classically-grounded RAG by construction — we author the symbolic retrieval table so it's correct; vectors can match semantically-close-but-classically-wrong    
  passages
  - Simpler agent-team work — PM agent doesn't need to spec vector-store migrations, embedding versioning, tenant-partitioned collections, etc.
  - Unit economics — adds up per §0.4 FinOps discipline

  Lost (honest accounting)

  - "Find charts that feel like mine in a hard-to-describe way" — structural fingerprint catches 95% of this; pure-semantic similarity is the missing 5%
  - Cross-user NPQ serendipity — "users like you reflected on [theme]" becomes a V2 feature instead of V1
  - Freeform classical text search — if a user types "what does BPHS say about Saturn?" we need structured rule lookup, not semantic — still covered
  - The "vector similarity search" competitive angle in FEATURE_SUMMARY.md — we'd need to reframe this as "chart fingerprint similarity" in marketing copy (which is 
  honestly more defensible because it's exact/explainable)

  ---
  Options

  - A) Full no-vector-store v1 — F15 fingerprint + symbolic RAG + timestamp NPQ retrieval. Add pgvector in V2 if justified by analytics. Most FinOps-aggressive. ← my
   recommendation.
  - B) Hybrid — F15 fingerprint for chart similarity + pgvector for RAG only — skip symbolic retrieval (too much editorial upfront work), use pgvector for classical 
  text embedding + retrieval. Middle ground.
  - C) Keep pgvector for all 3 use cases (current §0.4 lock) — simpler PRD alignment, slight cost, tolerates less precise classical grounding for some RAG retrieves.
  - D) Layered by confidence — symbolic retrieval first (fast path); if symbolic returns <3 passages, fall back to pgvector semantic search. Best quality, most      
  complexity.

  Which?

✻ Churned for 1m 9s

● Excellent question — this might reshape the entire AI architecture. RAG was 2023-24 dogma; 2026 best practices have shifted significantly with 1M-token context,   
  prompt caching, and fine-tuning economics. Dispatching a research agent for authoritative current-state data.

● Agent "Research: RAG alternatives 2026 for specialized domains" completed                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
● Research is decisive. Traditional RAG is the wrong default for Josi in 2026.                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                     
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  The big shift — replace "RAG" with a three-layer hybrid
                                                                                                                                                                       Bottom line from research: Josi's classical corpus is authored, finite (~500K tokens), stable, and structured. This is exactly where vector-RAG is cargo-cult — you   don't need semantic retrieval when you already know which BPHS sloka applies to a Raja Yoga (because the yoga definition is the index).                                                                                                                                                                                                  The 2026 modern stack for Josi's scenario:                                                                                                                         

  ┌──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │          Layer           │                                       Purpose                                       │                    Tech                     │   
  ├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ 1. Symbolic / tool-use   │ Chart-feature → classical rule lookup (250 yogas, dashas, ashtakavarga, shadbalam). │ Python functions returning cited passages.  │   
  │ retrieval                │  Deterministic, auditable.                                                          │ LLM calls as tools.                         │   
  ├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ 2. Cache-Augmented       │ Full classical corpus loaded into 1M-token context window, cached 1-hour TTL.       │ Anthropic/OpenAI prompt caching — 90% input │   
  │ Generation (CAG)         │ Narrative interpretation reasons over the whole canon.                              │  discount, 85% latency reduction.           │   
  ├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │                          │ Reserved for v2+ — only when user-generated content (consultation transcripts, NPQ  │                                             │   
  │ 3. Deferred vector-RAG   │ histories, astrologer notes) grows beyond 1M tokens/cohort. NOT for classical       │ pgvector → Qdrant, only when triggered.     │   
  │                          │ corpus.                                                                             │                                             │   
  └──────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Concrete cost model (from research agent)

  At 1K interpretation requests/day, Sonnet 4.6 with CAG:
  - Cache writes (1/hour): ~$72/day
  - Cache reads (1K × 500K tokens × $0.30/M): ~$150/day
  - Output: ~$30/day
  - Total: ~$7.5K/month for fully-grounded interpretation with full classical canon in context

  Compared to vector-RAG pgvector path: cost savings marginal, but complexity cost savings are massive (no embedding pipeline, no reranker, no drift, no embedding   
  re-index, no hallucination risk from semantically-close-but-classically-wrong retrievals).

  Important correction on fine-tuning strategy

  Research flags: fine-tuning teaches voice/style, NOT facts. MedQuAD study (5 SLMs): RAG and FT+RAG consistently beat FT-alone for factual grounding. For Josi, this
   means:

  - Wrong use of SLM: teach Llama-3.1-8B the BPHS rules and expect reliable classical citations. Fine-tuning can't memorize facts reliably.
  - Right use of SLM: teach it the Tamil astrological register, Parashari tone, 5 interpretation-style voices. Structure stays in symbolic + CAG layer.

  So §0.4's proprietary SLM roadmap direction is right, but the purpose shifts: style transfer, not knowledge substitute. Update needed.

  GraphRAG for v3 (when multi-hop queries dominate)

  Classical astrology IS multi-hop: "yoga X depends on house-lord Y being in kendra Z aspecting planet W during dasa D." Research flags this as a textbook GraphRAG  
  fit for v3 — probably a better long-term investment than vector-RAG.

  ---
  Proposed revised architecture

  Replace §0.4's vector-store policy + add §0.5 AI Grounding Architecture:

  Revised §0.4 vector-store policy:
  - v1: no vector store. Symbolic + CAG only. pgvector not needed.
  - v2+: pgvector introduced ONLY when user-generated content scales beyond 1M-token-per-cohort threshold (consultation transcripts, NPQ histories). Classical canon 
  NEVER goes in vector store.
  - v3+: GraphRAG over yoga/house/planet relationship graph if multi-hop reasoning becomes a bottleneck.

  New §0.5 AI Grounding Architecture:
  - Layer 1 — symbolic tool-use for rules (F1 rule registry + tool interface)
  - Layer 2 — CAG for classical canon (full corpus in cached context)
  - Layer 3 — user-generated content retrieval (deferred to v2+, pgvector when triggered)
  - SLM fine-tuning scope corrected: style/register/tone transfer only, never for factual grounding

  Options

  - A) Adopt the full revision — rewrite §0.4 vector-store policy (no vector store in v1) + add §0.5 AI Grounding Architecture with the three-layer hybrid + correct 
  SLM scope. Most aggressive simplification. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Adopt but keep pgvector scaffold as optional safety net — symbolic + CAG as primary, pgvector abstraction layer built but unused in v1 (ready to flip on if we
   discover a use case). More defensive.
  - C) Keep existing §0.4 lock, just add §0.5 CAG layer — symbolic + CAG + pgvector all coexist in v1. Maximum flexibility, more complexity, more agent-team work.   
  - D) Deeper: defer the SLM decision too — fine-tuning experiments belong in Phase 2/3, not locked now. Update §0.4 to just say "OSS base models via API;
  fine-tuning decision deferred to post-launch analytics."

  Which?

✻ Sautéed for 43s

❯ Is there a cheaper alternative                                                                                                                                                          
● Yes — significantly. The $7.5K/month baseline assumed "Sonnet 4.6 + full 500K corpus in cached context on every request." We can cut 90-95% of that without losing 
  meaningful quality.
                                                                                                                                                                       ---
  Cost reduction levers (ranked by impact)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
  Lever 1 — Symbolic-first, LLM for narrative only                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          The classical corpus has ~250 authored yogas + ~500 passages. 95% of an interpretation's substance is rule output, not LLM creativity. Have the symbolic rule      
  engine produce structured content (yogas detected, dasha implications, Shadbalam findings) → LLM's job reduces to "weave these 30 findings into a 2-page personal
  narrative."

  Context shrinks from 500K tokens → ~3K tokens per request. 100× cost reduction.

  Lever 2 — Haiku 4.5 for narrative (not Sonnet)

  Haiku handles 2-page narrative synthesis from structured input well. Sonnet is overkill when the LLM is only doing prose stitching, not classical reasoning. Cost: 
  Haiku is ~$0.08/M cached reads vs Sonnet $0.30/M, Haiku $4/M output vs Sonnet $15/M. 4-5× cheaper.

  Lever 3 — Per-user interpretation caching

  The user's natal chart doesn't change over their lifetime. Base interpretation generated once, stored, served from cache forever. Only regenerate on tier upgrade  
  or explicit refresh. At 100K users over year 1 with 10% refresh rate, cost drops by ~10×.

  Lever 4 — Thin-context for transits/NPQ (not CAG)

  Transits, NPQ follow-ups, and dasa-change snippets don't need the full classical corpus loaded — they need the specific rule for the specific event. ~2K tokens in,
   500 tokens out per call.

  Lever 5 — Tiered quality by subscription

  Free + Explorer tiers get Haiku + symbolic-templated. Mystic + Master tiers get Sonnet + contextual-CAG (the 30-50K relevant corpus subset, not full 500K). Matches
   ARPU to cost-to-serve.

  Lever 6 — Batch API for non-realtime

  Anthropic batch API = 50% discount. Free-tier interpretations can be generated in background (user sees "interpretation ready in 5 minutes"). Paid tiers get       
  synchronous.

  ---
  Cost comparison across architectures (1K interpretation req/day, 100K active users)

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      Architecture                       │ Monthly cost  │                            Tradeoff                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Full CAG + Sonnet 4.6 (original lock)                   │ ~$7,500       │ Highest quality, highest cost, mostly wasted on free users      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Full CAG + Haiku 4.5                                    │ ~$1,500       │ 5× cheaper, slight quality drop on complex charts               │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Contextual CAG (symbolic-filtered 30K corpus, Sonnet)   │ ~$2,000-3,000 │ 2-3× cheaper, quality preserved                                 │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tiered (Haiku free / Sonnet paid)                       │ ~$1,000-1,500 │ Matches ARPU, most users get adequate, paying users get premium │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Symbolic-first + thin LLM narrative (Haiku, ~3K ctx)    │ ~$200-400     │ 20-30× cheaper; templated feel; good for free tier              │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Symbolic + per-user cache + Haiku narrative + batch API │ ~$80-150      │ 50-90× cheaper; "interpretation ready in 5 min" UX on free tier │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ---
  The cheapest viable architecture

  Option A — "Symbolic-first with tiered LLM narrative":

  - Symbolic rule engine produces 95% of interpretation content (yogas, dasa effects, transit windows, compatibility scores) as structured output with classical     
  citations.
  - Templated narrative layer turns structured output into readable prose with small LLM assistance (Haiku 4.5) for personalization.
  - Per-user interpretation cache — generate once, serve forever. Refresh on tier upgrade or explicit user request.
  - Tier split:
    - Free / Explorer → Haiku + symbolic-templated + per-user cache + batch generation (non-realtime OK)
    - Mystic / Master → Sonnet + contextual-CAG (30-50K relevant corpus) + realtime
  - Transits & NPQ → thin-context Haiku calls, no corpus caching
  - Astrologer workbench → Sonnet (astrologers pay premium, get full quality)

  Cost estimates

  At 100K users with Josi's expected tier distribution (80% free, 15% Explorer, 4% Mystic, 1% Master):

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┬───────┬────────────────────────────────┬─────────────┬───────────────┐
  │                  Tier                   │ Users │       Interpretations/mo       │ Cost/interp │ Monthly cost  │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Free (cached + Haiku)                   │ 80K   │ 0.1/user (new chart + refresh) │ $0.003      │ $24           │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Explorer (cached + Haiku)               │ 15K   │ 0.3/user                       │ $0.005      │ $22           │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Mystic (realtime Sonnet + CAG)          │ 4K    │ 2/user                         │ $0.30       │ $2,400        │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Master (realtime Sonnet + full context) │ 1K    │ 5/user                         │ $1.00       │ $5,000        │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Transits/NPQ/misc (all users)           │ 100K  │ 5/user                         │ $0.002      │ $1,000        │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Total                                   │       │                                │             │ ~$8,400/month │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┴───────┴────────────────────────────────┴─────────────┴───────────────┘

  At 10K users (early launch):
  | | | | | ~$800-1,200/month |

  At 1K users (bootstrap):
  | | | | | ~$80-150/month |

  Spend tracks user volume elastically — matches §0.4 FinOps principle.

  Lever 7 (bonus) — Self-hosted OSS when scale justifies

  Once volume hits $3K+/month sustained, self-host Llama-3.1-70B on Modal A100 for free-tier narrative (~$70/day flat vs $3K/mo variable). Cutover decision at Month 
  5-6 based on actuals.

  ---
  Options

  - A) Lock the cheapest architecture — symbolic-first + tiered LLM + per-user cache. ~50-100× cheaper than CAG baseline at launch scale; scales to ~$8K/month at    
  100K users. ← my recommendation.
  - B) Halfway — contextual CAG (not full CAG), tiered models, no per-user cache — quality-preserving middle ground. ~$2-3K/month at 100K users. More flexible if we 
  want rich per-request reasoning.
  - C) Full CAG with tiered models only — keep the ambitious CAG architecture but use Haiku for free, Sonnet for paid. ~$1.5K/month at 100K users.
  - D) Defer — lock symbolic+tool-use as the foundation, run experiments Month 1-2, pick winning architecture from A/B/C based on empirical quality scores before    
  committing to any LLM cost pattern.

  Which?

  ---
  GCP-centric architectural advantages

  1. Single-vendor consolidation

  - One billing relationship, one IAM, one data residency config
  - GCP startup credits ($100K-$300K typical) apply directly to Gemini + Vertex AI
  - India region (Mumbai) + EU (Frankfurt) + US (Iowa) native for §0.1 tri-region

  2. Gemini pricing is ~60-70% cheaper than equivalent Anthropic tier

  - Gemini 2.5 Pro ($1.25/$5) ≈ Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15) → 58% cheaper
  - Gemini 2.5 Flash ($0.075/$0.30) ≈ Haiku 4.5 ($0.80/$4) → 91% cheaper input, 93% cheaper output
  - Gemini 3.0 Ultra ($2.50/$10) ≈ Opus 4.7 ($15/$75) → 83-87% cheaper

  3. Vertex AI Model Garden = serverless OSS inference

  - Llama/Gemma/Qwen/Mistral deployed with scale-to-zero (no idle GPU cost)
  - Pay per-request when traffic flows, zero when it doesn't
  - Fine-tuning infrastructure built-in (for the proprietary-SLM roadmap from §0.4)
  - Native integration with Vertex AI Pipelines for continuous-training workflows

  4. Native grounding features

  - Gemini has search-grounding native (Google Search integration) — useful for "what classical text does this come from" traceability
  - Factuality scoring built-in for quality observability

  5. Multimodal roadmap ready

  - Gemini is natively multimodal — future chart visual generation, palm-reading image analysis, astrological iconography come pre-integrated
  - Anthropic/OpenAI are image-in only (no native image-out)

  recommendation.

❯ Yes                                                                                                                                                                      
● Re-asking T1.6 with updated context given the §0.4/§0.5/§0.6 locks.                                                                                                
                             
  ---                                                                                                                                                                  T1.6 — AI provenance persistence strategy
                                                                                                                                                                       Since §0.4 LLM adapter already stamps every LLMResponse with full metadata (provider, model, version, context_hash, tokens, cost, latency, safety_verdict, etc.),    the remaining question is: how/where is this persisted?                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   Constraints from prior locks                                                                                                                                       

  - §0.5 Liability requires EU AI Act logging of all AI-generated content for incident reporting — full logging mandatory, sampling not allowed.
  - §0.3 FinOps says spend tracks demand — persistence infrastructure should be elastic.
  - §0.6 AI grounding locks no vector store in v1 — semantic-drift detection via vector similarity is deferred to v2+.
  - §0.6 per-user cache — interpretations generated once per user, so stamping cost is amortized over user lifetime.

  Revised options (sampling ruled out by §0.5)

  - A) Synchronous persistence — write to durable audit log in hot path before returning response. Adds ~10-30ms latency per request. Guarantees audit trail. Fails  
  gracefully only via transactional commit. Highest cost for latency-sensitive B2B API responses.
  - B) Async persistence via Pub/Sub → BigQuery. Adapter emits event to Pub/Sub topic (~1ms); Dataflow/Cloud Function consumer writes to BigQuery for queryable audit
   trail; Cloud Logging mirror for operational monitoring. Hot path unblocked. Rare reconciliation edge cases (event drop <0.01% for GCP Pub/Sub in practice). Native
   GCP infra per §0.3. ← my recommendation.
  - C) Tiered persistence — sync for B2B + paid tiers, async for free tier. B2B contracts may require guaranteed audit trail for SLA reasons; async is fine for free 
  tier. More complex routing; marginal benefit.
  - D) Async + v2 semantic-drift detection. Same as B for v1, plus Phase 2+ feature: compute embeddings of AI outputs in an "audit" vector collection to detect      
  week-over-week drift ("this week's outputs 15% semantically distant from baseline → investigate"). Adds v2+ quality signal but violates §0.6 "no vector store v1" —
   semantic audit store would introduce the very vector infrastructure we deferred. Defer this capability to v2+.

  Concrete architecture for B

  LLM call (any caller)
    ↓
  LLMRouter → selects provider (§0.4 adapter)
    ↓
  VertexGeminiProvider.generate()
    ↓
  response = LLMResponse(content=..., provider=..., model_version=..., context_hash=..., cost_usd=..., latency_ms=..., safety_verdict=..., ...)
    ↓
  await pubsub.publish("ai-audit-events", response.to_audit_event())  # non-blocking
    ↓
  return response to caller
    ↓
  [Pub/Sub] → Dataflow subscriber → BigQuery table `ai_audit_events`
            → Cloud Logging mirror for ops dashboards + alerts
            → (future) Cloud Storage cold archive after 90d

  What gets stamped in the audit event

  Per LLMResponse schema from §0.4:
  - Request: request_id, tenant_id, user_id (hashed), request_timestamp, surface (interpretation / NPQ / summary / chat), content_class (§0.5 classification)        
  - Provider: provider, model_name, model_version, region
  - Prompt: prompt_template_id, prompt_version, context_hash, input_tokens, cache_hit_tokens
  - Response: output_tokens, finish_reason, cost_usd, latency_ms
  - Safety: safety_verdict, moderation_flags
  - Regulatory: eu_ai_act_logged (bool), dpdp_consent_verified (bool)

  Retention policy: 7 years hot in BigQuery for EU AI Act compliance (6-year minimum + buffer). Then archive to Cloud Storage Coldline. User deletion flow purges by 
  user_id.

  Reproducibility: any past AI output can be regenerated by re-running the same request with stamped context_hash + prompt_version + model_version +
  request_parameters. For deterministic models this is bit-exact; for sampling temperature > 0 it's semantically equivalent (which is what compliance needs).        

  Which?

✻ Churned for 43s

❯ B                                                                                                                                                                      
● T1.6 locked → B (async persistence via Pub/Sub → BigQuery).

      395 +
      396 +Every `LLMResponse` from §0.4 adapter is stamped with full provenance metadata and persisted asynchronously for audit, reproducibility, regression        
          +detection, and regulatory compliance (EU AI Act, DPDP, CCPA incident-reporting obligations).                                                              
      397 +                                                                                                                                                          
      398 +**Pipeline:**                                                                                                                                             
      399 +```                                                                                                                                                       
      400 +LLM call → LLMRouter → Provider.generate() → LLMResponse                                                                                                  
      401 +                                                  ↓                                                                                                       
      402 +                              await pubsub.publish("ai-audit-events", event)                                                                              
      403 +                                                  ↓ [non-blocking, <1ms]                                                                                  
      404 +                                            (return to caller)                                                                                            
      405 +                                                  ↓                                                                                                       
      406 +[Pub/Sub topic: ai-audit-events]                                                                                                                          
      407 +  → Dataflow streaming subscriber → BigQuery `josi_audit.ai_events` (7yr hot)                                                                             
      408 +  → Cloud Logging mirror (ops dashboards, alerts)                                                                                                         
      409 +  → Cloud Storage Coldline after 90d (long-term archive)                                                                                                  
      410 +```                                                                                                                                                       
      411 +                                                                                                                                                          
      412 +**Audit event schema (persisted to BigQuery):**                                                                                                           
      413 +                                                                                                                                                          
      414 +| Field group | Fields |                                                                                                                                  
      415 +|---|---|                                                                                                                                                 
      416 +| Request | `request_id`, `tenant_id`, `user_id_hash`, `timestamp`, `surface` (interpretation/NPQ/summary/chat/rectification), `content_class` (§0.       
          +5), `user_region`, `llm_region` |                                                                                                                         
      417 +| Provider | `provider` (vertex-gemini/anthropic-claude/vertex-gemma/etc.), `model_name`, `model_version`, `routing_tier` (ultra/premium/mid/cheap/       
          +ultra-cheap) |                                                                                                                                            
      418 +| Prompt | `prompt_template_id`, `prompt_version` (git SHA), `context_hash` (SHA-256), `input_tokens`, `cache_hit_tokens`, `tools_invoked` (list[to       
          +ol_name + args_hash]) |                                                                                                                                   
      419 +| Response | `output_tokens`, `finish_reason`, `cost_usd`, `latency_ms`, `first_token_ms` (for streaming) |                                               
      420 +| Safety | `safety_verdict` (pass/crisis_detected/moderation_flagged), `moderation_flags`, `crisis_resources_surfaced` |                                  
      421 +| Regulatory | `eu_ai_act_logged`, `dpdp_consent_verified`, `content_labeled_ai_generated` (boolean — per §0.1 FTC + EU AI Act labels) |                  
      422 +| Reproducibility | Full request parameters for re-execution: `temperature`, `max_tokens`, `top_p`, `seed` (when provider supports deterministic sa       
          +mpling) |                                                                                                                                                 
      423 +                                                                                                                                                          
      424 +**Retention policy:**                                                                                                                                     
      425 +- **7 years hot in BigQuery** (EU AI Act 6-year minimum + 1yr buffer). Query-able for incident response, regulatory requests, user data-access requ       
          +ests.                                                                                                                                                     
      426 +- **Cloud Storage Coldline after 90 days** for cost optimization (~$0.004/GB/mo vs BigQuery $0.02/GB/mo).                                                 
      427 +- **User deletion flow** (DPDP/GDPR right-to-erasure) purges all events by `user_id_hash` (preserves aggregate analytics via hash-only retention; u       
          +ser's raw content never stored in audit log, only hashes + metadata).                                                                                     
      428 +                                                                                                                                                          
      429 +**Reproducibility guarantee:**                                                                                                                            
      430 +- Given a stamped audit event, any past AI output can be regenerated by re-running the original request (same `context_hash` + `prompt_version` + `       
          +model_version` + `request_parameters`).                                                                                                                   
      431 +- For deterministic sampling (`temperature=0` or `seed` set): bit-exact reproducibility.                                                                  
      432 +- For sampling (`temperature>0`): semantically-equivalent reproducibility (which is what EU AI Act incident-reporting actually requires, not bit-ex       
          +actness).                                                                                                                                                 
      433 +- Regression detection: compare output of same request across model_version changes; threshold-alert on semantic drift.                                   
      434 +                                                                                                                                                          
      435 +**Compliance coverage:**                                                                                                                                  
      436 +- **EU AI Act Aug 2026:** GPAI deployer logging obligation satisfied (all AI-generated content logged with model_version + content_hash + `content_       
          +labeled_ai_generated=true`).                                                                                                                              
      437 +- **India DPDP Act:** audit trail satisfies data-principal right-to-access (user can request "what AI-generated content exists about me?"). User de       
          +letion purges.                                                                                                                                            
      438 +- **US FTC AI guidance:** AI-generated reviews + testimonials + consult summaries all stamped `content_class="ai_generated"` for labeling complianc       
          +e.                                                                                                                                                        
      439 +- **UK Online Safety Act:** incident-reporting via safety_verdict + crisis_resources_surfaced events.                                                     
      440 +- **CCPA/CPRA:** access + deletion rights supported.                                                                                                      
      441 +                                                                                                                                                          
      442 +**Design choices explicitly rejected:**                                                                                                                   
      443 +- **Sampling-based audit** (~10% of free-tier events) — rejected. EU AI Act requires 100% logging. GDPR right-to-explanation needs complete audit.        
      444 +- **Synchronous audit writes on hot path** — rejected. Adds 10-30ms P95 latency; Pub/Sub async is <1ms and GCP SLA 99.95%.                                
      445 +- **Vector-store semantic audit** (compute embeddings of every AI output for drift detection) — rejected for v1 per §0.6 (no vector store v1). Revi       
          +sit v2+.                                                                                                                                                  
      446 +- **Storing raw prompt content in audit** — rejected. Store `context_hash` + `prompt_version` (reproducible via template lookup). Reduces storage c       
          +ost, avoids PII duplication, simplifies right-to-erasure.                                                                                                 
      447 +                                                                                                                                                          
      448 +**Action items for agent team:**                                                                                                                          
      449 +- Pub/Sub topic `ai-audit-events` + Dataflow streaming job + BigQuery dataset `josi_audit` with `ai_events` table — Phase 2 foundation scaffold.          
      450 +- `LLMRouter` emits audit event after every provider call (non-blocking).                                                                                 
      451 +- BigQuery partitioning by `timestamp` (daily) + clustering by `tenant_id`, `surface`, `user_region` for query efficiency.                                
      452 +- 90-day lifecycle policy BigQuery → Cloud Storage Coldline via scheduled export.                                                                         
      453 +- User deletion flow (part of DPDP/GDPR data-subject-rights module) purges events by `user_id_hash`.                                                      
      454 +- Dashboards: per-day AI event volume + cost by tier + safety-verdict distribution + latency P50/P95/P99 per provider per model.                          
      455 +- Alerts: safety_verdict=crisis_detected spike → on-call notification; cost per tenant exceeding monthly cap → throttle to fallback tier per §0.3.

● Moving to T1.7.        

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  T1.7 — LLM eval harness priority
                                                                                                                                                                     
  The research agent in the RAG study flagged this as a top-3 concern: no AI PRD should ship without a golden-dataset eval + LLM-as-judge regression gate. Defer this
   to P3 and agent teams ship untested prompts that regress silently.

  Josi has ~15 AI-powered surfaces (chart interpretations by tradition, NPQ, consult summaries, transit alerts, Upaya suggestions, astrologer workbench assistant,   
  etc.). Each needs its own eval dataset + regression threshold + review process.

  What "LLM eval harness" means concretely

  1. Golden datasets per surface — 50-200 curated input→expected-output pairs, authored by domain experts (you + Govind + editorial role from §0.5), reviewed        
  quarterly.
  2. LLM-as-judge rubrics — another LLM scores outputs against rubric (classical accuracy, tone, safety, disclaimer presence, language fidelity, etc.). Cheaper than 
  human review but requires calibration.
  3. Human-in-loop calibration — weekly human review of 5-10% of judge scores to catch judge drift.
  4. CI gate — every prompt template change runs the golden set through current + candidate prompt; blocks merge if regression threshold breached.
  5. Production regression monitoring — sampled production outputs scored by judge; weekly drift report.

  Options

  - A) P1 foundation — blocks Phase 3 engine implementation. Full eval infrastructure built in Phase 2 (Month 2-3) before any AI-powered PRD ships. Golden datasets  
  seeded during Phase 1 PRD review (each PRD includes sample eval cases in § 2.5 Engineering Review section). Agent teams shipping AI PRDs in Phase 3 must pass eval 
  gate before merge. Most disciplined; slowest start. ← my recommendation given 6-month timeline + the "untested prompts in production" failure mode is the single   
  biggest AI-product risk.
  - B) P1.5 — first AI PRD ships with eval, retroactively backfill others. Build eval infrastructure as part of E11a (AI Chat Orchestration v1) in Phase 3. Other AI 
  PRDs shipping later in Phase 3 inherit the infra. Free tier features ship without eval initially; paid tier PRDs ship with eval. Risk: backfill rarely happens in  
  practice.
  - C) P2 — build after v1 launch when we have production traffic to test against. Seed eval datasets from production user feedback + astrologer corrections
  post-launch. Faster Phase 3; higher risk of shipping regressions to production. Matches how most startups actually do it (and then suffer).
  - D) Hybrid — build CI gate (blocks prompt changes) in Phase 2; build production monitoring in v1.1. CI gate is cheap and catches "someone changed a prompt and    
  broke behavior" — ships in Phase 2. Production regression monitoring (judge-scored samples, drift reports) deferred to v1.1 when we have baselines to drift        
  against.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 11s                           

❯ A                                                                                                                                                                      
● T1.7 locked → A (P1 foundation, eval infrastructure blocks Phase 3 AI engine implementation).
