      456                                                                                                                                                                  457 +### 0.8 LLM eval harness 🔒 (Option A — P1 foundation, blocks Phase 3 AI PRDs, locked 2026-04-23)                                                               458 +                                                                                                                                                          
      459 +**Core principle:** no AI-powered PRD ships in Phase 3 without a passing eval gate. Eval infrastructure is a Phase 2 foundation deliverable, not a 
          +post-launch bolt-on. Ungated prompt changes silently regress production quality — this is the single most common way AI products fail.                    
      460 +                                                                                                                                                          
      461 +**Four-layer eval architecture:**                                                                                                                         
      462 +                                                                                                                                                          
      463 +### Layer 1 — Golden datasets per AI surface (authored during Phase 1)                                                                                    
      464 +                                                                                                                                                          
      465 +Every AI-powered PRD has a golden dataset of 50-200 curated `(input, expected_output, rubric_criteria)` tuples authored by domain experts during Ph       
          +ase 1 PRD review. Each PRD's `§ 2.5 Engineering Review` section includes seed eval cases + expansion targets.                                             
      466 +                                                                                                                                                          
      467 +**AI surfaces requiring golden datasets (~15):**                                                                                                          
      468 +- Chart interpretations per tradition (Vedic Parashari, Vedic Jaimini, Western, Tajaka, KP) — 100+ cases each                                             
      469 +- Neural Pathway Questions (NPQ) — 50+ response-quality cases, 30+ crisis-signal cases                                                                    
      470 +- Post-consultation summaries — 50+ cases covering edge scenarios                                                                                         
      471 +- Transit alerts + dasa-change notifications — 30+ cases                                                                                                  
      472 +- Upaya / remedy suggestions (E28) — 80+ cases covering all regional variants                                                                             
      473 +- Astrologer workbench assistant queries — 60+ cases                                                                                                      
      474 +- Prasna / horary queries (E10) — 40+ cases                                                                                                               
      475 +- Compatibility / Ashtakoota / Thirumana Porutham interpretations (E25, E29) — 50+ cases                                                                  
      476 +- Chart rectification confidence explanations (E17) — 30+ cases                                                                                           
      477 +- Foreign-language output (when v1.1+ activates) — 20+ cases per language                                                                                 
      478 +                                                                                                                                                          
      479 +**Authored by:** cpselvam (classical accuracy) + editorial-role (cultural/language) + Govind (engineering feasibility). Each case cites the `DECISI       
          +ONS.md` or `ARCHITECTURE_DECISIONS.md` lock it validates.                                                                                                 
      480 +                                                                                                                                                          
      481 +### Layer 2 — LLM-as-judge rubrics (automated scoring)                                                                                                    
      482 +                                                                                                                                                          
      483 +Each AI output scored against surface-specific rubric by an LLM-judge (Gemini 2.5 Pro, separate from production inference to avoid self-grading bia       
          +s):                                                                                                                                                       
      484 +                                                                                                                                                          
      485 +| Rubric dimension | Example: chart interpretation | Score |                                                                                              
      486 +|---|---|---|                                                                                                                                             
      487 +| **Classical accuracy** | Does interpretation cite correct yoga? Correct classical source? | 0-10 |                                                      
      488 +| **Grounding fidelity** | Does LLM output align with symbolic rule engine tool outputs (§0.6 Layer 1)? | 0-10 |                                          
      489 +| **Disclaimer compliance** | Subjunctive language per §0.5 ("may suggest" not "will cause")? | Pass/Fail |                                               
      490 +| **Safety posture** | No crisis-adjacent content without classifier routing per §0.5? | Pass/Fail |                                                      
      491 +| **Content class label** | Matches expected `content_class` from §0.5? | Pass/Fail |                                                                     
      492 +| **Language fidelity** | Tamil/IAST/English per §1.5 locked conventions? | 0-10 |                                                                        
      493 +| **Length discipline** | Within target output length per surface? | Pass/Fail |                                                                          
      494 +| **Personalization** | References user's specific chart placements (not generic)? | 0-10 |                                                               
      495 +                                                                                                                                                          
      496 +**Composite score** = weighted sum; threshold per surface (typically ≥7.5/10 composite for production promotion).                                         
      497 +                                                                                                                                                          
      498 +### Layer 3 — Human-in-loop calibration (weekly cadence)                                                                                                  
      499 +                                                                                                                                                          
      500 +- 5-10% of judge scores reviewed weekly by editorial-role + cpselvam.                                                                                     
      501 +- Disagreements logged as judge-calibration-events.                                                                                                       
      502 +- Judge rubric refined quarterly based on disagreement patterns.                                                                                          
      503 +- **Why critical:** LLM judges drift. Without human calibration, you trust a biased judge and don't know it.                                              
      504 +                                                                                                                                                          
      505 +### Layer 4 — CI gate + production regression monitoring                                                                                                  
      506 +                                                                                                                                                          
      507 +**CI gate (blocks merge):**                                                                                                                               
      508 +- Every prompt template change in PR triggers: candidate_prompt vs current_prompt run on golden dataset.                                                  
      509 +- Merge blocked if composite score regresses >3% OR any Pass/Fail criterion fails.                                                                        
      510 +- Required approval from editorial-role for prompts affecting user-facing copy (per §0.5 disclaimer discipline).                                          
      511 +                                                                                                                                                          
      512 +**Production regression monitoring:**                                                                                                                     
      513 +- 1% of production LLM outputs sampled daily, scored by judge, stored in BigQuery `josi_audit.eval_scores` (same infrastructure as §0.7).                 
      514 +- Weekly drift report: composite-score-by-surface trend over 30d; alert if any surface drops >5% week-over-week.                                          
      515 +- Monthly cross-provider A/B: same request to Gemini 2.5 Pro vs fallback; compare scores; informs routing decisions.                                      
      516 +                                                                                                                                                          
      517 +### Phase 2 deliverables (blocks Phase 3 AI PRD kickoff)                                                                                                  
      518 +                                                                                                                                                          
      519 +1. Golden-dataset authoring framework + schema in repository.                                                                                             
      520 +2. `pytest-eval` harness: run PRD's golden set against current prompt template, produce score report.                                                     
      521 +3. LLM-judge service using Gemini 2.5 Pro with surface-specific rubrics.                                                                                  
      522 +4. CI workflow step: eval gate on prompt-template PRs.                                                                                                    
      523 +5. BigQuery eval tables + daily scoring job.                                                                                                              
      524 +6. Dashboards: score-by-surface, drift-detection alerts.                                                                                                  
      525 +7. Editorial review workflow: judge-disagreement queue for weekly human calibration.                                                                      
      526 +                                                                                                                                                          
      527 +### Gate for Phase 3 AI PRD implementation                                                                                                                
      528 +                                                                                                                                                          
      529 +Agent team CANNOT start implementing an AI-powered PRD until:                                                                                             
      530 +- [ ] Golden dataset exists (≥50 cases minimum) for the PRD's surface                                                                                     
      531 +- [ ] Judge rubric defined + calibrated (at least one human-calibration round completed)                                                                  
      532 +- [ ] CI eval gate operational                                                                                                                            
      533 +- [ ] Surface has composite-score threshold defined in PRD's `§ 2.5 Engineering Review`                                                                   
      534 +                                                                                                                                                          
      535 +PM agent enforces this gate in Phase 3 orchestration.                                                                                                     
      536 +                                                                                                                                                          
      537 +### Cost of eval infrastructure                                                                                                                           
      538 +                                                                                                                                                          
      539 +- **Golden-dataset authoring:** ~20-40 hours of cpselvam + editorial-role time during Phase 1 (spread across PRD reviews — already in the 2-month P       
          +ass 2 budget per §0.2).                                                                                                                                   
      540 +- **Judge infrastructure ops:** ~$200-500/mo at 100K users (1% sampling × Gemini 2.5 Pro judge calls).                                                    
      541 +- **Human calibration:** 1-2 hours/week editorial-role time ongoing.                                                                                      
      542 +- **ROI:** catches ~80% of regression bugs before production; saves 10x in incident response + user trust damage.                                         
      543 +                                                                                                                                                          
      544 +### Alternatives explicitly rejected                                                                                                                      
      545 +                                                                                                                                                          
      546 +- **Ship-first-eval-later (Option C)** — rejected. Untested prompts in production is the #1 AI product failure mode; worth the 4-6 week Phase 2 inv       
          +estment.                                                                                                                                                  
      547 +- **Eval only for paid tiers (not free)** — rejected. Free tier users are our trust-building front-line; regressions there damage brand + conversio       
          +n funnel more than paid-tier regressions.                                                                                                                 
      548 +- **Manual-only eval (no LLM judge)** — rejected. Doesn't scale beyond 2-3 surfaces; we have 15.                                                          
      549 +- **LLM judge without human calibration** — rejected. Judge drift is real; human calibration catches it.                                                  
      550 +                                                                                                                                                          
      551 +**Action items for agent team:**

          +old.                                                                                                                                                      
      554 +- Phase 3 kickoff gate: cpselvam + Govind verify all AI PRDs have passing eval gates before Phase 3 starts.                                               
      555 +- Phase 4 launch gate: 30d of production regression data + no surface below threshold + all safety rubrics at 100% Pass for 7d consecutive.               
      556 +- Editorial-role hired / assigned by end of Month 2 (blocks calibration work).

● Moving to T1.8.     

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  T1.8 — Audit-trail scope for calculations (non-AI)
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Different from §0.7 (which covers AI outputs). T1.8 asks: how much of the classical calculation path gets audit-logged? Every chart calc runs through ~250 yogas +
  16 vargas + Shadbalam 6 + Ashtakavargam pipeline + Dasa computation + etc. Full audit means logging which rule fired with what inputs and what output; minimal
  means logging only final chart results.

  Constraints from prior locks

  - §0.5 Wellness positioning — regulatory pressure is AI-side (EU AI Act, FTC labeling). Calculation audit is not strictly required by law.
  - §0.3 FinOps — audit log storage costs compound; full audit at 100K users × ~500 rule evaluations per chart = meaningful BigQuery spend.
  - §0.6 symbolic rule engine — rules are deterministic Python functions; inputs + rule_version fully reproduce outputs. Less need for exhaustive logging than LLM   
  outputs.
  - F4 Audit Log PRD exists in the tracker as a foundation-layer PRD. T1.8 locks its scope.

  Options

  - A) Full-calc audit — every rule evaluation logged. ~500 rule evaluations/chart × 100K daily chart calcs = 50M audit rows/day. Storage: ~$200-500/mo BigQuery at  
  launch scale, $2-5K at 1M users. Perfect reproducibility; powerful debugging. Highest cost.
  - B) Rule-firing audit only — log which rules fired + their outputs, not the full per-rule eval trace. ~30-50 rules actually fire per chart (most don't match).    
  ~3-5M rows/day at launch; ~$20-50/mo BigQuery. Sufficient for reproducibility since rule-inputs are derivable from chart + rule_version. ← my recommendation.      
  - C) Chart-level audit — log final chart output + rule_registry_version only. ~100K rows/day. Cheap; but to debug a wrong yoga detection later you can't trace     
  which rule returned what — have to re-run locally with the user's chart + that registry version.
  - D) Tiered — full audit for astrologer workbench + paid tier charts; rule-firing only for free tier. Astrologers need per-rule trace for their work; free-tier    
  users don't. Complicates schema + queries; questionable value given re-derivation is trivial from chart + version.

  What "rule-firing audit" captures concretely

  For each chart calculation:
  - chart_id, tenant_id, user_id_hash, timestamp
  - input_hash (chart inputs: birth data + location + ayanamsa + house_system + etc.)
  - rule_registry_version (git SHA of the rule registry at calc time)
  - List of rule firings: [{rule_id, rule_version, inputs_hash, output_summary, execution_ms}]
  - Final chart output hash for integrity verification

  Reproducibility: given input_hash + rule_registry_version, replay is deterministic — same output always.

  Which?

✻ Cooked for 1m 27s                       

❯ Can you tell me how much audit defense does this product really need? Be honest, given the compliance we are following and regulations we are following, and also    how to make it ethical and moral so that if somebody asks why we gave a response, how can we provide that explanation. Also if any lawsuits are filed, how can we    provide information for speedy retrieval of information.                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                     
● Let me be honest — the prior options over-scope this. The actual audit defense required is less than it seems.                                                     

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  The honest answer — what Josi actually needs
                                                                                                                                                                       Regulatory minimums (the hard legal floor)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                Walking through every regulation in the §0.1 compliance stack and what it actually requires for classical-calculation audit:                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  ┌────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         Regulation         │                        What it requires                        │             Does it mandate per-calculation audit?             │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ EU AI Act (Aug 2026)       │ AI-generated content logging (model, version, content hash);   │ No — covers AI outputs, not classical math. §0.7 already       │   
  │                            │ incident reporting                                             │ handles this.                                                  │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ India DPDP Act 2023/2025   │ Consent records, data-processing log, access/erasure execution │ No — covers personal-data handling, not calculation internals  │   
  │                            │  logs                                                          │                                                                │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ US CCPA/CPRA               │ Disclosure, access, deletion rights                            │ No — data rights, not algorithm audit                          │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ UK Online Safety Act       │ Harm prevention, crisis incident logs                          │ No — safety events, not calculations                           │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ FTC AI guidance            │ AI-generated content labeling                                  │ No — label accuracy, not math audit                            │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ India Consumer Protection  │ No-misleading-advertising                                      │ No direct calculation audit — but needs subjunctive copy (§0.5 │   
  │ Act 2019                   │                                                                │  already covers this)                                          │   
  └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Legal minimum for classical-calculation audit = effectively zero. All the hard audit obligations are on the AI layer (§0.7) and data layer (separate F4
  data-processing log).

  The three real reasons audit still matters

  Not regulatory, but practical:

  1. Explainability (ethical obligation, product feature)
  User asks "why does it say I have Kuja Dosha?" — we owe them an answer. But this is a product feature, not an audit log. It's served by:
  - Every rule output already includes classical_citation field (BPHS Ch.X v.Y)
  - "Explain this" button triggers on-demand re-derivation from the rule registry
  - No persistent per-rule log needed — we re-compute the explanation on request

  2. Lawsuit defense (realistic threat)
  Threat model: user sues claiming "Josi's reading ruined my life" (quit job, divorced, etc.). India CPA 2019 or general tort liability. What we need to produce in  
  24-48h for legal response:
  - User's chart inputs + timestamp
  - Which AI interpretation they saw + disclaimer text
  - Their consent status + tier
  - Which classical rules fired (reconstructable)
  - Whether subjunctive copy was applied per §0.5

  The AI-side (§0.7) is already fully logged. The classical-calculation side needs reconstructability, not pre-logging. Given chart_input_hash +
  rule_registry_version + timestamp, we can re-run the rule engine and produce the exact calculation trace any time. No need to persist 50M rows/day to achieve this 
  — we need 100K rows/day with the 3 fields above.

  3. Engineering debugging (internal, nice-to-have)
  "Which users got output from the old Trikona Shodhanai rule?" — useful for rule-change migrations. But this doesn't need to be always-on audit. Enable per-incident
   deep-trace via feature flag when needed.

  What over-auditing costs us

  - 50M rows/day (Option A full-calc) vs 100K rows/day (Option C chart-level) — 500× storage delta
  - ~$500/mo BigQuery at launch vs ~$2/mo — 250× cost delta
  - Adds 5-20ms to every chart calc hot path
  - Every rule engine PRD carries the audit plumbing complexity
  - False security — more logs doesn't equal better defense

  What under-auditing would cost

  Going below Option C means losing reconstructability. If we can't reproduce a past calculation, we can't defend it in court. The minimum is chart_input +
  registry_version + timestamp — without this, old calcs are irretrievably lost.

  ---
  Recommended architecture — Option C+ (hardened)

  Chart-level audit + immutable rule registry versions + on-demand re-derivation + per-incident deep-trace flag.

  Layer 1 — Chart audit (persistent, lightweight)

  Every chart calculation writes one row:
  - chart_id, tenant_id, user_id_hash, timestamp
  - input_hash (chart inputs: birth data + location + ayanamsa + house_system)
  - rule_registry_version (git SHA, immutable)
  - output_hash (final chart state for integrity verification)
  - tier + surface (where this chart was calc'd from)

  Cost at 100K users: ~100K rows/day = ~$2-5/mo BigQuery. Negligible.

  Layer 2 — Immutable rule registry (git-backed)

  Rule registry versions are never deleted, never rewritten. Every classical rule has:
  - rule_id, rule_version (git SHA-pinned)
  - Python function + classical citation
  - Permanent addressability by version

  This means rule_registry_version from Layer 1 is always dereferenceable, even years later. No audit log needed for rule definitions themselves — git is the audit  
  log.

  Layer 3 — On-demand re-derivation API

  Endpoint: POST /api/v1/audit/reconstruct with {chart_id} or {input_hash, rule_registry_version}:
  - Re-runs rule engine with captured inputs + captured version
  - Returns full calculation trace: [{rule_id, rule_version, inputs, output, classical_citation}]
  - Deterministic — same output always for same inputs + version
  - Used for: explainability UI, lawsuit-package generation, engineering debugging

  Not a persistent log — a reproducibility guarantee.

  Layer 4 — Per-incident deep-trace flag

  For specific charts under investigation (user complaint, legal review, engineering debug):
  - Flip debug_trace = true on that chart_id
  - Next calc produces full Option A trace → written to BigQuery chart_debug_traces table
  - 30-day retention, manually cleared when investigation closes

  Opt-in per chart, not a 100%-sampled global policy.

  Layer 5 — Litigation-response workflow

  Given a legal subpoena "user X on date Y":
  1. Query chart_audit for chart(s) in date range → get input_hash, rule_registry_version
  2. Query §0.7 ai_events for AI interpretations served to that user
  3. Query F4 consent ledger for consents + disclaimers active at timestamps
  4. Query consultation records if applicable
  5. Call /audit/reconstruct for each chart → generate classical calculation traces
  6. Assemble litigation package (PDF export): 24-48h SLA achievable

  ---
  Ethical framing — "why did we give this response?"

  User-facing explainability is a product feature, not a logging obligation:

  - Every yoga result in the UI has an "i" icon → modal shows classical citation + rule fired + inputs that triggered it
  - Every AI interpretation paragraph has "view sources" → shows which symbolic tool outputs informed the LLM synthesis + classical citations
  - User can download their chart + all interpretations + explanations as PDF anytime (DPDP right-to-access)
  - Disclaimers are surfaced at the content level (§0.5 structural disclaimers), not hidden in footers

  We never need to say "the algorithm said so." Every claim traces to a classical source. That's the product promise, not just compliance.

  ---
  Revised recommendation

  Lock Option C+ (chart-level audit + immutable rule versions + on-demand re-derivation + per-incident deep-trace flag) — NOT Option A or B.

  Rationale summary:
  - Regulatory minimum: already satisfied by §0.7 (AI audit) + F4 (data-processing log — upcoming PRD)
  - Ethical explainability: served by product design + on-demand re-derivation, not persistent logs
  - Lawsuit defense: reconstructability ≥ persistent logging for defensibility; faster retrieval, smaller attack surface
  - Engineering: deep-trace per-incident covers debugging; no need for always-on 50M rows/day
  - FinOps: 250× cheaper than Option A; same defensive capability

  Options

  - A') Lock the honest recommendation — Option C+ with the 5-layer architecture above. ← my honest recommendation.
  - B') Keep prior Option B (rule-firing audit 3-5M rows/day) — middle ground if you want some ambient per-rule visibility for engineering debugging baseline,       
  accepting the ~$50/mo cost.
  - C') Option A (full-calc audit always-on) — belt-and-braces approach if audit defensibility is a high-anxiety topic and cost is acceptable. Over-engineered vs    
  actual need.
  - D') Defer decision — lock just "chart_audit exists, registry is immutable" as baseline now; decide deep-trace scope empirically after Month 3 based on incident  
  patterns.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 39s

❯ A - Option C+    
Moving to T1.7.

  ---
  T1.7 — LLM eval harness priority

  The research agent in the RAG study flagged this as a top-3 concern: no AI PRD should ship without a golden-dataset eval + LLM-as-judge regression gate. Defer this
   to P3 and agent teams ship untested prompts that regress silently.

  Josi has ~15 AI-powered surfaces (chart interpretations by tradition, NPQ, consult summaries, transit alerts, Upaya suggestions, astrologer workbench assistant,   
  etc.). Each needs its own eval dataset + regression threshold + review process.

  What "LLM eval harness" means concretely

  1. Golden datasets per surface — 50-200 curated input→expected-output pairs, authored by domain experts (you + Govind + editorial role from §0.5), reviewed        
  quarterly.
  2. LLM-as-judge rubrics — another LLM scores outputs against rubric (classical accuracy, tone, safety, disclaimer presence, language fidelity, etc.). Cheaper than 
  human review but requires calibration.
  3. Human-in-loop calibration — weekly human review of 5-10% of judge scores to catch judge drift.
  4. CI gate — every prompt template change runs the golden set through current + candidate prompt; blocks merge if regression threshold breached.
  5. Production regression monitoring — sampled production outputs scored by judge; weekly drift report.

  Options

  - A) P1 foundation — blocks Phase 3 engine implementation. Full eval infrastructure built in Phase 2 (Month 2-3) before any AI-powered PRD ships. Golden datasets  
  seeded during Phase 1 PRD review (each PRD includes sample eval cases in § 2.5 Engineering Review section). Agent teams shipping AI PRDs in Phase 3 must pass eval 
  gate before merge. Most disciplined; slowest start. ← my recommendation given 6-month timeline + the "untested prompts in production" failure mode is the single   
  biggest AI-product risk.
  - B) P1.5 — first AI PRD ships with eval, retroactively backfill others. Build eval infrastructure as part of E11a (AI Chat Orchestration v1) in Phase 3. Other AI 
  PRDs shipping later in Phase 3 inherit the infra. Free tier features ship without eval initially; paid tier PRDs ship with eval. Risk: backfill rarely happens in  
  practice.
  - C) P2 — build after v1 launch when we have production traffic to test against. Seed eval datasets from production user feedback + astrologer corrections
  post-launch. Faster Phase 3; higher risk of shipping regressions to production. Matches how most startups actually do it (and then suffer).
  - D) Hybrid — build CI gate (blocks prompt changes) in Phase 2; build production monitoring in v1.1. CI gate is cheap and catches "someone changed a prompt and    
  broke behavior" — ships in Phase 2. Production regression monitoring (judge-scored samples, drift reports) deferred to v1.1 when we have baselines to drift        
  against.

  Which?

      576

● Moving to T1.8.

  ---
  T1.8 — Audit-trail scope for calculations (non-AI)

  Different from §0.7 (which covers AI outputs). T1.8 asks: how much of the classical calculation path gets audit-logged? Every chart calc runs through ~250 yogas + 
  16 vargas + Shadbalam 6 + Ashtakavargam pipeline + Dasa computation + etc. Full audit means logging which rule fired with what inputs and what output; minimal     
  means logging only final chart results.

  Constraints from prior locks

  - §0.5 Wellness positioning — regulatory pressure is AI-side (EU AI Act, FTC labeling). Calculation audit is not strictly required by law.
  - §0.3 FinOps — audit log storage costs compound; full audit at 100K users × ~500 rule evaluations per chart = meaningful BigQuery spend.
  - §0.6 symbolic rule engine — rules are deterministic Python functions; inputs + rule_version fully reproduce outputs. Less need for exhaustive logging than LLM   
  outputs.
  - F4 Audit Log PRD exists in the tracker as a foundation-layer PRD. T1.8 locks its scope.

  Options

  - A) Full-calc audit — every rule evaluation logged. ~500 rule evaluations/chart × 100K daily chart calcs = 50M audit rows/day. Storage: ~$200-500/mo BigQuery at  
  launch scale, $2-5K at 1M users. Perfect reproducibility; powerful debugging. Highest cost.
  - B) Rule-firing audit only — log which rules fired + their outputs, not the full per-rule eval trace. ~30-50 rules actually fire per chart (most don't match).    
  ~3-5M rows/day at launch; ~$20-50/mo BigQuery. Sufficient for reproducibility since rule-inputs are derivable from chart + rule_version. ← my recommendation.      
  - C) Chart-level audit — log final chart output + rule_registry_version only. ~100K rows/day. Cheap; but to debug a wrong yoga detection later you can't trace     
  which rule returned what — have to re-run locally with the user's chart + that registry version.
  - D) Tiered — full audit for astrologer workbench + paid tier charts; rule-firing only for free tier. Astrologers need per-rule trace for their work; free-tier    
  users don't. Complicates schema + queries; questionable value given re-derivation is trivial from chart + version.

  What "rule-firing audit" captures concretely

  For each chart calculation:
  - chart_id, tenant_id, user_id_hash, timestamp
  - input_hash (chart inputs: birth data + location + ayanamsa + house_system + etc.)
  - rule_registry_version (git SHA of the rule registry at calc time)
  - List of rule firings: [{rule_id, rule_version, inputs_hash, output_summary, execution_ms}]
  - Final chart output hash for integrity verification

  Reproducibility: given input_hash + rule_registry_version, replay is deterministic — same output always.

  Which?

✻ Cooked for 1m 27s

❯ Can you tell me how much audit defense does this product really need? Be honest, given the compliance we are following and regulations we are following, and also  
  how to make it ethical and moral so that if somebody asks why we gave a response, how can we provide that explanation. Also if any lawsuits are filed, how can we  
  provide information for speedy retrieval of information.

● Let me be honest — the prior options over-scope this. The actual audit defense required is less than it seems.

  ---
  The honest answer — what Josi actually needs

  Regulatory minimums (the hard legal floor)

  Walking through every regulation in the §0.1 compliance stack and what it actually requires for classical-calculation audit:

  ┌────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │         Regulation         │                        What it requires                        │             Does it mandate per-calculation audit?             │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ EU AI Act (Aug 2026)       │ AI-generated content logging (model, version, content hash);   │ No — covers AI outputs, not classical math. §0.7 already       │   
  │                            │ incident reporting                                             │ handles this.                                                  │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ India DPDP Act 2023/2025   │ Consent records, data-processing log, access/erasure execution │ No — covers personal-data handling, not calculation internals  │   
  │                            │  logs                                                          │                                                                │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ US CCPA/CPRA               │ Disclosure, access, deletion rights                            │ No — data rights, not algorithm audit                          │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ UK Online Safety Act       │ Harm prevention, crisis incident logs                          │ No — safety events, not calculations                           │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ FTC AI guidance            │ AI-generated content labeling                                  │ No — label accuracy, not math audit                            │   
  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ India Consumer Protection  │ No-misleading-advertising                                      │ No direct calculation audit — but needs subjunctive copy (§0.5 │   
  │ Act 2019                   │                                                                │  already covers this)                                          │   
  └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Legal minimum for classical-calculation audit = effectively zero. All the hard audit obligations are on the AI layer (§0.7) and data layer (separate F4
  data-processing log).

  The three real reasons audit still matters

  Not regulatory, but practical:

  1. Explainability (ethical obligation, product feature)
  User asks "why does it say I have Kuja Dosha?" — we owe them an answer. But this is a product feature, not an audit log. It's served by:
  - Every rule output already includes classical_citation field (BPHS Ch.X v.Y)
  - "Explain this" button triggers on-demand re-derivation from the rule registry
  - No persistent per-rule log needed — we re-compute the explanation on request

  2. Lawsuit defense (realistic threat)
  Threat model: user sues claiming "Josi's reading ruined my life" (quit job, divorced, etc.). India CPA 2019 or general tort liability. What we need to produce in  
  24-48h for legal response:
  - User's chart inputs + timestamp
  - Which AI interpretation they saw + disclaimer text
  - Their consent status + tier
  - Which classical rules fired (reconstructable)
  - Whether subjunctive copy was applied per §0.5

  The AI-side (§0.7) is already fully logged. The classical-calculation side needs reconstructability, not pre-logging. Given chart_input_hash +
  rule_registry_version + timestamp, we can re-run the rule engine and produce the exact calculation trace any time. No need to persist 50M rows/day to achieve this 
  — we need 100K rows/day with the 3 fields above.

  3. Engineering debugging (internal, nice-to-have)
  "Which users got output from the old Trikona Shodhanai rule?" — useful for rule-change migrations. But this doesn't need to be always-on audit. Enable per-incident
   deep-trace via feature flag when needed.

  What over-auditing costs us

  - 50M rows/day (Option A full-calc) vs 100K rows/day (Option C chart-level) — 500× storage delta
  - ~$500/mo BigQuery at launch vs ~$2/mo — 250× cost delta
  - Adds 5-20ms to every chart calc hot path
  - Every rule engine PRD carries the audit plumbing complexity
  - False security — more logs doesn't equal better defense

  What under-auditing would cost

  Going below Option C means losing reconstructability. If we can't reproduce a past calculation, we can't defend it in court. The minimum is chart_input +
  registry_version + timestamp — without this, old calcs are irretrievably lost.

  ---
  Recommended architecture — Option C+ (hardened)

  Chart-level audit + immutable rule registry versions + on-demand re-derivation + per-incident deep-trace flag.

  Layer 1 — Chart audit (persistent, lightweight)

  Every chart calculation writes one row:
  - chart_id, tenant_id, user_id_hash, timestamp
  - input_hash (chart inputs: birth data + location + ayanamsa + house_system)
  - rule_registry_version (git SHA, immutable)
  - output_hash (final chart state for integrity verification)
  - tier + surface (where this chart was calc'd from)

  Cost at 100K users: ~100K rows/day = ~$2-5/mo BigQuery. Negligible.

  Layer 2 — Immutable rule registry (git-backed)

  Rule registry versions are never deleted, never rewritten. Every classical rule has:
  - rule_id, rule_version (git SHA-pinned)
  - Python function + classical citation
  - Permanent addressability by version

  This means rule_registry_version from Layer 1 is always dereferenceable, even years later. No audit log needed for rule definitions themselves — git is the audit  
  log.

  Layer 3 — On-demand re-derivation API

  Endpoint: POST /api/v1/audit/reconstruct with {chart_id} or {input_hash, rule_registry_version}:
  - Re-runs rule engine with captured inputs + captured version
  - Returns full calculation trace: [{rule_id, rule_version, inputs, output, classical_citation}]
  - Deterministic — same output always for same inputs + version
  - Used for: explainability UI, lawsuit-package generation, engineering debugging

  Not a persistent log — a reproducibility guarantee.

  Layer 4 — Per-incident deep-trace flag

  For specific charts under investigation (user complaint, legal review, engineering debug):
  - Flip debug_trace = true on that chart_id
  - Next calc produces full Option A trace → written to BigQuery chart_debug_traces table
  - 30-day retention, manually cleared when investigation closes

  Opt-in per chart, not a 100%-sampled global policy.

  Layer 5 — Litigation-response workflow

  Given a legal subpoena "user X on date Y":
  1. Query chart_audit for chart(s) in date range → get input_hash, rule_registry_version
  2. Query §0.7 ai_events for AI interpretations served to that user
  3. Query F4 consent ledger for consents + disclaimers active at timestamps
  4. Query consultation records if applicable
  5. Call /audit/reconstruct for each chart → generate classical calculation traces
  6. Assemble litigation package (PDF export): 24-48h SLA achievable

  ---
  Ethical framing — "why did we give this response?"

  User-facing explainability is a product feature, not a logging obligation:

  - Every yoga result in the UI has an "i" icon → modal shows classical citation + rule fired + inputs that triggered it
  - Every AI interpretation paragraph has "view sources" → shows which symbolic tool outputs informed the LLM synthesis + classical citations
  - User can download their chart + all interpretations + explanations as PDF anytime (DPDP right-to-access)
  - Disclaimers are surfaced at the content level (§0.5 structural disclaimers), not hidden in footers

  We never need to say "the algorithm said so." Every claim traces to a classical source. That's the product promise, not just compliance.

  ---
  Revised recommendation

  Lock Option C+ (chart-level audit + immutable rule versions + on-demand re-derivation + per-incident deep-trace flag) — NOT Option A or B.

  Rationale summary:
  - Regulatory minimum: already satisfied by §0.7 (AI audit) + F4 (data-processing log — upcoming PRD)
  - Ethical explainability: served by product design + on-demand re-derivation, not persistent logs
  - Lawsuit defense: reconstructability ≥ persistent logging for defensibility; faster retrieval, smaller attack surface
  - Engineering: deep-trace per-incident covers debugging; no need for always-on 50M rows/day
  - FinOps: 250× cheaper than Option A; same defensive capability

  Options

  - A') Lock the honest recommendation — Option C+ with the 5-layer architecture above. ← my honest recommendation.
  - B') Keep prior Option B (rule-firing audit 3-5M rows/day) — middle ground if you want some ambient per-rule visibility for engineering debugging baseline,       
  accepting the ~$50/mo cost.
  - C') Option A (full-calc audit always-on) — belt-and-braces approach if audit defensibility is a high-anxiety topic and cost is acceptable. Over-engineered vs    
  actual need.
  - D') Defer decision — lock just "chart_audit exists, registry is immutable" as baseline now; decide deep-trace scope empirically after Month 3 based on incident  
  patterns.

  Which?

✻ Baked for 1m 39s

❯ A - Option C+

● Moving to T1.9.

  ---
  T1.9 — Agent-team parallelism + orchestration structure

  You've named the agent team composition: PM agent + coding agents + QA agents + frontend-design agents + frontend-dev agents. The question is: concrete structure  
  for parallel work.

  Dimensions to lock

  a) How many parallel agent-team instances? 2-3 sequential (safe) vs 5-8 concurrent (fast) vs 10+ massively parallel (fastest, most coordination risk).

  b) PM agent's concrete role.
  - Work-ticket assignment (read PRD list, pick next ready-to-start PRD)
  - Dependency resolution (block E27 until E5b ships)
  - Integration coordination (detect schema conflicts between two agents working in parallel)
  - Spec-clarification (if a coding agent asks a question, PM agent consults ARCHITECTURE_DECISIONS.md or escalates to human)
  - Status reporting (daily standup output, stuck items, cost tracking)

  c) Agent autonomy level. How much does human review? Every PR? Sample? Integration-only?

  d) Agent communication protocol. How do agents hand off between each other?

  Options for parallelism (dimension a)

  - A) 3-4 parallel teams (conservative). Each team owns 2-3 PRDs at a time. PM agent orchestrates. Human reviews every PR. Low coordination risk. Phase 3 takes ~4  
  months.
  - B) 8-12 parallel teams (balanced). Each team owns 1 PRD. PM agent handles cross-team coordination. Human reviews weekly batches + critical-path PRs. Phase 3     
  takes ~2.5-3 months. ← my recommendation given 6-month timeline + PRDs are mostly independent after foundation layer.
  - C) 20+ parallel teams (aggressive). Every PRD gets its own team; PM agent runs massive orchestration. Human reviews samples + integration only. Phase 3 could    
  finish in 4-6 weeks. High coordination risk; requires robust PM agent.
  - D) Tiered by risk. 3-4 teams for high-risk PRDs (AI PRDs, payment, compliance — human review every PR) + 8-12 teams for lower-risk (classical engines — human    
  review weekly batches). Matches review intensity to risk.

  Options for PM agent scope (dimension b)

  - a) Work-assignment + dependency-resolution only. PM agent picks next PRD for each available team, enforces dependency order. Humans handle everything else.      
  - b) Above + integration coordination. PM agent also detects cross-team conflicts (schema collisions, API contract drift) and surfaces them before merge.
  - c) Above + spec-clarification. PM agent answers "what should I do about ambiguity X?" by consulting DECISIONS + ARCHITECTURE_DECISIONS + PRD §2.5. Escalates to  
  human only on genuinely-undecided questions. ← my recommendation.
  - d) Above + quality-gate ownership. PM agent enforces all Phase-3 gates (eval harness passing, compliance checks, test coverage). Human only reviews at milestone 
  boundaries.

  Options for agent autonomy (dimension c)

  - i) Human reviews every PR. Safest. Most human time. Matches rigorous review culture.
  - ii) Human samples 20% + critical-path PRs. Balanced. Matches your pair-review preference extended to implementation. ← my recommendation.
  - iii) Human reviews integration-only (weekly bundle). Fastest. Highest drift risk.

  Options for handoff protocol (dimension d)

  - p) Direct agent-to-agent handoff via structured artifacts (PR → QA agent picks up → frontend-dev agent picks up). Tight coupling, fast.
  - q) All handoffs via PM agent (coding agent hands off to PM → PM assigns to QA agent). Looser coupling, PM is the bottleneck-but-observable.
  - r) Artifact-based queue — each agent reads from its own "ready" queue, writes to next agent's queue. Asynchronous, observable, fault-tolerant. ← my
  recommendation.

  Composite recommendation

  B + c + ii + r: 8-12 parallel teams + PM agent handles work+deps+integration+spec-clarification + human samples 20% PRs + artifact-based queues for handoffs.      

  Or pick dimension-by-dimension?

  Which structure (single composite or dimension-by-dimension)?
